gmw_00004980.overview: PKS 1343--601, also known as Centaurus B, is a large elliptical radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0603692] that is classified as a relic radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0002463]. It is heavily obscured by the Milky Way [arXiv:astro-ph/0002463] and may mark the center of a hidden galaxy cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0603692].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 1343--601 as a large elliptical radio galaxy and possible center of a hidden, highly obscured group or cluster, with radio lobes explaining its point-like X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: PKS 1343--601 PKS 1343--601 is the second brightest extragalactic radio source in the southern sky mil52 . The associated galaxy, lying at 309.7 , +1.7 , 3872 , wes89 , is a large elliptical galaxy lau77,wes89 , typical of those found in cluster cores. Hence it has been suggested that PKS 1343--601 may mark the centre of another highly obscured ( A _B 12) cluster wou98,kra99 . X-ray studies have yet to reveal any indication that such a hidden cluster exists. No corresponding source is seen in the CIZA survey and the point-like X-ray emissions reported by are consistent with the radio lobes of PKS 1343--601 rather than intracluster gas ebe02 .

sources.summary: The table notes describe Centaurus B as a relic radio galaxy, identify its radio and X-ray data sources, and model an average lobe because the X-rays cover the whole source.

quotes.quote: CENTAURUS B (a.k.a. PKS1343-601) This is a relic radio galaxy, somewhat obscured by the Milky Way. Radio data are from McAdam (1991) and the X-ray data are from Tashiro et al. (1998). Since the X-ray data are for the entire source, and since the lobes are not the same size and brightness, we model an 'average lobe', taking the size, =300" (that of the S lobe), and divide both radio and X-ray fluxes by 2.

gmw_00004981.name: SGAS 143845.1+145407
gmw_00004981.overview: SGAS 143845.1+145407 is a strongly lensed, red luminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.816 [arXiv:1202.5269]. It features extended, organized star formation across a spatial scale of approximately 7 kpc, driven by ongoing dusty star formation rather than a compact merger [arXiv:1202.5269].

sources.summary: The paper presents SGAS 143845.1+145407 as a strongly lensed red LIRG at z=0.816, magnified by about 17, with cool far-infrared emission, a roughly 7 kpc size, and extended organized star formation.

quotes.quote: We present the discovery and a detailed multi-wavelength study of a strongly-lensed luminous infrared galaxy at z=0.816. Unlike most known lensed galaxies discovered at optical or near-infrared wavelengths this lensed source is red, (-K_s)_ AB =3.9 , which the data presented here demonstrate is due to ongoing dusty star formation. The overall lensing magnification (a factor of 17) facilitates observations from the blue optical through to 500 , fully capturing both the stellar photospheric emission as well as the re-processed thermal dust emission.

Its far-infrared spectral energy distribution is well--fit by local templates that are an order of magnitude less luminous than the lensed galaxy; local templates of comparable luminosity are too hot to fit. Its size ( D 7 kpc) is much larger than local luminous infrared galaxies, but in line with sizes observed for such galaxies at z 1 . The star formation appears uniform across this spatial scale. In this source, the luminosity of which is typical of sources that dominate the cosmic infrared background, we find that star formation is spatially extended and well organised, quite unlike the compact merger-driven starbursts which are typical for sources of this luminosity at z 0 .

gmw_00004982.name: Sagittarius dwarf galaxy
gmw_00004982.overview: The Sagittarius dwarf galaxy is a nearby dwarf spheroidal satellite of the Milky Way [arXiv:0710.2493, arXiv:1307.4918]. Located approximately 24 kiloparsecs from the Sun, it has an extremely low surface brightness and is highly tidally disrupted [arXiv:1307.4918].

sources.summary: The paper presents H.E.S.S. observations of the Sagittarius dwarf as a dark-matter target, noting its Local Group satellite status, difficult visible mass profile, no detected gamma-ray excess, and dark-halo modeling.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The pointing position is thus (RA = 18^h55^m59.9s, Dec = -30d28'59.9'') in equatorial coordinates (J2000.0).

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy is a Galaxy satellite of the Local Group. It was recently discovered in the direction of the Galactic Center. The center of Sgr is located at 24 kpc from the Sun. The visible mass profile is difficult to measure because of the contamination of foreground galactic stars. The central velocity dispersion has been measured by various groups .

H.E.S.S. observed the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy and no significant gamma-ray excess has been detected. Two DM halo modellings of Sgr have been investigated to derive contraints on the velocity-weighted annihilation cross-section.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy as a nearby, low-surface-brightness Milky Way companion targeted by H.E.S.S.; non-detections constrain gamma-ray flux and WIMP annihilation models.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: RA = 18 ^ h 55 ^ m 59.9 ^ s , Dec = -30 ^ 28 ^ 59.9 ^ in equatorial coordinates (J2000.0)

quotes.quote: The Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy (SgrD) was discovered in 1994 and it is located at RA = 18 ^ h 55 ^ m 59.9 ^ s , Dec = -30 ^ 28 ^ 59.9 ^ in equatorial coordinates (J2000.0), at a distance of about 24 kpc from the Sun . SgrD is one of the nearby dwarf spheroidal companion galaxies of the Milky Way. Characterised by an extremely low surface brightness it has been estimated that SgrD orbits the Milky Way within less than a billion years and therefore it must have already passed through the galactic plane at least about ten times. Although its high level of tidal disruption, the hypothesis of a large DM content would be responsible to hold onto so many of its stars and for so long.

The H.E.S.S. collaboration observed SgrD searching for a potential emission of very-high-energy (VHE) gamma rays during 11 h in 2006. In absence of any signal an upper limit on the gamma-ray flux and a constraint on the velocity-weighted annihilation cross-section in the frame of SUSY models as well as extra-dimensions models were published . A deeper observation of SgrD was conducted accumulating further 90 hours of observation in 2007-2012 using the four 12 m dish telescopes.

gmw_00004983.name: GN20.2
gmw_00004983.overview: GN20.2 is a gas-rich, hyper-luminous submillimeter galaxy at a redshift of approximately 4.05 [arXiv:1307.4763, arXiv:2302.10323] that exhibits a strong radio excess [arXiv:0810.3108, arXiv:1401.6079]. It is modeled as an elongated, disc-like galaxy with a nuclear point source [arXiv:2402.18672] and hosts a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0810.3108].

sources.summary: The paper treats GN20.2a as the primary counterpart of GN20.2, detects significant CO emission at its radio/optical position, and argues from its radio excess that it hosts a powerful radio-loud AGN.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Averaging the velocity channels between -300 km s ^ 1 and 825 km s ^ 1 a source is detected in the PdBI map with S/N=5.8 at the position of (J2000)= 12^h37^m08^s.77 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m01^s.7 in the B-configuration observations (Fig. fig:2Db left and Fig. fig:1D right).

quotes.quote: Averaging the velocity channels between -300 km s ^ 1 and 825 km s ^ 1 a source is detected in the PdBI map with S/N=5.8 at the position of (J2000)= 12^h37^m08^s.77 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m01^s.7 in the B-configuration observations (Fig. fig:2Db left and Fig. fig:1D right). Positive flux with S/N just below 3 is observed also in the D-configuration data at this position (Fig. fig:2Db left). We extracted the spectra by fitting point sources in the D- and B-configuration datasets independently and coadded the spectra with weighting after correcting for primary beam attenuation. Averaging the total resulting spectrum within -300 km s ^ 1 and 825 km s ^ 1 we find a positive signal significant at the 7 level. The position of this source is within 0.2 '' of the relatively bright radio source that P06 identified with the counterpart of the SMG GN20.2, close but well distinguished from GN20 that is 24'' to the NE.

The 850 m to VLA radio flux density ratio for GN20.2a is about 5 lower than that for GN20 (Fig. fig:colorS ), implying a strong radio excess of a factor of 4-5 . GN20.2a has a radio luminosity of 2 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 at 1.4 GHz rest frame, using again a radio continuum index =0.8 . This demonstrates that a powerful radio-loud AGN is hidden in this z=4.05 source.

sources.summary: The paper treats GN20.2a as a z=4.05 submillimeter galaxy near GN20, measuring its dust and CO properties and interpreting its CO profile as possible rotation or merger activity.

quotes.quote: Two additional SMGs, GN20.2a and GN20.2b, were found to lie within 25 '' of GN20 (projected physical separation 170 kpc) and have redshifts of z 4.055 0.005. These two galaxies are separated by only a few arcseconds and hence are not spatially separated from each other in existing submm images (e.g., SCUBA 850 m).

The deep, high-resolution CO(2-1) observations reveal a clumpy, extended gas disk (14 4 kpc in diameter) for GN20 hodge12 , and extended gas reservoirs ( 5--8 kpc) for GN20.2a and GN20.2b hodge13 . hodge12,hodge13 have attempted to constrain the estimate of CO-to-H _2 conversion factor by dynamical analysis, deriving _ CO of 1--2 for these three galaxies. For the dust continuum emission, however, only GN20 has been reported in Herschel and (sub)mm detections daddi09b,magdis11 . Here we use PACS and PdBI mm data for GN20.2a and GN20.2b to study the dust properties of these two galaxies.

For GN20.2a, the fits with a single Gaussian and two Gaussian functions yield FWHM of 820 237 \ and 763 180 , respectively. The spectrum is extracted at the fixed CO position from daddi09b . We find the CO spectrum of GN20.2a appears to be better described by a double Gaussian profile (Fig. fig1 ), given the relatively low uncertainties of FWHM derived from double Gaussian fitting and the rapid decrease of the flux at the edges of the spectrum, though there is no clear indication of double-peaked emission in the spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper models GN20.2a as an elongated disc-like DSFG with a likely nuclear point source, asymmetric residuals, lower stellar mass, and rapid star formation.

quotes.quote: The presence of a bright excess in the nuclear region of GN20.2a and its light asymmetry along the semi-major axis cause that this galaxy cannot be modelled with a single S ersic profile. We therefore also considered, as for GN20, the presence of a point-source component during the brightness distribution modelling. We obtain that, after masking some diffuse emission present at the North-East of the object that is not confirmed to be part of GN20.2a, the extended emission from the galaxy can be modelled as a very elongated ( b/a = 0.25 0.01) S ersic profile with n = 0.84 0.10 and R_ eff = 2.13 0.06 kpc. The axial ratio of the galaxy implies, assuming that GN20.2a is a disc-like galaxy with q_0 = 0.2, an inclination of i = 81 2 . Contrary to GN20, we do not find any offset between the S ersic and the point-like components, suggesting that this source is likely linked to the nucleus of the galaxy.

In this galaxy, the residuals represent about 31 of original flux, which can be understood as due to the diffuse emission in the NE of the galaxy visible in HST/F160W and MIRI/F560W (see Fig. fig:UV-MIRI_images ) and to light excess along the NW semi-major axis. In fact, this asymmetry along the major axis, depicted by the contours in Figure fig:lenstronomy , can be also observed in the residual map. Possible origins of this asymmetry could be an irregular dust distribution or/and the presence of very bright blobs located somewhere in the NW semi-major axis, intensified by the high inclination of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper resolves GN20.2a in CO, deriving compact molecular-gas size, high gas surface density, redshift, dynamical constraints, and evidence that it is in a different merger stage than GN20.2b.

quotes.quote: The SMGs GN20.2a and GN20.2b have deconvolved sizes of 5 kpc 3 kpc and 8 kpc 5 kpc (Gaussian FWHM) in CO(2--1), respectively, and we measure gas surface densities up to 12,700/1,700 (sin i ) ( _ CO /0.8) M _ pc ^ 2 for GN20.2a/GN20.2b in the highest--resolution maps. Dynamical mass estimates allow us to constrain the CO--to--H _2 conversion factor to _ CO = 1.7 0.8 M _ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 ) ^ 1 for GN20.2a and _ CO = 1.1 ^ 1.5 _ 1.1 M _ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 ) ^ 1 for GN20.2b. We measure significant offsets (0.5 ^ -1 ^ ) between the CO and optical emission, indicating either dust obscuration on scales of tens of kpc or that the emission originates from distinct galaxies. CO spectral line energy distributions imply physical conditions comparable to other SMGs and reveal further evidence that GN20.2a and GN20.2b are in different merging stages.

GN20.2a was identified by 2009ApJ...694.1517D as the primary counterpart to the SCUBA source GN20.2 2006MNRAS.370.1185P . Its CO(2--1) spectrum was shown in 2011ApJ...739L..33C for the D--configuration data and has not changed significantly with the addition of the B--configuration data, presented here for the first time. A Gaussian fit to the spectrum using the B + D--configuration data yields a peak flux density for GN20.2a of 660 120 Jy beam ^ 1 and a derived redshift of 4.051 0.001.

sources.summary: The paper treats GN20.2a as GN20’s gas-rich, hyper-luminous companion in a z=4.05 protocluster and finds a compact associated overdensity.

quotes.quote: GN20 and GN20.2a were discovered as bright distant SMGs by Daddi09 . They have similarly high gas masses M_ H_2 =(0.9-1.5) 10^ 11 M_ and FIR luminosities, L_ FIR /L_ =13.20-13.46 , typical of HyLIRGs e.g., RowanRobinson2000,Neri2020 , which are often associated with infall/outflows, mergers, and mutual interactions. They are thus possibly interacting as indeed they are separated by just 24 arcsec (i.e., 167 kpc) in projection, while being at the same redshift z=4.05 .

Furthermore, Daddi09 found a strong overdensity of B-band dropouts and IRAC (Infrared Array Camera on the Spitzer Space Telescope) -selected massive z>3.5 galaxies, appearing to be centered on the two SMGs GN20 and GN20.2a, which suggests the presence of a z=4.05 protocluster. These three overdensities are those around ID.19 (in the case of both B19 and L18 catalogs) and GN20.2a (B19), and for our analysis we consider them altogether with the others. In some cases (e.g., GN10, GN20, and GN20.2a) they have compact morphologies, similarly to CL J1001+0220 at z=2.51 in the COSMOS field Wang16 .

sources.summary: The paper treats GN20.2 as a spectroscopically confirmed z~4 submillimeter galaxy, with a photometric redshift consistent with spectroscopy and lying in the GN20/GN20.2 proto-cluster region.

quotes.quote: For two of these galaxies, GN20 and GN20.2, (objects 15541, 13857 in our sample) spectroscopic redshifts around z 4 were recently determined by hereafter D08 Daddietal2008 . Those objects are among the most distant SMGs spectroscopically confirmed known at the present. Our photometric redshifts for these galaxies are consistent within the error ( 0.2 ) with the spectroscopic ones (being 3.75 and 3.95 for GN20 and GN20.2 respectively).
It is interesting to remark that D08 discuss the evidence of a galaxy proto-cluster at z 4 in the GN20 and GN20.2 area, due to the finding of an over-density of B-dropout galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper identifies GN20.2 at z=4.05 via CO emission, notes Chandra non-detection despite GN10-like FIR luminosity, and finds a low radio-to-FIR q value indicating strong radio excess.

quotes.quote: A nearby radio source, GN20.2, was subsequently identified at z=4.05 through detection of the same CO transition.

On the other hand, GN20 and GN20.2 with a similar L_ FIR as GN10, are not detected in the Chandra 2Ms imaging, and thus they have L_ x < 10 ^ 42.6 erg s ^ 1 . If the X-ray emission from GN10 were due to intensive star formation, we would expect a similar L _ x from GN20 and GN20.2.
GN20.2 has a much lower q value indicating a strong radio excess, in agreement with Daddi et al. (2009).

gmw_00004984.name: HCG 15C
gmw_00004984.overview: HCG 15C is an elliptical galaxy with a discordant recession velocity of 9687 km/s [arXiv:0805.4015]. It is likely a distant interloper projected onto the HCG 15 group rather than a physical member [arXiv:0805.4015].

sources.summary: The paper treats HCG 15C as an elliptical with a discordant recession velocity, likely a projected interloper whose inclusion changes HCG 15 group configuration tests.

quotes.quote: A more recent measurement shows that the elliptical galaxy HCG 15C has a recession velocity of 9687 22 km s^ 1 galaxy UGC 01620 in the Updated Zwicky Catalog - UZC, fac99 , that puts this galaxy as an interloper in the group. We will perform the analysis of this group for the two possible configurations (sextet and quintet, excluding HCG 15C from the group), in order to test which one is compatible with the stage of dynamical evolution of the group.
The scenario where HCG 15C is a distant galaxy projected onto the group would explain why the galaxy in the center of the group does not present signs of interaction.
The new discordant velocity of HCG 15C, which has a much larger radial velocity (by 2720 km s^ 1 ) than the median velocity of the group, is a point to be considered with care.

gmw_00004985.name: HCG 51C
gmw_00004985.overview: HCG 51C is classified as an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:0805.4015]. Its radial velocity is 1200 km/s above the median velocity of the HCG 51 group, leading to debate over whether it should be excluded or included in the group's configuration [arXiv:0805.4015].

sources.summary: The paper discusses HCG 51C as an S0 galaxy with a velocity just beyond the group threshold, testing whether including it changes HCG 51 dynamics.

quotes.quote: Originally classified as a sextet of galaxies hic82 , HCG 51C's radial velocity is 1200 km s^ 1 above the group's median velocity, and therefore it was excluded from the group by hic92 . On the other hand, as explained by these authors, 1000 km s^ 1 is an arbitrary threshold, so we decided to analyze both possibilities. This group is dominated by an E1 galaxy and has 2 late-type and 4 early-type galaxies and HCG 51C is an S0 galaxy.
Using the new recession velocities would not change the difference between the velocity of HCG 51C and the median velocity of this group.

Altering the original group configuration, as excluding HCG 15C (due to its new published recession velocity) or including HCG 51C (extending the definition of ``discordant velocity'') puts a challenge on defining which is the ``correct'' configuration for each of the groups.

gmw_00004986.name: NGC2713
gmw_00004986.overview: NGC2713 is a spiral galaxy with a structure that has been analyzed using both radio and optical data [arXiv:2207.02906]. Researchers combined extended VLA neutral hydrogen observations with inner MaNGA integral field spectroscopy to construct a hybrid rotation curve for the galaxy [arXiv:2207.02906].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC2713 as a detailed modeling example, combining extended VLA H I and inner MaNGA data to build a well-fit hybrid rotation curve.

quotes.quote: As an example, in fig:modex we show the best-fit 3D kinematical model for the galaxy NGC2713. For this particular galaxy, we had both new extended VLA \ data (top rows) and IFU \ data from MaNGA (bottom rows) for the very inner regions of the galaxy (red box in the uppermost left panel). A selection of channel maps of the data (blue contours) are compared to the corresponding channel maps of the best-fit model (red contours). For all velocity channels, the 3D model can accurately reproduce the line emission in both radio and optical data, signalling an optimal fit. The resulting hybrid \ rotation curve for NGC2713 can be found in the left panel of fig:decomp , where dots denote \ and stars denote .

gmw_00004987.name: HCG 37a
gmw_00004987.overview: HCG 37a, also known as NGC 2783, is an E7 early-type galaxy and the brightest member of the Hickson 37 compact group [arXiv:0805.1709, arXiv:2103.05006]. Classified as a dwarf LINER [arXiv:1003.0345], it features a rich Type I shell system [arXiv:2103.05006] and a detected nuclear point source [arXiv:0805.1709].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HCG 37a as the early-type galaxy coincident with the group X-ray peak, with a detected nuclear point source and surrounding irregular intragroup emission.

quotes.quote: Irregular diffuse X-ray emission was detected in this group with ROSAT out to 8 arcmin mulc03 , well beyond the region covered by a single ACIS chip in our Chandra data. The latter clearly indicate that the group emission is sharply peaked on the early-type galaxy HCG 37a ( = NGC 2783), the nucleus of which is also detected as a point-like source in the data. The association of the IGM X-ray peak with HCG 37a was not obvious from the earlier ROSAT data, as the much broader ROSAT PSF required mulc03 to exclude point-like emission out to =1.5 arcmin from the peak, thus effectively masking out HCG 37a in the data. Despite the overall irregularity of the group emission, the X-ray centroid (when masking out the HCG 37a nucleus) coincides to within 10 arcsec with the optical position of HCG 37a as listed in NED.

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 37a as an E7 dwarf LINER with central H emission whose low signal-to-noise profiles prevented a rotation-curve measurement.

quotes.quote: The brighter member, HCG 37a, has a morphological type E7 (Hickson 1992). Coziol et al. (2004) classified galaxies HCG 37a, HCG 37b, HCG 37c and HCG 37d as a dwarf LINER (dLINER), LINER, low luminosity AGN and a Star Forming Galaxy (SFG), respectively.

HCG 37a shows a central H emission, as was reported in Vilchez et al. (1998), however, the H profiles have a low signal to noise ratios. For this galaxy it was not possible to perform a rotation curve.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2783 as the brightest galaxy in Hickson 37, with a rich Type I shell system and a rapidly rotating ionized-gas disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 2783 is the brightest member of a small compact group of five galaxies, Hickson 37 rub91,wch95 . It is another rich Type I shell system with at least six shells visible in the Legacy Surveys image, Fig. fig:ls7 . The galaxy also contains a rapidly rotating disk of ionized gas cal89 .

gmw_00004988.name: HCG 37d
gmw_00004988.overview: HCG 37d is an emission-line galaxy that exhibits a regular velocity field [arXiv:1003.0345]. Its kinematic position angle is aligned with its optical axis, though its rotation curve is asymmetric and does not reach the optical radius [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper finds HCG 37d has extended two-peaked H emission, a regular velocity field, aligned kinematic and optical axes, and an asymmetric rotation curve.

quotes.quote: The monochromatic map of HCG 37d shows an extension of 13 arcsec (5.2 kpc). It has two peaks in the emission, as was found in Vilchez et al. (1998), the peak located in the north west side of the galaxy being stronger. The continuum image shows the same peaks as the monochromatic image.

The velocity field of this object is regular. The kinematic position angle is aligned with the optical one (as derived by Mendes de Oliveira 1992 at 2 h ^ 1 kpc), differing in only 2 ^ . The rotation curve of HCG 37d is not symmetric and both sides do not match. The receding and approaching sides reach velocities of 60 km s ^ 1 and 53 km s ^ 1 respectively. The rotation curve does not reach the optical radius of the galaxy (R _ 25 ).

gmw_00004989.name: HCG 40a
gmw_00004989.overview: HCG 40a is an early-type galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345] that was previously classified as a radio source [arXiv:1003.0345]. Although once categorized as a no-emission galaxy, recent observations have detected hydrogen emission in its central region [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper treats HCG 40a as an early-type galaxy reclassified as no-emission, but newly detects central H emission and cannot derive a rotation curve.

quotes.quote: HCG 40a, HCG 40d and HCG 40e were classified as radio sources (dLNR, LNR and Sy2 respectively) in Coziol et al. (1998) and re-classified as no-emission galaxy, SFG and SFG in Coziol et al. (2004).

We also detected H emission in the central region of HCG 40a and HCG 40b (both galaxies were classified as early-type galaxies by Hickson (1992), E3 and S0, respectively). Interestingly, no emission was detected in Coziol et al. (2000) for these objects. As for HCG 40d, we could not obtain the rotation curve for these objects.

gmw_00004990.name: HCG 40b
gmw_00004990.overview: HCG 40b is classified as an early-type S0 galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. Although previously classified as a no-emission galaxy, central H-alpha emission has been detected within it [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 40b as an early-type no-emission galaxy where central H emission was detected, contrary to earlier work, but no rotation curve was obtained.

quotes.quote: HCG 40b is a no-emission galaxy and HCG 40c is a SFG (Coziol et al. 2004).

We also detected H emission in the central region of HCG 40a and HCG 40b (both galaxies were classified as early-type galaxies by Hickson (1992), E3 and S0, respectively). Interestingly, no emission was detected in Coziol et al. (2000) for these objects. As for HCG 40d, we could not obtain the rotation curve for these objects.

gmw_00004991.name: HCG 40c
gmw_00004991.overview: HCG 40c is an edge-on galaxy that displays a regular velocity field and a symmetric rotation curve [arXiv:1003.0345, arXiv:1509.05779]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus, exhibits a bar and ring molecular structure, and is tidally interacting with HCG 40e [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 40c as an edge-on CARMA CO detection in transition colors, hosting AGN signatures, interacting with HCG 40e, and showing bar/ring molecular structure.

quotes.quote: HCG 40c : Figure fig:hcg40 shows that HCG 40c is an edge-on galaxy strongly detected with CARMA. While HCG 40c lies in the red part of the WISE IRTZ, it is found in the optical green valley, though near the red sequence. It also has a radio core and optically identified AGN martinez+10 . Two Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS) imaging also shows that HCG 40c is tidally interacting with HCG 40e 2mass .

The PVD of HCG 40c (Fig fig:hcg40 ) also shows a significant bar (seen as the large velocity structure with very little position shift), similar to what was seen by alatalo+13 in several \ early-type galaxies. This is likely what is responsible for the slight appearance of broad wings in the CO spectrum. Given the frequency with which bars arise during gravitational encounters athanassoula96,ath+bureau99 , it is unsurprising that HCG 40c is morphologically classed as a bar+ring.

sources.summary: The paper reports extended H emission in HCG 40c, a regular velocity field, stable kinematic position angle, and a symmetric rotation curve reaching a plateau.

quotes.quote: HCG 40c displays an extended monochromatic map. We detected a peak in the H emission at the south-east side of HCG 40c, coincident with the peak in the HI distribution shown in Verdes--Montenegro et al. (2001). Its velocity field is regular. The kinematic position angle does not change with radius.

The rotation curve is symmetric, reaching a plateau at 6 kpc, however, this curve does not reach the optical radius R _ 25 . At radii larger than 10 kpc, there is no more H emission, as it is possible to note from the monochromatic image.

gmw_00004992.name: HCG 40d
gmw_00004992.overview: HCG 40d is a barred early-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It is classified as a star-forming galaxy that exhibits concentrated, two-peaked H-alpha emission likely associated with star formation at the end of its bar [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HCG 40d as a star-forming barred early-type spiral with concentrated two-peaked H emission probably tied to star formation at the bar end.

quotes.quote: HCG 40a, HCG 40d and HCG 40e were classified as radio sources (dLNR, LNR and Sy2 respectively) in Coziol et al. (1998) and re-classified as no-emission galaxy, SFG and SFG in Coziol et al. (2004).

The strongest H emission of the group is detected in two peaks, in the barred early-type spiral HCG 40d. This emission is very concentrated in the central region, probably associated with star formation at the end of the bar. We could not derived the rotation curve for this object.

gmw_00004993.name: HCG 40e
gmw_00004993.overview: HCG 40e is a far-infrared source [arXiv:1003.0345] classified as a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It exhibits weak hydrogen emission across its disk, and the two sides of its rotation curve do not match [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 40e as a radio/FIR source reclassified as star-forming, with weak disk H emission and a rotation curve whose two sides do not match.

quotes.quote: HCG 40a, HCG 40d and HCG 40e were classified as radio sources (dLNR, LNR and Sy2 respectively) in Coziol et al. (1998) and re-classified as no-emission galaxy, SFG and SFG in Coziol et al. (2004).
HCG 40e is a FIR source (Allam et al. 1996).
HCG 40e shows a weak H emission on its disk. Both sides of the rotation curve do not match.

gmw_00004994.name: HCG 47a
gmw_00004994.overview: HCG 47a is an oblong barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345, arXiv:1509.05779] that is tidally interacting with HCG 47b [arXiv:1509.05779]. It features a southwest ring structure with strong emission [arXiv:1003.0345], while its center contains an active galactic nucleus and lacks molecular gas [arXiv:1509.05779].

sources.summary: The paper presents HCG 47a as an SBb absorption-line galaxy with a southwest ring, strong H emission in that ring, and a modest kinematic-optical PA offset.

quotes.quote: HCG 47a is a SBb (Hickson 1993) galaxy, showing a ring-shape structure on its south west side (Fasano et al. 1994). Shimada et al. (2000) classified HCG 47a as an absorption-line galaxy. Hickson classified HCG 47b, HCG 47c and HCG 47d as E3, Sc and Sd galaxies. HCG 47a and HCG 47b are radio and FIR sources (Allam et al. 1996).
We detected a strong H emission in the ring of HCG 47a.

The velocity field shows higher velocities in the eastern part of the approaching side than in the western region, probably due to the presence of the ring. The kinematic position angle differs from the optical one, at 2 h ^ 1 kpc and at R _ 25 , in 14 ^ in both cases.

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 47a as an oblong spiral interacting with HCG 47b, near the MOHEG threshold, with central AGN/radio emission and ring-plus-spiral molecular gas.

quotes.quote: HCG 47a : Figure fig:hcg47 shows that HCG 47a is an oblong spiral (in both PAH and optical images from SDSS; sdss ) that is tidally interacting with HCG 47b. HCG 47a is also on the cusp of being considered a MOHEG ( L_ H_2,warm /L_ 7.7 m PAH = 0.035; cluver+13 ). The center, while void of molecular gas, contains a spectrally classified AGN stern+12 as well as 1.4 GHz emission. The CO(1--0) emission traces the oblong spiral, and so the molecular gas is morphologically classified as both a ring and a spiral.

gmw_00004995.name: HCG 47d
gmw_00004995.overview: HCG 47d is classified as an Sd spiral galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It features a regular velocity field, an offset between its kinematic and optical position angles, and extended disk emission with the strongest hydrogen emission located on its northern side [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 47d as an Sd galaxy with strong northern H emission, extended disk emission, a regular velocity field, and a kinematic-optical PA offset.

quotes.quote: Hickson classified HCG 47b, HCG 47c and HCG 47d as E3, Sc and Sd galaxies.
The north side of HCG 47d shows the strongest H emission in this galaxy. However, we also note an extended emission in its disk.

HCG 47d shows a regular velocity field. However, there is an offset between the kinematic and optical position angle of 32 ^ and 23 ^ (Mendes de Oliveira 1992 and HyperLeda, respectively). For HCG 47a and 47d it was not possible to obtain the rotation curves (the procedure developed by Epinat et al. 2008a does not converge for these galaxies).

gmw_00004996.name: HCG 49a
gmw_00004996.overview: HCG 49a is a spiral galaxy that exhibits hydrogen-alpha emission across its entire disk [arXiv:1003.0345]. The galaxy features a regular velocity field and a symmetric rotation curve where both sides match relatively well [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper finds HCG 49a's emission comes from one object, with H across the disk, a regular velocity field, modest PA differences, and a symmetric rotation curve.

quotes.quote: Fasano et al. (1996) noted that HCG 49a seems to be a superposition of two galaxies but our monochromatic map shows that the emission is coming from only one object. We analyzed the H profile in the region where the superposition appears, and we do not detect a defined profile. The monochromatic map of HCG 49a shows H emission across the disk of this object. This emission peaks at the same place in the monochromatic and the continuum map.

This object shows a regular velocity field. Kinematic position angle differs only in 7 ^ from the optical position angle at 2 h ^ 1 kpc. However, the difference is larger if we take the optical position angle at R _ 25 (16 ^ ). The rotation curve is symmetric and both sides match relatively well.

gmw_00004997.name: HCG 49b
gmw_00004997.overview: HCG 49b is a low-mass galaxy dominated by a recent burst of star formation [arXiv:1304.4493] that has a low oxygen abundance [arXiv:1304.4493]. It exhibits a perturbed velocity field [arXiv:1003.0345] and is classified as an outlier on the Tully-Fisher relation [arXiv:1003.0345, arXiv:1304.4493].

sources.summary: The paper treats HCG 49b as a low-mass Tully-Fisher outlier with a perturbed velocity field, changing kinematic PA, and mismatched rotation-curve sides.

quotes.quote: We confirm the tendency for compact group galaxies at the low-mass end of the Tully-Fisher relation (HCG 49b, 89d, 96c, 96d and 100c) to have either a magnitude that is too bright for its mass (suggesting brightening by star formation) and/or a low maximum rotational velocity for its luminosity (suggesting tidal stripping). These galaxies are outside the Tully Fisher relation, at the 1 level, even when the minimum acceptable values of inclinations are used to compute their maximum velocities.

HCG 49b shows a perturbed velocity field. The kinematic position angle changes along the radius and it differs from the optical one at 2 h ^ 1 kpc in 17 ^ . However, at R _ 25 the optical and kinematic position angles reach a similar value (Table table3 ). The rotation curve of HCG 49b extended out to 12.5 arcsec( 8.5 kpc). Both sides do not match. The receding side reaches values of 70 km s ^ 1 and the approaching side reaches values of 75 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 49b as a low-mass Tully-Fisher outlier dominated by recent star formation, with low oxygen abundance and unusually high H luminosity.

quotes.quote: HCG 49b In Fig. spectra_49b we show the SED and the SDSS optical spectra of HCG 49b (left and right panels, respectively). This object is clearly dominated by star formation, displaying little continuum. As described in 3.4, the oxygen abundance measurement obtained for 49b is well below solar 12+log(0/H)=8.15 0.14. We found fairly good agreement between the observed and the synthetic spectral energy distribution of HCG 49b.

In fact, HCG 49b has the highest H luminosity in the sample of 200 HCG galaxies studied by Mart inez et al. (2010), even higher than the H luminosity of the individual members of the merger system HCG 31. Therefore, HCG 49b has clearly a recent burst of star formation, which could increase its luminosity.

gmw_00004998.name: HCG 49c
gmw_00004998.overview: HCG 49c is classified as an irregular Im galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It exhibits weak hydrogen-alpha emission, a regular velocity field, and a hydrogen-alpha bridge connecting it to the neighboring galaxy HCG 49b [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 49c as an Im galaxy with weak H emission, an H bridge toward member b, a regular-looking velocity field, and a large PA mismatch.

quotes.quote: HCG 49a and HCG 49b are late-type spiral galaxies, HCG 49c is a Im and HCG 49d is a E5 (Hickson 1993).
The monochromatic map of HCG 49c shows a much lower intensity than HCG 49a and b. We detected a bridge in the H emission between members b and . The velocity field seems regular.

However, a disagreement of 64 ^ is observed between the kinematic and optical position angles. Both sides of the rotation curve match from the center out to 2.5 kpc. Beyond this radius, no H emission is detected in the approaching side.

gmw_00004999.name: HCG 54
gmw_00004999.overview: HCG 54 was historically classified as a group containing three irregular galaxies and one Sdm galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. However, more recent data supports the scenario that HCG 54 is actually a single merger-remnant galaxy surrounded by neutral hydrogen tidal tails and shells [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper discusses HCG 54 as a merger-remnant system with HI tidal tails and shells, and says its data support interpreting the group as one galaxy.

quotes.quote: HCG 54 Hickson et al. (1989) classified this group as formed by three irregular galaxies and one Sdm galaxy. Verdes-Montenegro et al. (2002) found HI tidal tails and shells in the environment of HCG 54. They concluded that this group is a merger remnant.

From our monochromatic map we note that HCG 54b shows the strongest H emission and its velocity field shows an amplitude of 30 km s ^ 1 . HCG 54b and 54d also appear in the monochromatic map, however it is difficult to get some information from the velocity field. HCG 54a is detected in the continuum image. Our data supports the scenario that HCG 54 is in fact one only galaxy and not a group. Due to the nature of this object (Verdes-Montenegro et al. 2002) we decided do not obtain any kinematic parameter of its members.

gmw_00005000.name: HCG 56a
gmw_00005000.overview: HCG 56a is a galaxy featuring extended hydrogen-alpha emission along its disk, with two symmetric emission peaks detected on its northern and southern sides [arXiv:1003.0345]. The galaxy exhibits a regular velocity field and a symmetric rotation curve, with no misalignment between its kinematic and optical position angles [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper finds HCG 56a has extended disk H emission with two symmetric peaks, a regular velocity field, no PA misalignment, and a symmetric rotation curve.

quotes.quote: The monochromatic map of HCG 56a shows an extended emission along the disk. Peaks in the H emission are detected in the southern and northern side of this galaxy. Both peaks are located at the same distance with respect to the peak in the continuum image.

The velocity field of HCG 56a seems to be regular. We noted that the approaching side is more extended than the receding side. We do not detect any misalignment between the kinematic and optical position angles for this galaxy.
The rotation curve of HCG 56a is symmetric. The approaching and receding sides match from 3 to 18 (2 to 10 kpc), where the approaching side reaches a plateau.

gmw_00005001.name: HCG 56b
gmw_00005001.overview: HCG 56b is an SB0 lenticular galaxy classified as a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It has been detected in the infrared and exhibits central hydrogen-alpha emission [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 56b as an SB0 Seyfert galaxy with infrared detections and central H emission, but says only central emission prevented a rotation curve.

quotes.quote: HCG 56 This quintet if formed by the late-type spiral HCG 56a, the SB0 HCG 56b and three SO galaxies (, d, e). Coziol et al. (2004) classified members a, d and e as Star Forming Galaxies, HCG 56b as a Seyfert galaxy and HCG 56c as a no-emission line galaxy.
Allam et al.(1996) detected members b, and d at 25 60 and 100 m.

We detected H emission at the center of the S0 galaxies HCG 56b, HCG 56c and HCG 56d. Given that we only detected H emission at the center of these objects, it was not possible to obtain their rotation curves.

gmw_00005002.name: HCG 68c
gmw_00005002.overview: HCG 68c, also known as NGC 5350, is a blue barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345, arXiv:1508.07043] classified as either a LINER or a starburst galaxy [arXiv:1705.06210]. It features a regular velocity field [arXiv:1003.0345], bright H II regions along its spiral arms [arXiv:1003.0345], and a large, unperturbed neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1508.07043].

sources.summary: The paper describes HCG 68c as a barred spiral with bright HII regions, nuclear H emission, a regular velocity field, aligned PA at R25, and an extended rotation curve.

quotes.quote: HCG 68 This group is formed by four early-type galaxies (HCG 68a, b, d and e) and one barred spiral galaxy (HCG 68c) (Hikcson 1993).

We detected several bright HII regions on the well defined arms of HCG 68c. On the nuclear region, we detected a peak in H , nevertheless, no emission was detected across the bar of this object, as also reported by Vilchez et al. (1998). The maximum intensity in the monochromatic map of HCG 68c corresponds to an HII region located 60 from its center (north west side of the galaxy).

The velocity field of HCG 68c is quite regular. The kinematic and optical (R _ 25 ) position angles are aligned. The difference between the kinematic and optical position angle at 2 h ^ 1 is caused by the influence of the bar at this radius. The rotation curve of HCG 68c reaches 115 (19 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5350 as a northern HCG 68 member with radio emission, a surrounding envelope, LINER or starburst classification, and extended envelope emission.

quotes.quote: At 4.85 GHz the radio emitting structure covers three group members: northernmost NGC 5350 is connected with the close pair NGC 5353 and 5354 through a narrow trail that is most probably an effect of the beam smearing. The whole envelope extends far beyond the closest vicinity of the member galaxies, also containing an object without an optical counterpart (most possibly a background source) on its western side. Its northern part forms an envelope surrounding NGC 5350. At 1.40 GHz, there is no connection between this galaxy and the aforementioned pair, but hints at the presence of an envelope remain.

The most recognisable part of the group is the triplet of galaxies NGC 5350--5353--5354. All of these galaxies are radio loud, with NGC 5350 classified as either LINER, or starburst chapelon99 and NGC 5354 as a possible Seyfert galaxy veron-cetty06 . Subtraction of the compact sources at 1.40 GHz leaves most of the emission from NGC 5354 intact, as well as two small extensions of the envelope of NGC 5350.

sources.summary: The paper identifies HCG 68C as NGC 5350, a blue spiral whose H I forms a double-horned profile and disk-like distribution matching the VLA high-surface-brightness features.

quotes.quote: For example, HCG 68 shows a double-horned profile at the systemic redshift of HCG 68C (NGC 5350) peaking at the pointing closest to its spatial location.

HCG 68 The \ detected in this group comes from the double-horn profiled centered at the redshift of galaxy HCG 68C (NGC 5350). The spatial position of is consistent with originating in the disk of this blue spiral galaxy HCG 68C. The peaks at position B4 that is closest to the position of galaxy HCG 68C. The good match between observed and modeled masses thus suggests that the total distribution is almost identical to the HSB features observed by the VLA. The profile and distribution are consistent with a large unperturbed disk as imaged by the VLA and shown in Figure fig-vla_maps also see Figure 25 of tang08 .

gmw_00005003.name: HCG 93b
gmw_00005003.overview: HCG 93b is a barred spiral galaxy that has been classified as both an active galactic nucleus and a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1003.0345]. It features hydrogen-alpha emission across its bar, H II regions in its arms, and a twisted velocity field with asymmetric rotation [arXiv:1003.0345].

sources.summary: The paper presents HCG 93b as a barred spiral classified as AGN or star-forming, with bar H emission, HII regions, a twisted velocity field, and asymmetric rotation.

quotes.quote: HCG 93 This quartet is formed by two barred spiral galaxies, HCG 93b and HCG 93c, one E1 galaxy (HCG 93a) and the SB0 galaxy HCG 93d (Hickson 1993). Members a and were classified as AGN by Shimada et al. (2000) and Coziol et al. (2004). HCG 93b is classified as an AGN by Shimada et al. (2004) but Coziol et al. (2004) classified it as a Star Forming Galaxy.

Its monochromatic map shows H emission across the bar and two bright knots at the end of it, produced by star formation resulting from the compression of gas in this place, in agreement with its morphological type (Phillips 1996, Pisano et al. 2000). In the arms, we detected a few HII regions.

The velocity field of HCG 93b shows a twist. We detected a disagreement between optical and kinematic position angles of 17 ^ and 134 ^ (Mendes de Oliveira et al. 2003 and HyperLeda, respectively). Both sides of the rotation curve of HCG 93b do not match. From 20 (7 kpc), the approaching side falls, reaching values of 120 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00005004.name: RA =13^h30^m16.^s ; =+47 10 22
gmw_00005004.overview: Located east of the galaxy M51, RA =13^h30^m16.^s; =+47 10 22 is a background radio galaxy [arXiv:1012.0212]. It features strong radio lobes and exhibits a steep spectrum, being brighter at 20 cm than at 6 cm [arXiv:1012.0212].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a background radio galaxy east of M51 with strong radio lobes, a steep spectrum implied by brighter 20 cm than 6 cm emission, and no 3.6 cm detection.

quotes.quote: In the 20 cm and 6 cm images, one background radio galaxy with strong radio lobes is observed east (RA =13^h30^m16.^s ; =+47 10 22 ) of M51. This object is brighter at 20 cm than 6 cm, which implies a steep spectrum source. It is not seen in the 3.6 cm map since it lies outside the mosaic.

gmw_00005005.name: Sumo Puff
gmw_00005005.overview: Sumo Puff is a giant low-surface-brightness stellar cloud with an elongated morphology and a flat light distribution [arXiv:1704.06681]. It is interpreted either as a galaxy-sized tidal feature from a recent merger event or as a foreground, quenched ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1704.06681].

sources.summary: The paper reports Sumo Puff as a giant low-surface-brightness stellar cloud, weighing tidal debris from a recent merger against a foreground disturbed ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy scenario.

quotes.quote: While we do not have a redshift for this object, it is in close angular proximity to a post-merger galaxy at redshift z=0.0431 and is projected within a few virial radii (assuming similar redshifts) of two other L_ galaxies, which we use to bracket a potential redshift range of 0.0055 < z < 0.0431 . The object's light distribution is flat, as characterized by a low \ index ( n 0.3 ). It has a low central g band surface brightness of 26.4 , large effective radius of 13 ( 11 kpc at z=0.0431 and 1.5 kpc at z=0.0055 ), and an elongated morphology ( b/a 0.4 ). Its red color ( g-i 1 ) is consistent with a passively evolving stellar population and similar to the nearby post-merger galaxy , and we may see tidal material connecting Sumo Puff with this galaxy. We offer two possible interpretations for the nature of this object: (1) it is an extreme, galaxy-size tidal feature associated with a recent merger event, or (2) it is a foreground dwarf galaxy with properties consistent with a quenched, disturbed ultra-diffuse galaxy.

Deeper images could confirm the presence of this bridge material and would argue strongly that Sumo Puff is tidal. Considering the evidence presented in this section, we favor a scenario in which \ is tidal debris from a satellite that is being accreted by . This accretion event is not necessarily associated with the more compact tidal features that are clearly present in .

gmw_00005006.name: SDSS J144916.46-004240.5
gmw_00005006.overview: SDSS J144916.46-004240.5 is a post-merger galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0431 [arXiv:1704.06681]. It features a massive tidal stream, shell-like structures, and a possible low-surface-brightness bridge connecting it to a nearby object [arXiv:1704.06681].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J144916.46-004240.5 as a z=0.0431 post-merger galaxy near Sumo Puff, with a massive tidal stream, shell-like features, and possible bridge material linking the objects.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, it is in close angular proximity to a visually striking post-merger galaxy (SDSS J144916.46-004240.5; \ hereafter) with a similar red color, which hosts a massive tidal stream with a sharp turnaround point in stellar orbits that manifests as a caustic in surface brightness Tremaine:1999aa . It is tempting to assume that \ is tidal debris associated with .

We note that the combination of \ and the sharp shell-like feature in the upper-left region of the post-merger \ (see fig:image ) is still quite puzzling, since both structures appear on the same northern side of the main galaxy. This may suggest that they are due to a single merger event, but it is also possible that they were created during independent merger events.

Close inspection of the images shown in s fig:image and fig:model-fits reveals a possible bridge of low-surface-brightness material connecting \ and . If this feature is real, it would strongly support the tidal scenario.

gmw_00005007.name: RG01
gmw_00005007.overview: RG01 is a radio galaxy located in the Bullet cluster field [arXiv:1705.08405]. Its radio jet and lobe exhibit a non-thermal Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect [arXiv:1705.08405, arXiv:2009.03440], which has been used to constrain the low-energy threshold of its electron spectrum [arXiv:2009.03440].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RG01 as a Bullet-cluster-field radio galaxy whose jet/lobe shows non-thermal SZ emission, a typical non-thermal electron spectrum, and no detectable Chandra X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: RG01 is one of the radio galaxies detected in the Bullet cluster field at frequencies 2.1, 5.5, 9.0 and 16--24 GHz in ATCA observations. The radio galaxy RG01 -- and therefore its radio jet/lobe -- is located approximately 180 or 800 kpc away from the center of the Bullet cluster. The radio galaxy, and the jet/lobe region, is also approximately 160 , or 700 kpc away from the nearest radio halo region with diffuse radio emission, which is detected up to 10 GHz 2016Ap&SS.361..255M,2015MNRAS.449.1486S . In addition, there is no detectable X-ray emission with Chandra in this region. These facts (i.e., the large distance between RG01 and the center of the Bullet cluster, the absence of X-ray emission, the region containing RG01 being far away from the shock front as well as the cold front, and the SZE being in the radio jet/lobe region where synchrotron has already been detected at 2.1, 5.5 and 9.0 GHz) imply that the SZE we detect can only be of non-thermal origin.

The non-thermal SZE in the RG01 lobe We detected a non-thermal SZE signal in the jet/lobe of the radio galaxy RG01 located at coordinates (J2000)RA: 06^ h 58^ m 14.2^ s DEC: 55 54 25 and shown in the blue-colored region of Fig. composite01 .

sources.summary: The paper models RG01 as the first detected non-thermal SZ radio galaxy and uses the detection to constrain the low-energy cutoff of its electron spectrum to p_min about 1-2.

quotes.quote: The analysis presented in this paper can successfully model the recent first detection of non-thermal SZ effect from the radio galaxy RG01 MDCMSNW2017 . This gives us the confidence in predicting the non-thermal SZ distortion for a further sample of 20 radio galaxy sources (in Table tab:table2 ) which can be targeted by upcoming ground based SZ searches. For a given intensity of distortion on the CMB, there is a degeneracy between y_ NT and g_ NT (x) . In contrast to previous studies, we can predict y_ NT from our galaxy evolution model which can then constrain the value of spectral function g_ NT (x) . Radio and SZ detection of radio cocoons can help determine the physical properties of energetic electrons inside the cocoons. For the non-thermal SZ detection from RG01, we are able to constrain the value of p_ min (the lowest energy threshold of the electron spectrum) using SZ effect for the very first time. We find p_ min 1-2 is needed to explain the observations.

gmw_00005008.name: TXS 1700+685
gmw_00005008.overview: TXS 1700+685 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of z = 0.301 [arXiv:2203.02672]. The source features relativistic jets oriented toward the observer and has exhibited multiple periods of enhanced gamma-ray activity, including flaring states in 2009 and 2021 [arXiv:2203.02672].

sources.summary: The paper studies TXS 1700+685 as a z=0.301 FSRQ/blazar with renewed 2021 gamma-ray activity, modelling its flaring-state broadband emission with SSC and BLR-dominated external Compton components.

quotes.quote: The flat rising and steep falling profile in the ray SED as well as the break or spectral curvature at 1 GeV are in commensuration with the flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) nature of the source. galaxies: active – galaxies: jets – quasars: individual: TXS 1700+685 – gamma-rays: galaxies – ultraviolet: galaxies – X-rays: galaxies Blazar sources are a subclass of active galactic nuclei (AGNs) that emit copiously in all frequencies extending from the radio to very high-energy (more than a few tens of GeV) ray band, and their relativistic jets are oriented almost along the line of sight (angles less than 14^ ) to the observer urry1995unified .

Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope onboard the Large Area Telescope (LAT) detected enhanced ray activity on March 22, 2009 from the blazar TXS 1700+685 2009ATel.1986....1G . This source is also known as 4FGL J1700.0+6830 2020ApJS..247...33A and is located at redshift z = 0.301 1997MNRAS.290..380H with R.A. = 255.038 deg. and Decl. = +68.5019 petrov2008sixth . Recently this source has shown renewed ray activity on March 15, 2021 when the daily averaged gamma-ray flux ( E > 100 MeV) rising to (1.1 0.2) 10^ 6 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , with the detection of a 29 GeV photon 2021ATel14463....1C .

In the present study, we comment on the variability and the spectral characteristics of the blazar TXS 1700+685 using a two-component external Compton model along with SSC for the first time. For the broadband spectral modeling, we focus on the flaring phase for which the simultaneous X-ray observation is present, and the emission region is found to be within BLR. The high energy peak of the broadband SED corresponding to the flaring state is mainly reproduced by the EC process of seed photons originating from the BLR and DT, and the contribution from the disk is found to be sub-dominant.

gmw_00005009.name: AndXXVII
gmw_00005009.overview: Andromeda XXVII is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy located in the Local Group [arXiv:1102.2902]. It is embedded in a great northern arc of stars and appears to be undergoing tidal disruption by M31, serving as the likely progenitor of the Northwest stream [arXiv:1102.2902, arXiv:1102.3501].

sources.summary: The paper identifies And XXVII as a newly discovered M31 dwarf spheroidal embedded in the North-West arc and possibly undergoing tidal disruption.

quotes.quote: The five new dwarf galaxies were straightforward to detect as over-densities in the matched-filter surface density maps of metal-poor RGB stars. There are no other obvious candidate satellites visible in this map or in our equivalent average metallicity and metal-rich maps. For simplicity and following convention, we have named them Andromeda XXIII - XXVII (And XXIII - XXVII) after the constellation they are found in. As noted earlier the locations of all previously known dSphs (blue circles) and the five new dwarfs (red circles) are readily visible in Fig. fig_map . Moving from East to West (left to right in the Figure) the new galaxies are And XXIII, And XXIV, And XXVII, And XXV and And XXVI.

And XXVII appears to be in the process of being tidally disrupted by M31. It is embedded in a great Northern arc of stars which loops around M31. The stream of stars surrounding the galaxy has made it very difficult to extract a clean reference field to characterize the properties of the foreground and/or background.

sources.summary: The paper describes AndXXVII as a newly identified dwarf near the NW stream and treats it as a likely or possible progenitor/source inferred from stream globular clusters and expected velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: The newly identified dwarf galaxy AndXXVII Richardson:11 , just above the upper branch of the stream in Figure fig_M31 , is masked out to exclude it from the local background correction.

The presence of several globular clusters along the course of the stream Mackey:10 and the possibility that AndXXVII Richardson:11 is the source of the stream suggests that the internal velocity dispersion is at the level that a dwarf galaxy would create, 3 or more.
The association of globular clusters with the NW stream Huxor:08,Mackey:10 indicates that a dwarf galaxy, likely AndXXVII, is the source of the stream.

gmw_00005010.name: BDF-3299
gmw_00005010.overview: BDF-3299 is a high-redshift galaxy at z=7.109 that exhibits a multi-component morphology [arXiv:2106.05279, arXiv:1601.03442]. The system features resolved UV- and IR-bright components with spatial offsets between its [CII] and [OIII] emission lines, which may indicate an ongoing merger [arXiv:2106.05279, arXiv:2006.02447].

sources.summary: The paper includes BDF3299 among bright EoR [OIII] and [CII] emitters, notes its multi-component morphology and spatial line offsets, and warns against clump-level modeling.

quotes.quote: More than 30 EoR galaxies have been so far detected, and often spatially resolved in [ CII ] e.g. for compilations carniani2018, matthee2019 and nine very bright galaxies (see Table tab:higzsources ) at z 6-9 have been detected in [ OIII ] . The list includes MACS1149-JD1 hashimoto2018 , A2744-YD4 laporte2017 , MACS416-Y1 tamura2019 , SXDF-NB1006-2 inoue2016 , B14-65666 hashimoto2019 , BDF3299 carniani2017 , J0121 J0235, J1211 harikane2020 . All of them have been detected also in [ CII ] , albeit at fainter fluxes with respect to [ OIII ] carniani2020, bakx2020, hashimoto2019, maiolino2015, harikane2020 , and therefore are suitable for use in our model.

Among the galaxies in our sample, B14-65666 and BDF3299 show a clear multi component morphology, MACS416-Y1 and BDF3299 have spatial offsets between [ CII ] and [ OIII ] . B14-65666 has been spatially resolved in two clumps (A and B) which are both detected in [ CII ] , [ OIII ] and IR continuum hashimoto2019 . This allow us to apply our model to B14-5666 and its two clumps separately. Doing the same for BDF3299 is impossible because the [ CII ] and the [ OIII ] are not co-spatial.

sources.summary: The paper describes this close z~7 Ly emitter as part of a bright pair with strong Ly equivalent width, 4.4 pMpc separation, and Ly visibility requiring ionized surroundings plus nearby sources.

quotes.quote: The BDF is the only field where two close-by bright Ly emitting galaxies, BDF-3299 (z=7.109) and BDF-521 (z=7.008) have been found Vanzella2011 . These two L L ^* objects, originally selected as z-dropout candidates C10b hereafter Castellano2010b , show Ly equivalent width > 50 and are separated by a distance of only 4.4 proper Mpc (pMpc). The detection of bright Ly emission from them can be explained by these sources being embedded in an HII region that allows Ly photons to redshift far away from the line center before they reach the almost neutral IGM. Vanzella2011 has compared the size of the HII region these galaxies can build Loeb2005 to the minimum ionized radius allowing their Ly photons to escape Wyithe2005 , finding that they both cannot generate HII regions large enough to explain the visibility of their lines even if a maximum Lyman continuum escape fraction f _ esc =1 is assumed. Instead, the visibility of their Ly can be explained by the presence of additional ionizing sources in their vicinity Dayal2009 .

sources.summary: The paper describes BDF-3299 as a resolved z>7 system with UV- and IR-bright components whose nominal metallicity differences could indicate a merger, though uncertainties remain large.

quotes.quote: BDF-3299 is a compelling example of resolved structure in our sample. Our fiducial case gives limits <7.9 and >8.1 for the UV- and IR-bright components respectively. Likewise B14-65666 is resolved into two distinct clumps, where luminosities reported by suggest that clump ``A'' is more metal-rich by a few tenths of a dex than clump ``B'' for our fiducial case. These nominal differences could indicate mergers in both systems, where the more enriched component likely dominates the stellar mass. Although the metallicities of each component are not distinguishable given the uncertainties, it illustrates the prospects of resolving chemical enrichment and metal mixing at z>7 .

gmw_00005011.name: Pisces II
gmw_00005011.overview: Pisces II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 180 kpc [arXiv:1002.0504]. It has a half-light radius of 60 pc and contains both red giant branch and blue horizontal branch stars [arXiv:1002.0504].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Pisces II as an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy at 180 kpc, reports a 60 pc half-light radius, and uses deeper KPNO photometry to clarify its CMD and stellar populations.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Properties of Pisces II and Segue 3 Parameter & Pisces II & Segue 3 RA (J2000) & 22:58:31 6 & 21:21:31 4 Dec (J2000) & +05:57:09 4 & +19:07:02 4

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of two new Milky Way satellites in the neighboring constellations of Pisces and Pegasus identified in data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. Pisces II, an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy lies at the distance of 180 kpc, some 15 ^ away from the recently detected Pisces I. Segue 3, an ultra-faint star cluster lies at the distance of 16 kpc. We use deep follow-up imaging obtained with the 4-m Mayall telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory to derive their structural parameters. Pisces II has a half-light radius of 60 pc, while Segue 3 is twenty times smaller at only 3pc.

Pisces II - The KPNO photometry reaches at least 2 magnitudes fainter than the SDSS and plays a crucial role in the identification and analysis of Pisces II, as can be seen from Fig. fig:fig1_kpno . The stellar over-density is enhanced, with a significance of 8 . The CMD is now quite unambiguous, with both RGB and BHB clearly visible together with a pile-up of stars around the main sequence turn-off (MSTO). The very tight BHB can now be used to estimate the distance modulus of the system (m M) _0 = 21.3 .

gmw_00005012.name: VLA J2000 02:17:37.21 -05:13:27.96
gmw_00005012.overview: VLA J2000 02:17:37.21 -05:13:27.96 is an elliptical lens galaxy located within a massive galaxy group [arXiv:0902.4804]. Its optical surface brightness is well fit by a Seersic profile with an index of approximately 5.2 [arXiv:0902.4804].

sources.summary: The paper models this foreground lens galaxy in a group environment, using CFHT/HST photometry and spectroscopy to constrain its surface-brightness shape, stellar component, and dark-matter contribution.

quotes.quote: The coordinates of the elliptical lens on the CFHTLS image is _ J2000 =02:17:37.136, _ J2000 =-05:13:29.41. Radio and X-ray properties Simpson2006 found this object to be a radio source (VLA J2000 02:17:37.21 -05:13:27.96 ; SXD J2000 02:17:37.21 -05:13:28.0). Its environment was investigated by geach021737 . The lens galaxy inhabits a massive galaxy group (or equivalently a low mass galaxy cluster) at z_ grp = 0.648 0.001 , with an optical spectroscopic velocity dispersion ^ spec _ grp = 770 170 .

The HST imaging allows us to make precise shape measurements of the lens galaxy optical surface brightness. Using the galfit facility galfit , we found that the lens galaxy is well fit by a S ersic surface mass density profile (assuming constant stellar mass-to-light ratio) of the form (R) = _e b_n (R/ )^ 1/n 1 , with S ersic index n 5.2 (corresponding to b_n 10.07 ) and effective radius . We thus measured total model magnitudes F606W=20.90 0.05 and F814W=19.71 0.01 and effective radii =0 90 0 10 6.20 0.7 in the F814W band.
The lensing galaxy is modeled with two components (stars and dark matter), and we include the perturbing effect of the group environment, and all available constraints.

gmw_00005013.name: dE5
gmw_00005013.overview: The dwarf early-type galaxy dE5, also known as UGCA 298, has been detected in the ALFA-ALFA radio survey [arXiv:2007.13984]. It possesses a low gas mass that suggests its current star-formation activity is temporary and in its final phase [arXiv:2007.13984].

sources.summary: The paper identifies dE5 as UGCA 298, reports an ALFA-ALFA gas detection, and argues its low gas mass implies brief, final-phase star formation.

quotes.quote: Examining the literature for radio observations of these dEs, we find that dE5 (UGCA 298) was detected by the ALFA-ALFA survey. It has a gas mass of 1.7 10 ^ 7 M _ , approximately 10 times lower than its stellar mass. Assuming an HI consumption fraction of 10 , the star-formation activity will last only a few Myr with the current SFR (0.025 M _ yr ^ 1 ). It is thus very likely that dE5's star formation is temporal and at its final phase before turning into a true red and dead dE.

gmw_00005014.name: dE6
gmw_00005014.overview: The dwarf early-type galaxy dE6 is located near the NGC 3945 group at a sky-projected distance of 780 kpc [arXiv:2007.13984]. It is characterized by strong hydrogen emission, which requires direct emission-line flux measurements [arXiv:2007.13984].

sources.summary: The paper places dE6 near the NGC 3945 group, adopts that distance, and notes its strong H emission required direct emission-line flux measurements.

quotes.quote: One, dE6, is located near to NGC 3945 group at a sky-projected distance of 780 kpc from the group center, and the relative line-of-sight radial velocity is as low as 155 km s ^ 1 . We consider a distance of NGC 3945 (21.6 Mpc; ) as the distance to dE6.
The fit is not satisfactory in the cases of dE1 and dE6, which have the strongest H emission.
In the cases of dE1 and dE6, we directly measure the emission-line fluxes from their original SDSS spectra because, as mentioned above, the fitting of the old stellar population is not satisfactory.

gmw_00005015.name: J033245.11-274724.0
gmw_00005015.overview: J033245.11-274724.0 is a compact, luminous infrared galaxy dominated by a dust-enshrouded disk [arXiv:0901.0718]. The galaxy forms stars at a rate of 20 solar masses per year and physically resembles a gas-rich major-merger remnant [arXiv:0901.0718].

sources.summary: The paper describes J033245.11-274724.0 as a compact LIRG dominated by a dust-enshrouded disk, forming stars rapidly and resembling a gas-rich major-merger remnant that could become a massive late-type spiral.

quotes.quote: A significant part of the evolution in the density of cosmic star formation is related to the rapid evolution of both Luminous IR galaxies (LIRGs) and Luminous Compact galaxies: here we study the properties of a distant, compact galaxy, J033245.11-274724.0, which is also a LIRG. Given the photometric and spectrophotometric accuracies of data of all wavelengths, we can decompose the galaxy into sub-components and correct them for reddening. Combination of deep imagery and kinematics provides a reasonable physical model of the galaxy. The galaxy is dominated by a dust-enshrouded disk revealed by UDF imagery. The disk radius is half that of the Milky Way and the galaxy forms stars at a rate of 20 M _ /yr.

The detailed physical properties of J033245.11-274724.0 resemble the expectations of modeling the merger of two equal-mass, gaseous-rich galaxies, 0.5 Gyr after the merger. They cannot be reproduced by any combination of intrinsic disk perturbations alone, given the absence of any significant outflow mechanisms. In its later evolution, J033245.11-274724.0 could become a massive, late-type spiral that evolves to become part of the Tully-Fisher relation, with an angular momentum induced mostly by the orbital angular-momentum of the merger.

gmw_00005016.name: AEGIS 8907
gmw_00005016.overview: AEGIS 8907 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.5 [arXiv:2009.03926]. Its resolved spectral energy distributions appear redder and younger on average because its rest-frame ultraviolet slope is poorly constrained by the available resolved imaging [arXiv:2009.03926].

sources.summary: AEGIS 8907 is used as a case where resolved SEDs appear redder and younger because the rest-frame UV slope is poorly constrained; the Wuyts method better reproduces integrated photometry.

quotes.quote: A notable exception is that the resolved SEDs of the z 1.5 galaxies are on average redder and younger than indicated by the broadband SED fits, as is showcased by AEGIS 8907 in fig:sedcomp . The AEGIS 8907 photometry demonstrates that this discrepancy is caused by the lack of resolved imaging covering the rest-frame UV for our z 1.5 sample.

With the exception of AEGIS 8907, the integrated SEDs are consistent with each other. As discussed in sec:intparam , the UV slope of AEGIS 8907 is not well constrained due to there only being one resolved filter available at 1250--2500 . Therefore, our SED fitting procedure prioritizes fitting the data in the near-IR that is covered by the IRAC photometry, whereas the Wuyts et al. method emphasizes the shape of the optical photometry initially and later adjusts to best-match the integrated 3D-HST SED.

However, in cases such as AEGIS 8907, the Wuyts et al. method better reproduces the overall shape of the integrated 3D-HST photometry since the UV slope is unconstrained by the resolved photometry for low-redshift objects using our methodology (see sec:intparam ).

gmw_00005017.name: 3C 328
gmw_00005017.overview: 3C 328 is an asymmetric Fanaroff-Riley type-II-like radio galaxy with a hotspot in only one of its lobes [arXiv:2104.13183]. Its nucleus has recently restarted active jet production after a period of quiescence, showing a short sub-arcsecond jet pointing toward its fading near-side lobe [arXiv:2104.13183].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 328 as an asymmetric FR II-like radio galaxy whose nucleus recently restarted after quiescence, with a short VLBA jet pointing toward the fading near-side lobe.

quotes.quote: As a rule, both lobes of Fanaroff-Riley (FR) type-II radio sources are terminated with hotspots, but the 3C 328 radio galaxy is a specimen of an FR II-like object with a hotspot in only one lobe. A conceivable reason for such asymmetry is that the nucleus of 3C 328 was temporarily inactive. There was no energy transfer from it to the lobes during the period of quiescence, and so they began to fade out. However, under the assumption that the axis connecting the two lobes makes an appreciable angle with the sky plane, and hence one is considerably farther from the observer than the other, the lobes are observed at two distinct stages of evolution due to the light-travel lag. While the far-side lobe is still perceived as being of the FR II type with a hotspot, decay of the near-side lobe is already apparent.

No jets are visible in the VLA images, but the VLBA observations of the inverted-spectrum core component of 3C 328 have revealed that it has a jet of a sub-arcsecond length pointing towards the lobe that shows evidence of decay. Since the jet always points to the near side, its observed orientation is in line with the scenario proposed here. The presence of the jet supports the inference that the nucleus of 3C 328 is currently active; however, given the fact that the jet is short ( 200 pc in projection), the activity must have restarted very recently.

gmw_00005018.name: HIPASS J1131--31
gmw_00005018.overview: HIPASS J1131--31 is a nearby dwarf irregular galaxy located at a distance of approximately 6.8 Mpc [arXiv:2212.03478]. It is an extremely metal-poor system with a tenuous red giant branch compared to its young stellar populations, suggesting that star formation in the galaxy may have begun only within the last few billion years [arXiv:2212.03478].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HIPASS J1131--31 as the nearby Peekaboo dwarf irregular, resolved by HST at 6.8 Mpc, extremely metal-poor, and possibly dominated by young stellar populations.

quotes.quote: The dwarf irregular galaxy HIPASS J1131--31 was discovered as a source of HI emission at low redshift in such close proximity of a bright star that we call it Peekaboo. The galaxy resolves into stars in images with Hubble Space Telescope, leading to a distance estimate of 6.8 0.7 Mpc. Spectral optical observations with the Southern African Large Telescope reveal HIPASS J1131--31 to be one of the most extremely metal-poor galaxies known with the gas-phase oxygen abundance 12+log(O/H) = 6.99 0.16 dex via the direct [ OIII ] 4363 line method and 6.87 0.07 dex from the two strong line empirical methods. The red giant branch of the system is tenuous compared with the prominence of the features of young populations in the color-magnitude diagram, inviting speculation that star formation in the galaxy only began in the last few Gyr.

HIPASS J1131--31 (PGC 5060432) is a nearby dwarf irregular galaxy with an \ systemic velocity of 716 , originally discovered in the \ Parkes All Sky Survey (HIPASS; Barnes2001 ) by B.S. Koribalski. No stellar counterpart could be identified at the time due to the bright foreground star TYC 7215-199-1 (10.4 mag) and its extensive diffraction pattern close to the fitted HIPASS position. Follow-up Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) 21-cm observations, carried out as part of the LVHIS project Koribalski2018 revealed an unresolved \ source, centered at , (J2000) = 11^ h 31^ m 34.6^ s , --31 40 28.3 . Koribalski2018 found this position to coincide with a compact blue dwarf galaxy in GALEX far-ultraviolet (FUV) images.

gmw_00005019.name: NGC 3424
gmw_00005019.overview: NGC 3424 is an edge-on galaxy [arXiv:1302.5011] that appears to be in the early stages of a merger with NGC 3430 [arXiv:1906.06720]. It exhibits variable gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1906.06720] and is classified as hosting an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1906.06720, arXiv:2007.07430].

sources.summary: The paper flags NGC 3424 as above the usual TS threshold but only 2.36 significant after background modeling, far off the FIR-gamma correlation and possibly a fluctuation or non-SFR source.

quotes.quote: In addition to the SFGs identified in the 3FGL, our analysis identifies the systems NGC 2403 and NGC 3424 to have ray emission with a statistical significance that exceeds the TS = 25 barrier typically used for source identification, with TS values of 37.5 and 30.9 respectively. However, by using P _ bg to determine the possibility that these systems are the result of background fluctuations, we find that the statistical significance of these systems is only 2.55 and 2.36 , respectively. Given that we analyze 584 SFGs, it is reasonable that two false detections would be found at these significances.

The systems NGC 3424 and NGC 2403 lie far off of the FIR to ray correlation, providing some evidence that these systems either stem from background fluctuations, or are ray bright for reasons unrelated to their star-formation rate. We note that Equation eq:theequation is resilient to the significant mismodeling of these systems. Since they lie far off of the FIR to ray correlation, no values of , , and are likely to provide a good fit. Thus, they have only a marginal influence on our best-fit parameters.

sources.summary: The paper argues that NGC 3424 has variable, over-luminous gamma-ray emission likely dominated by an obscured AGN, with supporting evidence from AGN classification and early merger status.

quotes.quote: Recently, gamma-ray excesses are reported to be spatially coincident with two starburst galaxies NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 in the fourth Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) source catalog (4FGL). Different from other starburst galaxies detected by Fermi LAT, we find that the gamma-ray emission associated with NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 show significant flux variability. With relatively weak infrared and radio emission, NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 appear as outliers of the L_ L_ IR and L_ L_ 1.4 \ GHz relations of starburst galaxies. These results suggest that NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 may harbor obscured active galactic nuclei (AGNs) and the AGN activities provide the dominant contribution to the gamma-ray emission as compared to that provided by the starburst activities.

Therefore we suggest NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 may harbor obscured AGNs, and attribute the gamma-ray emission associated with NGC 3424 and UGC 11041 to originate from AGN activity. For NGC 3424, this is supported by the fact that NGC 3424 is classified as a AGN based on the [ NII ] / H_ ratio combined with the strength of the H_ line 2013A&A...558A..68G . Interestingly, NGC 3424 appears to be in the early stage of merger with NGC 3430 2013ApJ...768...90L , which is capable of triggering not only AGN activity, but also star formation process.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3424 as an outlier: its SFR estimate disagrees with literature values and its extreme gamma-ray luminosity may indicate AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: The only exception is NGC 3424 , for which we find a 3.1 difference between our SFR and that published by 2013ApJ...768...90L , where is the combined error of both measurements. Our value is higher by 0.9 M _ yr ^ 1 , similar to the offset they find between their SED-based and FUV-based SFRs. These authors explain it as an effect of the different SFR time scales probed by their method and FUV-based techniques.

A clear trend is seen, from which only NGC 3424 deviates significantly. The extreme ray luminosity of this galaxy has already been noted by ajello2020 , and may be due to the presence of an AGN Gavazzi2011 , although this hypothesis has not been confirmed yet.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3424 as an edge-on galaxy whose central region brightens at longer wavelengths, with a steep UV slope, imperfect MAGPHYS fit, and UVOT/GALEX agreement.

quotes.quote: NGC 3424: This edge-on galaxy's central region becomes increasingly bright relative to its disk with increasing wavelength. Similarly to NGC 3190, the other nearly-edge on galaxy, its NUV-FUV slope is quite steep and not particularly well fit by MAGPHYS.
