We used this galaxy as a test case for analyzing UVOT photometry and found good agreement with the GALEX photometry. It lacks UBV photometry.

gmw_00005020.name: DRC-1
gmw_00005020.overview: DRC-1 is a massive, gas-rich galaxy that hosts a heavily obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2008.10613]. High-resolution imaging resolves the galaxy into three interacting, star-forming clumps [arXiv:1709.02809, arXiv:2008.10613] with a substantial molecular gas reservoir [arXiv:1709.02809, arXiv:2008.10613].

sources.summary: The paper identifies DRC-1 as the brightest dusty component, resolves it into three extreme star-forming clumps, and derives a molecular gas mass of about 1.1e11 solar masses from CO.

quotes.quote: Project 2013.A.00014.S consisted of high-spatial-resolution ( 0.12'' ) continuum observations of the brightest DRC component, what we will call DRC-1, at 870 m .

This is what happens for DRC-1, the brightest component of the proto-cluster. Our high-resolution ALMA imaging at 870 m reveals that this source is resolved into at least three bright star-forming clumps with extreme SFR densities of _ SFR 800 - 2,000 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 (Oteo et al., in prep.), which is among the highest known Riechers2014ApJ...796...84R,Oteo2017ApJ...837..182O,Iono2016ApJ...829L..10I,Riechers2017arXiv170509660R .

^ 12 CO(4--3) emission is detected in the moment-0 map only from DRC-1. Using this line flux to measure the molecular gas mass of this DRC component, assuming the average luminosity ratio of CO lines in DSFGs, L'_ CO(4-3) / L'_ CO(1-0) = 0.46 Ivison2011MNRAS.412.1913I,Bothwell2013MNRAS.429.3047B,Carilli2013ARA&A..51..105C and the _ CO for local ULIRGs, _ CO = 0.8 M _ ( K km s^ 1 pc^2 )^ 1 Downes1998ApJ...507..615D , the derived molecular gas mass for DRC-1 from ^ 12 CO(4--3) is M_ H_2 1.1 10^ 11 M _ . This value is lower than the molecular gas mass of DRC-1 derived from [ C i ] (1--0), but is consistent given the large uncertainties involved in such determinations.

sources.summary: The paper detects DRC-1 as a heavily obscured AGN host, one of the DRC's most massive gas-rich galaxies, resolved into interacting star-forming clumps.

quotes.quote: According to these values, which are in line with those estimated from the hardness ratio, DRC-1 hosts a heavily obscured, and possibly Compton-thick, AGN with moderate luminosity. DRC-1 is the confirmed protocluster member with the highest molecular gas mass ( M_ H_2 9 10^ 11 M_ ), and one of the most massive in terms of stellar mass M_ * 2 10^ 11 M_ ; Long20 . Moreover, it is resolved into three star-forming clumps by a high-resolution ( 0.12'' ) ALMA observation at 870 m see green contours in Fig. Fig_DRC1 Oteo18 and rest-frame UV HST observations Long20 . Both the availability of a large gas reservoir and possible galaxy interaction are thought to trigger luminous AGN activity e.g., Vito14b,Donley18,Weigel18 . SMGs, in particular, are both gas rich and often associated with galaxy mergers e.g., and references therein Alexander12, Narayanan15 , as in the case of DRC-1.

gmw_00005021.name: DRC-6
gmw_00005021.overview: DRC-6 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:2008.10613] that exhibits continuum emission from 5.5 to 28 GHz [arXiv:1709.02809]. Its flat radio spectrum and elevated far-infrared-to-radio flux ratio suggest the presence of a low-luminosity or obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2008.10613, arXiv:1709.02809].

sources.summary: The paper identifies DRC-6 as a radio galaxy in the DRC protocluster, not detected by Chandra, but likely hosting a low-luminosity or obscured AGN.

quotes.quote: Radio observations ( 5.5-28 GHz) identified DRC-6 as a radio galaxy, with a radio flux 50 brighter than that expected from star formation Oteo18 .

While all galaxies contribute at most 20 to the stacked X-ray flux, one of them, DRC-6, is a known radio galaxy (see intro and AGN_content ) that is expected to emit in the X-ray band, and in fact provides one of the major contributions (3 out of the 16 total counts in the source region). We repeated the stacking analysis excluding DRC-6, and found that the significance of the stacked emission decreases to (1-P_B)=0.984 (i.e., slightly below the detection threshold we set for the detection of individual sources in results ).
reported that DRC-6, which is not detected in our Chandra observations, is a radio galaxy (see intro ). Therefore DRC-6 likely hosts a low-luminosity and/or obscured AGN.

sources.summary: The paper detects DRC-6 in radio continuum from 28 to 5.5 GHz; its flat radio spectrum and elevated FIR-to-radio ratio suggest an active galactic nucleus.

quotes.quote: Our deep Jansky VLA and ATCA observations revealed continuum emission from DRC-6 (see Fig. figure_LABOCA_wide_map ) from 28 to 5.5 GHz ( S_ 28 GHz = 96 15 Jy , S_ 9.0 GHz = 120 8 Jy and S_ 5.5 GHz = 128 6 Jy ). For a DSFG, its radio spectrum is relatively flat e.g. Ibar2010MNRAS.401L..53I,Murphy2017ApJ...839...35M . Furthermore, the elevated FIR-to-radio flux density ratio in this component -- the radio emission is 50 brighter than would be predicted from the ALESS template Swinbank2014MNRAS.438.1267S used in several discussions in this paper -- suggests the presence of an AGN Guidetti2017MNRAS.471..210G .

gmw_00005022.name: B2 0838+32A
gmw_00005022.overview: B2 0838+32A is a restarting radio galaxy [arXiv:0809.2534, arXiv:0908.4391] associated with a dumbbell galaxy [arXiv:0908.4391] at the center of a nearby group [arXiv:0809.2534]. The host galaxy shows a moderate ultraviolet excess likely caused by a young stellar component [arXiv:1508.03287], but lacks evidence of a recent merger [arXiv:0809.2534].

sources.summary: The paper presents B2 0838+32A as a restarting radio galaxy in a foreground group whose young lobes drive a strong shock, while its host lacks evidence for a recent merger.

quotes.quote: We present Chandra and radio observations, and analysis of Sloan Digital Sky Survey data, of the radio galaxy B2 0838+32A (4C 32.26) and its environment. The radio galaxy is at the centre of a nearby group that has often been identified with the cluster Abell 695, but we argue that the original Abell cluster is likely to be an unrelated and considerably more distant system. The radio source is a restarting radio galaxy and, using our Chandra data, we argue that the currently active lobes are expanding supersonically, driving a shock with Mach number 2.4^ +1.0 _ 0.5 into the inter-stellar medium. This would be only the third strong shock round a young radio source to be discovered, after Centaurus A and NGC 3801. However, in contrast to both these systems, the host galaxy of B2 0838+32A shows no evidence for a recent merger, while the AGN spectrum shows no evidence for the dusty torus that would imply a large reservoir of cold gas close to the central black hole.

sources.summary: The paper associates 0838+32 with a dumbbell brightest galaxy, describing WAT and compact FR II structures, possible restarting activity, and unresolved VLBA emission.

quotes.quote: 0838+32 (4C 32.26) -- This radio source is associated with a dumbbell galaxy identified as the brightest galaxy in Abell 695, although recent works claim that it is at the centre of a group unrelated and much closer than the Abell cluster it was originally identified with ( ). At low resolution ( ) it appears to be a Wide Angle Tailed (WAT) source, but at high resolution it shows a compact FRII structure with core flux density S _ , 1.4 7.5 mJy ( ). The radio structure suggests a possible restarting activity and X-ray data suggest that the currently active lobes are expanding supersonically ( ). In our VLBA image, the source is unresolved with total flux density S_5 = 7.0 mJy, with little or no arcsecond core flux missing on parsec scales ( S_ VLBA /S_ 90 ).

sources.summary: The paper attributes B2 0838+32's moderate UV excess to a young stellar component and notes WISE data follow the old stellar model.

quotes.quote: B2 0838+32. The moderate UV excess can be very well explained by a young stellar component. Unfortunately, no optical spectra are available (to our knowledge). We note that the WISE data follow the old stellar population model quite well, up to and including the 22 m point.

gmw_00005023.name: J0804+4726
gmw_00005023.overview: J0804+4726 is an unresolved emission line galaxy [arXiv:2401.14981]. It is a strong Lyman continuum leaker that exhibits the lowest metallicity in its sample, which may explain its lack of detected magnesium II emission [arXiv:2401.14981].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0804+4726 as an unresolved strong LyC leaker with no Mg II detection, only an O II scale-length upper limit, uncertain escape fraction, and the sample’s lowest metallicity.

quotes.quote: Spatial extent of the Mg ii and O ii emission Of the 22 galaxies, two objects do not show any Mg ii radiation (J0130-0014 and J0804+4726) and one (J1503+3644) lacks [ O ii ] observations (Sect. sec:222 ). From our analysis of the PSF and detection limits (Appendix ap:1 and Sect. sec:33 ), we have identified four objects (J0130-0014, J0804+4726, J1154+2443, and J1256+4509) that have all of their measured scale lengths below their respective detection threshold. These objects are considered as unresolved in our study.

We also note that J0804+4726 is a strong leaker ( 60 ) with no Mg ii detection and only an upper limit on its [ O ii ] scale length. J0804+4726 has large uncertainties on its stellar continuum fit leading to a poorly constrained LyC escape fraction ( 0.58 0.40). This object is also our lowest metallicity source (12+log _ 10 (O/H)=7.5), which might explain its weak Mg ii emission.

gmw_00005024.name: J1033+6353
gmw_00005024.overview: J1033+6353 is a strong Lyman continuum emitting galaxy that exhibits extended magnesium II emission, indicating the presence of neutral gas in its circumgalactic medium [arXiv:2401.14981]. This galaxy also features extended oxygen II emission and a patchy interstellar medium configuration with a relatively low neutral hydrogen covering fraction [arXiv:2401.14981].

sources.summary: The paper says J1033+6353 differs from other strong LyC emitters, combining extended neutral and ionized gas, patchy covering, low O32, and likely ionized escape channels through the ISM and CGM.

quotes.quote: The J1033+6353 galaxy however behaves differently compared to the other strong LyC emitters which might indicate a different leakage mechanism. It is indeed the only strong leaker of the sample with 30 to show Mg ii emission, i.e., neutral gas, beyond its stellar continuum. Although Mg ii is only more extended than the continuum by a factor 1.5, this result suggests that the presence of neutral gas in the CGM is not incompatible with strong LyC leakage, which might thus escape anisotropically through ionized channels that go through both the ISM and CGM, as proposed in the literature Borthakur14, Trebitsch2017, Gazagnes2018, Wang19, Mauerhofer21, Gazagnes2020, Flury22b . This scenario is reinforced by the detection of extended [ O ii ] emission that traces the ionized gas.

Moreover, measured a relatively low H i covering fraction of C_ f ( H I ) 0.6 for J1033+6353 compared to the rest of the sample ( 0.4<C_ f ( H I )<1 ) which favors a patchy configuration of the ISM/CGM. Interestingly, J1033+6353 has a lower O _ 32 ratio ( 5) compared to the other strong leakers (>14). This suggests that LyC photons may escape J1033+6353 through ionized channels that cross through otherwise lower ionization gas e.g., Flury22b .

gmw_00005025.name: Yagi093
gmw_00005025.overview: Yagi093, also known as DF26, is an ultra-diffuse galaxy located in the Coma Cluster [arXiv:1801.09695, arXiv:1803.06298]. It is characterized by an unusually large size and shows some indications of tidal disruption [arXiv:1801.09695].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Yagi093, alias DF26, as a Coma UDG with disruption hints and unusually large size, suggesting similarity to outer rather than central Coma UDGs.

quotes.quote: Two of them are found in common with the vanDokkum2015a Dragonfly catalog: Yagi093=DF26 and Yagi276=DF28. Interestingly, only one of the seven UDGs (Yagi093) shows some indications of being disrupted, which is in agreement with the general lack of tidal features in UDGs in the central parts of Coma (e.g. Mowla2017 , and see similar results for the Perseus cluster; Wittmann2017 ). This is reinforced by the fact that Yagi093 is the largest and one of the furthest UDGs in our sample.

One of our UDGS, Yagi093 presents somewhat larger sizes to the rest of our Coma UDGs, being the only one with some hints of tidal features. It could therefore be more similar to these outer Coma UDGs than to the more central ones in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper includes DF26 as a Coma UDG with prior structural and stellar-content work, younger age and lower metallicity than earlier estimates, and a rotation upper limit.

quotes.quote: Three of these systems (DF26, Yagi 090, and Yagi 418) were previously identified as UDGs and their structural parameters determined 2015ApJ...798L..45V, 2016ApJS..225...11Y . In addition, the stellar content of DF26 and Yagi 418 have also been analysed previously in 2018arXiv180109695F , allowing for an interesting comparison.

In particular, we seem to find younger average ages (DF26: 6.8 1.3 Gyr as compared to 7.88 1.76 Gyr; Yagi 814: 8.1 0.8 Gyr compared to 9.69 2.02 Gyr), and slightly lower metallicities (DF26: --0.78 0.08 as compared to --0.72 0.18; Yagi 814: --1.25 0.05 compared to --1.10 0.95).
The values of V_ Max that we recovered for DF26, Yagi 090, Yagi 418, OGS1, and OGS2 are 66.5, 124.4, 80.5, 50.3, and 45.9 km/s, respectively.

gmw_00005026.name: Yagi398
gmw_00005026.overview: Yagi398 is classified as an ultra-diffuse galaxy, although its size of 1.3 kiloparsecs places it on the margin of some size criteria [arXiv:1801.09695]. It is characterized by low metallicity and is among the oldest ultra-diffuse galaxies in its sample [arXiv:1801.09695].

sources.summary: The paper keeps Yagi398 in the UDG class despite its marginal size and notes its low metallicity and old age make possible age-systematic effects mild.

quotes.quote: Yagi398 is on the limit of the UDG size criteria defined by vanDokkum2015a (with R_e =1.3 kpc) but it is within the limits from Yagi2016 and those in the theoretical simulations. Therefore we keep this galaxy in the UDG class, although we will check if our results in Section 4 are affected by such assumption.

Only two of our UDGs could potentially be affected, as they have metallicities lower than or similar to --1 dex (Yagi398 and Yagi418). However, they are already the oldest UDGs in our sample, and thus such effect would be milder. At most, the ages for these two UDGs would be considered lower limits.

gmw_00005027.name: Yagi418
gmw_00005027.overview: Yagi418 is a ultra-diffuse galaxy located within the Coma cluster [arXiv:1803.06298, arXiv:2405.09104]. It hosts an intermediate-to-old stellar population [arXiv:2405.09104] characterized by low metallicity [arXiv:1801.09695, arXiv:1803.06298] and an extended star-formation history [arXiv:1803.06298].

sources.summary: The paper compares Yagi418 with RL+18, finding old age, low metallicity, positive Mg/Fe, matching formation times, and a possible age lower limit from low metallicity.

quotes.quote: For Yagi418, we find t = 7.9 2.0 Gyr, [ Z/H ] = 1.10 0.95 and [ Mg/Fe ] = 0.27 0.52 as opposed to their t = 8.1 1.1 Gyr, [ Z/H ] = 1.25 0.05 and [ Mg/Fe ] = 0.60, showing a fairly good agreement. They also show similar formation ages t _ 50 and t _ 90 , with 11 vs 12 Gyr and 4.0 vs 6 Gyr for Yagi093, and 10 vs 10 Gyr and 4 vs 4 Gyr for Yagi418, respectively).

Only two of our UDGs could potentially be affected, as they have metallicities lower than or similar to --1 dex (Yagi398 and Yagi418). However, they are already the oldest UDGs in our sample, and thus such effect would be milder. At most, the ages for these two UDGs would be considered lower limits.

sources.summary: The paper includes Yagi 418 as a previously identified UDG with prior stellar-content analysis, an extended star-formation history, and younger/lower-metallicity estimates than earlier work.

quotes.quote: Three of these systems (DF26, Yagi 090, and Yagi 418) were previously identified as UDGs and their structural parameters determined 2015ApJ...798L..45V, 2016ApJS..225...11Y . In addition, the stellar content of DF26 and Yagi 418 have also been analysed previously in 2018arXiv180109695F , allowing for an interesting comparison.

For instance, while DF26, OGS1 and OGS2 best examplify the described behaviour, Yagi 418 and Yagi 090 display more extended SFHs as well as an apparent secondary peak in the mass fraction at ages 8-9 Gyr, probably a consequence of a delayed formation.

In particular, we seem to find younger average ages (DF26: 6.8 1.3 Gyr as compared to 7.88 1.76 Gyr; Yagi 814: 8.1 0.8 Gyr compared to 9.69 2.02 Gyr), and slightly lower metallicities (DF26: --0.78 0.08 as compared to --0.72 0.18; Yagi 814: --1.25 0.05 compared to --1.10 0.95).

sources.summary: Yagi 418 is a Coma UDG whose V magnitude is converted from R band, with preferred mass-weighted age and metallicity values that agree with prior light-weighted ages.

quotes.quote: Yagi 418 Yagi 418 is a Coma cluster galaxy. The M_V was calculated from R band using V = R + 0.5 (based on Virgo dEs and Coma LSBs; vanZee2004, Alabi2020 ). Stellar population properties for this galaxy are presented in but here we prefer the age/metallicity values due to their being mass-weighted in contrast to the RuizLara2018 light-weighted values. We note that the ages are in good agreement between the two studies, as is expected for such intermediate-to-old stellar populations.

gmw_00005028.name: And VI
gmw_00005028.overview: Andromeda VI is a luminous classical dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0410489, arXiv:1103.2121] located within the M31 subgroup [arXiv:astro-ph/0410489]. It exhibits typical kinematics for its size [arXiv:1103.2121] and shows little to no evidence of young or intermediate stellar populations [arXiv:astro-ph/0410489].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Andromeda VI as a recent M31 subgroup dwarf spheroidal and derives its TRGB distance from central RGB photometry.

quotes.quote: Andromeda V, Andromeda VI (Pegasus dSph) and Andromeda VII (Cassiopeia dSph) Andromeda V ( 1 h  10 m  17.1 s , +47^  37'  41'' ), VI ( 23 h  51 m  46.3 s , +24^  34'  57'' ), VII ( 23 h  26 m  31 s , +50^  41'  31'' ) were, prior to the discovery of And IX, the three newest additions to the M31 subgroup. And V and VI were discovered by after they had performed an analysis on the disgitised version of the second Palomar Sky Survey (POSS-II; reid1991,reid1993,lasker1993 ). At the same time, independently discovered And V (which they named the Pegasus dSph) as well as yet another companion, the Cassiopeia dSph (also named And  VII) from their analysis of the POSS-II plates. All of these satellites appear to resemble typical dwarf spheroidals, with little or no evidence for young or intermediate stellar populations ( armandroff1998,armandroff1999,grebel1999a,hopp1999,davidge2002 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents And VI as a luminous M31 classical dwarf spheroidal, re-derives its structural scale radii from Subaru data, and measures typical Keck velocity dispersion for its size.

quotes.quote: And VI Structural properties with Subaru Suprime-Cam And VI is one of the more luminous of the M31 classical dSphs, with M_V=-11.5 mcconnachie06b , and it too was discovered using the Second Palomar Sky Survey armandroff99 .
Using the mcconnachie05a distance for And VI of 783 kpc (distance modulus of 24.47) we obtain physical scale radii of r_ half =440 16 pc and r_t=1.6 0.2 .

In terms of their kinematics, we measured systemic velocities of v_r=-393.1 4.2 and 344.8 2.5 , and dispersions of _v=11.5^ +5.3 _ 4.4 and _v=9.4^ +3.2 _ 2.4 for And V and And VI respectively, meaning that these two dSph appear very typical for objects of their size when compared with MW dSphs.

gmw_00005029.name: SDSS J021740.96--085447.9
gmw_00005029.overview: SDSS J021740.96--085447.9 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1808.03140]. Its broad-absorption line troughs contain at least seven narrow absorption line systems, which exhibit line-locking signatures that suggest a clumpy outflow accelerated by radiative line-driving [arXiv:1808.03140].

sources.summary: The paper identifies line-locked narrow absorption systems inside the BAL troughs of quasar SDSS J021740.96--085447.9, arguing for clumpy, radiatively driven outflows viewed nearly along the wind streamlines.

quotes.quote: Utilizing the two-epoch spectroscopic observations of J0217--0854 from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, we find that each of its C IV , Si IV , N V , and Ly BAL troughs actually contain at least seven NAL systems.

In this paper, we report the discovery of the line-locking signatures of NALs within BALs in quasar SDSS J021740.96--085447.9 (hereafter J0217--0854) as a warm-up of our systematic study program on Type N BALs (Lu, W.-J. et al., in preparation).

First, the phenomenon of multiple line-locked NALs within a BAL trough strongly favors the ideas that the BAL outflow (at least in J0217--0854) is made up of several physically separated clumpy structures with similar locations, kinematics, and physical conditions and that the radiative line-driving plays an important role in the acceleration of these clumpy clouds. The line-locking signatures in J0217--0854 also indicate that our line of sight is approximately parallel to the wind streamlines (e.g., Hamann2011 ).

gmw_00005030.name: PGC 047177
gmw_00005030.overview: PGC 047177 is an early-type galaxy that exhibits aligned gas and stellar rotation axes [arXiv:2012.07882]. Despite this alignment, the galaxy displays peculiar gas distribution and kinematics, featuring a compact second ionized-gas component with a high velocity dispersion [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper reports PGC 047177 has two ionized-gas components, a compact high-dispersion second component, and peculiar gas distribution and kinematics despite aligned gas and stellar rotation axes.

quotes.quote: PGC 047177 The ionized-gas emission lines were fitted with two components (see Fig. fig:lines ) while \ with only one Gaussian component (no \ was detected). In this case, the broader Gaussian component is not actually red/blueshifted (see Fig fig:047177_2 in the Appendix A), so we will refer to it as ``second'' component.

The second component of PGC 047177 (Fig. fig:047177_2 ) is more confined with respect to the first one, it is characterized by a large velocity dispersion ( 400 \ for ) and a slightly negative velocity going outwards.

Peculiar gas distributions and kinematics PGC 047177 and PGC 047273 present peculiar features in the gas distribution and kinematics so that it is more difficult to speculate on the origin of their gas. In PGC 047177, PA _ g and PA _ s are aligned with respect to each other and their rotation axis are aligned to the isophotal major axis.

gmw_00005031.name: PGC 047273
gmw_00005031.overview: PGC 047273 is an early-type galaxy characterized by an oblate-like regular stellar rotation [arXiv:2012.07882]. It hosts a large amount of ionized gas in a strongly misaligned polar disk that is visibly precessing and bending toward the galaxy's major axis [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PGC 047273 as a massive galaxy with a strongly misaligned polar ionized-gas disk, likely externally acquired and visibly precessing toward the galaxy plane.

quotes.quote: In PGC 047273, the gas appears to be distributed in a strongly tilted polar disk settled close to the minor axis.

PGC 047273 is characterized by an oblate-like regular rotation but presents a large amount of ionized-gas with a rotation axis that is strongly misaligned with respect to the stellar one. The presence of misalignment between stars and gas points to an external origin of the gas. After the accretion, the gas settled in a sort of polar disk which is, however, not exactly along the galaxy minor axis.

Fig. fig:047273 shows all the detected emission-lines for this galaxy, some with more extended coverage of the gas distribution. From these lines it can be noticed that in the outskirts the disk is visibly ``precessing'' and bending towards the major axis of the galaxy. This suggests that the galaxy might be oblate axisymmetric and the accreted gas disk is in the process of setting in the principal plane of the galaxy.

gmw_00005032.name: PGC 046860
gmw_00005032.overview: PGC 046860 is an early-type galaxy that exhibits centrally concentrated gas emission [arXiv:2012.07882]. This emission is restricted to a central line detection, showing no regular gas rotation and a central peak in gas velocity dispersion [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper finds PGC 046860 has only a centrally concentrated detected emission line, no regular gas rotation, and a central gas velocity-dispersion peak among compact gas systems.

quotes.quote: Overall, the strongest and most spatially extended emission is the one coming from \ lines (see Fig. fig:BCGsmaps and fig:SATsmaps ), that are indeed the only ones detected in PGC 046860, PGC 047197 and PGC 097958.
The gas is centrally concentrated in most of satellites (PGC 046860, PGC 047177, PGC 047197, PGC 047590 and PGC 097958).

In satellites, the gas is clearly rotating in PGC 047590, it seems to be in rotation in PGC 097958 and PGC 047197, while it does not show any rotation in PGC 046860 . These 4 galaxies present a central peak in gas velocity dispersion ( 300 ).

gmw_00005033.name: PGC 047197
gmw_00005033.overview: PGC 047197 is an early-type galaxy that exhibits only central emission lines [arXiv:2012.07882]. The galaxy hosts a small nuclear gas disk that is nearly aligned with its stellar rotation, which may have originated from internal stellar gas loss or a merger [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper describes PGC 047197 as having only a detected central emission line, a small nuclear gas disk nearly aligned with stellar rotation, and possible internal gas loss or merger origin.

quotes.quote: Overall, the strongest and most spatially extended emission is the one coming from \ lines (see Fig. fig:BCGsmaps and fig:SATsmaps ), that are indeed the only ones detected in PGC 046860, PGC 047197 and PGC 097958.

From a visual inspection, we inferred that in PGC 047197 and PGC 097958 the gas is rotating in a small (1 \ of diameter) nuclear disk and that PA _ g is nearly aligned to PA _ s . As a consequence, we added these two galaxies to the subsample of objects with no kinematic misalignments. In PGC 047197, we are considering the PA _ s measured within 4 \ from the center, since the kinematics is quite complex outwards (see Fig. fig:SATsmaps ).

This small amount of gas contained in objects with oblate-like regular rotation (PGC 046860, PGC 047197 and PGC 049940) may originate from internal stellar gas loss, even if simulations showed that it could also come from mergers Voort2015 . In particular, this could be the case of PGC 047197 which shows prolate-like rotation krajnovic2018 .

gmw_00005034.name: PGC 047202
gmw_00005034.overview: PGC 047202 is a brightest cluster galaxy that contains centrally concentrated ionized gas [arXiv:2012.07882]. This ionized gas component does not exhibit rotation or a regular rotation pattern [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper describes PGC 047202 as a BCG with centrally concentrated ionized gas, a non-rotating first gas component, and no regular gas pattern from which to define a position angle.

quotes.quote: The ionised-gas of the first component is centrally concentrated in three of the BCGs (PGC 047202, PGC 049940 and PGC 065588) while extended with outward filaments in the remaining two (PGC 015524 and PGC 073000).
Concerning the velocity field of the BCGs, the gas of the first component is regularly rotating in PGC 049940 and PGC 065588 while not showing rotation in PGC 047202.
The gas in PGC 046860 and PGC 047202 does not exhibit a regular rotation pattern, thus it is not possible to define a PA _ g .

gmw_00005035.name: PGC 097958
gmw_00005035.overview: PGC 097958 is an early-type satellite galaxy that exhibits a detected central emission line [arXiv:2012.07882]. Visual inspection indicates that its gas rotates within a small nuclear disk that is nearly aligned with the stellar rotation [arXiv:2012.07882].

sources.summary: The paper reports PGC 097958 has only a detected central emission line and a visually inferred small nuclear gas disk nearly aligned with the stellar rotation.

quotes.quote: Overall, the strongest and most spatially extended emission is the one coming from \ lines (see Fig. fig:BCGsmaps and fig:SATsmaps ), that are indeed the only ones detected in PGC 046860, PGC 047197 and PGC 097958.
In satellites, the gas is clearly rotating in PGC 047590, it seems to be in rotation in PGC 097958 and PGC 047197, while it does not show any rotation in PGC 046860 .

This analysis was not successful for PGC 046860, PGC 047197 and PGC 097958 since the spatial extent of the gas is limited to a few central bins. From a visual inspection, we inferred that in PGC 047197 and PGC 097958 the gas is rotating in a small (1 \ of diameter) nuclear disk and that PA _ g is nearly aligned to PA _ s .

gmw_00005036.name: GRB 080607
gmw_00005036.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 080607 is a massive, optically faint galaxy at redshift z = 3.036 [arXiv:1009.0004, arXiv:1507.03909]. It features a highly extinguished, dust-rich interstellar medium [arXiv:1010.1002] containing strong neutral hydrogen, molecular hydrogen, and carbon monoxide absorption with near-solar metallicity [arXiv:0901.0556, arXiv:1301.4908].

sources.summary: The paper reports discovery and SED analysis of the dusty GRB 080607 host; revised IRAC photometry lowers its inferred mass and SFR, with dust extinction driving uncertainties.

quotes.quote: Here we report the discovery of the host galaxy of GRB 080607 in an extensive imaging follow-up campaign.

The host galaxy is clearly resolved in deep HST WF3/IR F160W images and well detected in the Spitzer IRAC 3.5 m and 4.5 m channels, while displaying little/no fluxes in deep optical images from Keck and Magellan. The extremely red optical--infrared colors are consistent with the large extinction seen in the afterglow light, suggesting that the large amount of dust and gas surface mass density seen along the afterglow sightline is not merely local but likely reflects the global dust content across the entire host galaxy.

The likelihood analysis described in Equation (1) of Chen \ (2010) yields an extinction-corrected SFR of (8-12) \ h^ 2 M _ yr ^ 1 , a mean ISM radiation field I_0 (2.3-3.5) 10^ 5 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 2 Hz ^ 1 , and M_*=(0.5-1.4) 10^ 10 \ h^ 2 M _ for the host galaxy. These are about an order of magnitude lower than those originally published. We note that the uncertainties in the derived M_* , I_0 and SFR are driven by the uncertainties in the global dust extinction law of the host galaxy. The galaxy is still fairly massive, but not as extreme as previously thought. The result indicates a large spatial variation in the dust content across the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper reports strong H2 and CO absorption in the GRB 080607 host, finding high column density, roughly solar metallicity, Milky-Way-like molecular-cloud properties, and a lower integrated dust-to-metals ratio.

quotes.quote: We report on strong H _2 and CO absorption from gas within the host galaxy of gamma-ray burst (GRB) 080607. Analysis of our Keck/LRIS afterglow spectrum reveals a very large H 1 column density ( = 10^ 22.70 0.15 2 ) and strong metal-line absorption at z_ GRB = 3.0363 with a roughly solar metallicity. We detect a series of A-X bandheads from CO and estimate CO = 10^ 16.5 0.3 2 and T_ ex ^ CO > 100  K. We argue that the high excitation temperature results from UV pumping of the CO gas by the GRB afterglow. Similarly, we observe H _2 absorption via the Lyman-Werner bands and estimate = 10^ 21.2 0.2 2 with T_ ex ^ H_2 = 10 -300 K.

Remarkably, this molecular gas has extinction properties, metallicity, and a CO/H _2 ratio comparable to those of translucent molecular clouds of the Milky Way, suggesting that star formation at high z proceeds in similar environments as today. However, the integrated dust-to-metals ratio is sub-Galactic, suggesting the dust is primarily associated with the molecular phase while the atomic gas has a much lower dust-to-gas ratio.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 080607 host as a dusty, high-redshift galaxy whose sightline contains a molecular cloud, strong extinction, HST/Spitzer detections, and extreme optical faintness.

quotes.quote: This event is remarkable not only for its intrinsic properties, but also because of its unusual environment: a Keck spectrum obtained starting only 20 min after the burst Prochaska+2009 reveals that the sightline penetrates a giant molecular cloud in the host galaxy, obscuring the rest-frame visible light by A_V 3 mag of extinction (or 6 mag at 1600 , corresponding to the observed R band) before it even began its journey through intergalactic space.

In this paper, we analyze several other aspects of this burst, from the prompt emission (and simultaneous optical detection) through a late-time search for the host galaxy, and we present a significantly expanded discussion of its extinction properties. In sec:obs we describe our early-time multicolor observations of the afterglow with several different robotic telescopes. We analyze the optical light curve in sec:lc - sec:earlylc and show no correlation between the prompt emission behavior and the early optical observations, starting at only 6 s post-trigger in the host frame, and we present limits on color variations at early times. In sec:sed - sec:extbeta we examine in more detail the combined photometric and spectroscopic spectral energy distribution (SED) and place our final constraints on the host-galaxy extinction properties, demonstrating the firm detection of a 2175 \ bump, the highest-redshift detection of this signature to date.

None of these integrations resulted in a secure detection of the host galaxy, with the possible exception of the g band, in which a faint source is detected at about 3 above background at the afterglow position ( g = 27.4 0.3 mag). A log of our ground-based host observations is reported in Table tab:host . lllll 0pc Host-Galaxy Limits Instrument & Obs. date & Exp. time & Filter & 5 limit & (UT) & (s) & & (mag) Keck I / LRIS & 2009-02-19 & 2490 & g & > 27.3 Keck I / LRIS & 2009-02-19 & 2220 & I & > 25.3 Keck I / NIRC & 2009-05-31 & 3600 & K_s & > 21.6 5 limiting magnitudes on a host galaxy at the afterglow position from our ground-based optical and IR observations at the Keck Observatory. The host galaxy is, however, well detected at 1.6 m in a deep Hubble Space Telescope (HST) image using WF3, as well as in both of the warm Spitzer IRAC channels (3.6 m and 4.5 m). The extreme optical faintness of this system, while partially due simply to its high redshift ( z=3.036 ), makes this galaxy of particular interest: determination of the redshift would be exceptionally difficult using traditional field-survey techniques, illustrating the unique ability of GRBs to select and study optically faint galaxies at high redshift.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 080607 host galaxy at z = 3.0363 as containing star-forming molecular gas with H2 and CO absorption, plus atomic gas and shielded molecular material near the burst sight line.

quotes.quote: Recently, hereafter P09 Proc09 presented the first unambiguous spectroscopic detection of absorption bands from H _2 and CO molecules along a translucent GRB sight line (GRB 080607, z = 3.0363). Their initial analysis provided the first discovery of molecular gas with properties very similar to those of Galactic molecular clouds, but located in the star-forming ISM of a GRB host galaxy. The detection by P09 of neutral species (e.g., C 1 , Mg 1 ) toward GRB 080607 indicates that the bulk of the atomic gas was 100 pc from the GRB, presumably beyond the photoionization sphere formed by the progenitor Whal08 . However, whereas Whal08 introduced an additional galactic-wide FUV field as means to suppress the formation of unobserved H _2 , the discovery of more than 10 ^ 21 cm ^ 2 of H _2 toward GRB 080607, well shielded by dust extinction (rest-frame A_V = 3.2 mag), raises the possibility that the molecular material could have been in close proximity to the GRB. The detection by P09 of absorption from rotationally-excited CO, and the determination that atomic and molecular lines are kinematically distinguished by 30 15 km s ^ 1 , specifically allow for this scenario.

sources.summary: The paper uses the GRB 080607 host as an abnormal dusty high-redshift GRB environment, noting extreme neutral and molecular gas, a strong UV bump, and high stellar mass.

quotes.quote: Remarkable is the relatively high fraction of molecular hydrogen f_ H_2 = 2N( H_2 )/ [ N( H I )+2N( H_2 ) ] 1.14 found in GRB 080607 at z=3.0363 with the extreme column density of neutral hydrogen N_ HI = 10^ 22.7 (Prochaska et al. 2009).

For instance, Prochaska et al (2009) detected it in GRB 080607 at z=3.036 , from which A_V = 3.2 was derived. In the same spectrum, a large HI column density and molecular absorption is measured as well (see Section s4 ). The host of this dark GRB was widely investigated from the UV to the infrared, and a relatively high stellar mass is derived, M_ 4 10^ 10 M _ (Perley et al. 2013). This is another host which, according to the canonical view, can be considered 'abnormal'.

sources.summary: The paper treats the GRB 080607 host as a high-redshift H2-absorbing galaxy with solar-like absorption metallicity, detected stellar emission, CO non-detection, and globally molecule-deficient or CO-dark gas.

quotes.quote: These are: GRB 080607 Prochaska09 , GRB 120815A Kruhler13 , and GRB 181020A Heintz19 . All show H _2 column densities above N (H_2) > 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 and also constitute the GRB absorbers with the largest molecular-hydrogen fractions, f_ H_2 Bolmer19,Heintz19 . GRB 080607 also shows a high absorption-derived gas-phase metallicity consistent with Solar ( [ X/H ] > -0.2 ) and significant dust extinction A_V 3 mag.
The host-galaxy emission counterpart of GRB 080607 is well detected in several bands Chen10,Wang12 .

While no strong conclusion can be inferred for the molecular-gas content of the hosts of GRBs 120815A and 181020A, we demonstrate that the host galaxy of GRB 080607 is globally deficient in molecules or ``CO-dark''.

sources.summary: The host of GRB 080607 is described as a very red, faint galaxy fit by an extinguished template, with published physical parameters and star formation estimates.

quotes.quote: The host of GRB 080607 The afterglow of GRB 080607 is heavily reddened ( A_V 3 mag ), has a modest 2175 dust feature and is characterized by a strong neutral hydrogen absorber with roughly solar metallicity and molecular gas 2009ApJ...691L..27P, 2010arXiv1009.0004P . Data from 2011ApJ...727L..53C show a R 27 mag , very red host with a synthetic color of (R-K)_ AB 3 mag (Fig. hostfigs ). The host is well described with an extinguished galaxy template ( A'_V 1 - 2 mag ), and physical parameters of M_B=-21.1 0.3 mag , which is 0.3 L^* at z 3 , (M_ ) = 9.9^ +0.4 _ 0.6 , an extinction corrected SFR of 40_ 26 ^ +60 /yr, and SSFR of 8 Gyr ^ 1 . These values are in good agreement with previously published ones 2011ApJ...727L..53C .

sources.summary: The chapter presents the GRB 080607 host as a z=3.036 system with very high H I, near-solar metallicity, dust, H2 and CO absorption, and no host detection by June 2010.

quotes.quote: Two interesting examples of what GRB afterglow spectroscopy can teach us about GRB hosts are the cases of GRB 030323 at redshift z=3.372 (Vreeswijk et al. \ 2004) and GRB 080607 at z=3.036 (Prochaska et al. \ 2009).

The optical spectrum of GRB 080607 displays an even larger HI column density of (N_ HI / cm ^ 2 ) = 22.7 , but contrary to GRB 030323, the GRB 080607 host is responsible for a very metal rich absorption system (close to solar metallicity) with significant dust extinction and a clear detection of both H _2 and CO molecular absorption (Fig. fig:080607 ). At the time of writing (June 2010) the host galaxy has not been detected.

gmw_00005037.name: SDSS J090110.10+623719.6
gmw_00005037.overview: SDSS J090110.10+623719.6 is a bright elliptical galaxy that serves as a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2110.03153]. It hosts a wide-angle tail radio galaxy with bent jets and a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2110.03153].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J090110.10+623719.6 as the bright elliptical BCG hosting a WAT radio galaxy, with bent jets, a massive black hole, merger-driven motion, and antique filaments.

quotes.quote: The optical counterpart of the source S1 has been identified as the bright elliptical galaxy SDSS J090110.10+623719.6 ( z= 0.09).

Black hole mass and accretion state The black hole mass of the host galaxy of the BCG is computed using the M _ BH - relation where reprsents the effective stellar velocity dispersion Gultekin . The value of of the central host galaxy is 342 6.5 km s ^ 1 which translates into a black hole mass of (1.4 0.4) 10 ^ 9 M _ .

The WAT radio galaxy is hosted by the BCG (SDSS J090110.10+623719.6), based on our images at 612 MHz and 3 GHz, using the GMRT and VLASS radio data, respectively. The East-West orientation of the WAT radio galaxy suggests a peculiar motion of the BCG in line with the merger scenario. The BCG that hosts the WAT appears to be significantly displaced, offset by 2 arcmin (195 kpc), as projected in the sky plane, from the X-ray centroid of the cluster, indicating that the cluster is at an early stage of the merger.

gmw_00005038.name: host galaxy of GRB 180325A
gmw_00005038.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 180325A is a high-redshift, star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1806.00293]. It has an estimated stellar mass of approximately 10^9.3 solar masses and a dust-corrected star formation rate of 46 solar masses per year [arXiv:1806.00293].

sources.summary: The paper reports emission lines from the host galaxy of GRB 180325A, deriving a dust-corrected SFR of 46 solar masses per year and stellar mass near 10^9.3 solar masses.

quotes.quote: In the VLT/X-shooter NIR spectrum, we clearly detect H and [ O iii ] emission lines from the GRB host galaxy. We measure line fluxes of F_ H =(5.0 1.2) 10^ 17 erg s ^ 2 cm ^ 1 ^ 1 and F_ [ O iii ] =(7.7 0.9) 10^ 17 erg s ^ 2 cm ^ 1 ^ 1 for these bright emission lines. The H line flux corresponds to a star-formation rate (SFR) of 15.8 3.8 M _ yr ^ 1 , based on the calibration from kennicutt98 , and without correcting for dust extinction. Correcting for SED-derived dust extinction ( A_V 1.5 ), the host galaxy SFR is 46 4 M _ yr ^ 1 . We measure a Full-Width-Half-Maximum (FWHM) of 106 28 km s ^ 1 for the H line.

Using the correlation between stellar mass and velocity width of the H line derived for GRB host galaxies in arabsalmani18 , we estimate a stellar mass of M_ /M_ 10^ 9.3 0.4 for the host galaxy of GRB 180325A. The estimated stellar mass and SFR place the host galaxy amongst the main-sequence star-forming galaxies with similar redshifts, though towards higher SFRs on the envelope of the main-sequence SFR- M_ relation see Fig. 1 of rodighiero11 .

gmw_00005039.name: SDSS J153350.89+272729
gmw_00005039.overview: SDSS J153350.89+272729 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.03243 [arXiv:2102.05795]. It exhibits weak Type II Seyfert activity in its nucleus and has a stellar mass of approximately 10^10.5 solar masses [arXiv:2102.05795].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J153350.89+272729 as J1533+2727's host, finding the radio transient nuclear, the galaxy at z=0.03243, weak Type II Seyfert activity, spiral morphology, and stellar mass near 10^10.5 solar masses.

quotes.quote: The centroid of the FIRST position of J1533+2727 is located 0.150 from the optical center of the galaxy SDSS J153350.89+272729. With a FIRST positional uncertainty of 0.400 , J1533+2727 is therefore consistent with being coincident with the galaxy nucleus. Figure fig:host shows the SDSS DR16 sdss16 image and spectrum of this galaxy, which we adopt as the host of J1533+2727. The host galaxy lies at a redshift of z=0.03243 0.00001 (luminosity distance of 147.14 0.01 Mpc), and the difference between the radio position of J1533+2727 and the center of light of the host galaxy corresponds to a projected separation of just 107 pc.

An inspection of the SDSS optical spectrum indicates weak Type II Seyfert activity, according to standard line-ratio diagnostics kewley , with ( [ NII ] /H ) =-0.05 and ( [ OIII ] /H ) =0.78 . Stellar population synthesis fits to the SDSS photometry indicate a stellar mass of between 10^ 10.45 M_ and 10^ 10.49 M_ , and an ongoing star-formation rate of 0.6 M_ yr ^ 1 `stellarMassFSPSGranEarlyDust' table; sdssspec . The quoted uncertainties are purely statistical, and do not reflect the range of possible parameterizations. The galaxy was classified morphologically as a spiral by class_SPIRAL .

gmw_00005040.overview: The active galactic nucleus 0607 157 has been observed across multiple radio frequencies [arXiv:astro-ph/0511838]. It exhibits circular polarization in its radio core, with detections recorded at 4.8, 5, 8, and 15 GHz [arXiv:astro-ph/0511838].

sources.summary: The paper places a 15 GHz core limit on 0607 157 and compares it with earlier 8, 5, and 4.8 GHz circular-polarization detections.

quotes.quote: 0607 157: We find a limit of |m_c| < 0.21 in the core at 15 GHz in March of 2003. HW03 measured only m_c = -0.23 0.12 in the core at 15 GHz in January of 1998, but they simultaneously detected m_c = -0.65 0.07 in the core at 8 GHz. HAW01 detected circular polarization in core of m_c = -0.18 0.05 at 5 GHz thirteen months earlier in December of 1996. AAP03 found strong circular polarization, m_c = -0.23 0.04 at 4.8 GHz in integrated measurements with the UMRAO during the period 2001--2002.

gmw_00005041.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2134 + 004 is a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1804.03703]. It is tentatively classified as a compact core-jet radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0108185] that exhibits strong circular polarization [arXiv:astro-ph/0511838] and variable radio flux density [arXiv:astro-ph/0108185].

sources.summary: The paper tentatively classifies 2134+004 as a compact core-jet radio source with a double VLBI structure, variable flux density, component polarization, and complex magnetic field.

quotes.quote: 2134+004 : the VLBI image at 15 GHz has a double structure just a few resolution elements across the source. The least-squared fit on the components suggests that the eastern component is more compact, in agreement with the MEM image, which also suggests another weak middle component. Due to the strong variability of radio flux density for this object (see references in Stanghellini et al 1998) we tentatively classify this source as a core-jet source with the core being the more compact eastern component. The VLBA image at 8.4 GHz shown by Fey et al. (1996) is consistent with our view.

Polarization emission is detected in both components at a level of 2-3 , while the total fractional polarization at 8.4 GHz is 0.5 . The B field has a rather complicated structure and indicates that beam depolarization may still play a role even at this frequency/resolution.

sources.summary: The paper maps 2134 + 004 at 15 GHz, detecting strong circular polarization in its jet core and a similar nearby component, with positive integrated detections historically.

quotes.quote: 2134 + 004: Figure f:2134cp shows our observations of 2134 + 004 at 15 GHz in May of 2002. The top panel is Stokes I, the middle panel is linear polarization, and the bottom panel is circular polarization. Positions where circular polarization measurements or limits were taken are marked and numbered on the Stokes I map. We detect strong circular polarization of m_c=+0.35 0.12 on the jet core, we measure a similar value at location number 1 of m_c=+0.29 0.12 , and we find a limit on the CP at location 2 to be |m_c| < 0.34 . In recent integrated measurements from 2001--2002, AAP03 found strong circular polarization at 4.8 GHz with m_c = +0.62 0.06 .

sources.summary: The paper classifies 5BZQJ 2136+0041 as a high-redshift FSRQ with a short-lived, low-significance gamma-ray flare whose TS map matches the source position.

quotes.quote: 5BZQJ 2136+0041. This source is classified as a FSRQ with z=1.941, and for this case we detected a short-lived \ flare from January 2 to February 2, 2014, where TS 7.5. A TS map integrating over E > 300 MeV photons (Fig. bzq2136 , top left) shows that the signature emerges as a point-like source and the 68 containment region is compatible with 5BZQ J2136+0041 position. Although the \ signature has low significance, it is important to report it as a potential faint \ transient. Since we integrated along short time period, the lower limits computed in the \ SED (Fig. bzq2136 , top right) are not so restrictive.

gmw_00005042.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2145 + 067 contains a radio core with two possible rotation-measure fits [arXiv:0901.2023]. One of these fits suggests a near alignment between its optical and VLBI-core polarization angles [arXiv:0901.2023], while historical observations of the source show repeated detections of negative circular polarization [arXiv:astro-ph/0511838].

sources.summary: The paper presents two acceptable rotation-measure fits for 2145+067, including a high core rotation measure solution with nearly aligned optical and VLBI-core polarization angles.

quotes.quote: Similarly, Fig. 2 shows two possible rotation-measure fits for 2145+067. The left option shows the fit to the nominal observed radio-core polarization angles, which agree with each other to within about 6^ . This fit is acceptable, and implies an offset 53^ . However, if we add 2 to the and 5 to the polarization angles (right panels in Fig. 2), we obtain a comparably good fit that corresponds to a much larger but still reasonable core rotation measure of about +47,000 rad/m ^2 and a zero-wavelength polarization angle of about 74^ . This fit implies a near alignment between the optical and VLBI-core polarization angles, 5^ , which is consistent with an optically thin VLBI core ( = -0.30 between and and = -0.60 between and ).

sources.summary: The paper reports a near-threshold 15 GHz core limit for 2145 + 067 despite repeated historical negative circular-polarization detections, noting the measured value if the cutoff is relaxed.

quotes.quote: 2145 + 067: We find a limit of |m_c| < 0.26 in the core at 15 GHz in March of 2003. In recent integrated measurement from 2001--2002, AAP03 measured m_c = -0.50 0.07 at 4.8 GHz with the UMRAO. The historical integrated measurements (WdP83 and K84) show consistent, repeated detections of negative circular polarization at 5 GHz and below. Given this historical record of detection, it is interesting that the 'limit' reported here came from measurements that had a significance of 1.98 , just barely less than the 2 cutoff we had for defining measurements versus limits. If we allow 1.98 as a measurement, then our fractional level for the core is m_c = -0.17 0.09 .

gmw_00005043.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2345 167 exhibits a compact radio source structure [arXiv:1410.6653] with a core polarization that suggests a longitudinal magnetic field [arXiv:1410.6653]. It displays a monotonic transverse rotation measure gradient southeast of its core [arXiv:1410.6653] and has shown positive circular polarization in its core at 15 GHz [arXiv:astro-ph/0511838].

sources.summary: The paper finds 2345 167 has compact source structure, longitudinal-field core polarization, and a significant monotonic transverse RM gradient southeast of the core.

quotes.quote: 2345 167 Figure fig:pmaps2 d shows our 7.9 GHz polarization map for 2345 167. The core polarization is roughly orthogonal to the jet direction, suggesting a longitudinal magnetic field. Due to the compactness of the source structure, it was not possible to determine reliable shifts between the maps at the different frequencies. Thus, our RM maps were derived without applying additional alignments to the input data.

A transverse RM gradient is visible across the region just southeast of the core. Figure fig:2345RM also shows a slice taken roughly perpendicular to the jet direction in this region, plotted together with its errors. The observed transverse RM gradient is monotonic, and has a significance of about 6 .

sources.summary: The paper detects positive 15 GHz core circular polarization in 2345 167, contrasting it with a 5 GHz VLBA limit and earlier positive integrated detections.

quotes.quote: 2345 167: We detect m_c = +0.40 0.11 in the core at 15 GHz in October of 2002. HAW01 found a limit of |m_c| < 0.11 in the core at 5 GHz in December of 1996. WdP83 and K84 reported intermittent detections (but always with positive sign) of circular polarization in integrated measurements at frequencies of 5 GHz and below.

gmw_00005044.name: NGC 5104
gmw_00005044.overview: NGC 5104 is a galaxy that was not detected in hard X-rays by NuSTAR [arXiv:2004.02884]. However, it was clearly detected by Swift/XRT, showing a thermal spectrum and X-ray luminosity consistent with star-formation-dominated emission [arXiv:2004.02884].

sources.summary: NGC 5104 was not detected in hard X-rays by NuSTAR but was detected by Swift/XRT, with a thermal spectrum and luminosity consistent with star-formation-dominated X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 5104 This source was not detected by , and we determined a 3 upper limit to the observed 3-24 keV luminosity of <4.05 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 . NGC 5104 was clearly detected by Swift /XRT, and the spectrum is well fit by a single thermal plasma model ( tbabs apec in XSPEC), resulting in C-Stat/DOF=26/24. This spectral model is consistent with the idea that most of the X-ray emission in this object is related to star formation. The observed 2 10 keV luminosity of this object is 1.5_ 0.6 ^ +0.7 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 . Following the same approach used for IRAS F06076--2139, we find that the predicted 2--10 keV luminosity associated with the SFR is 9.2 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 , which is even higher than the observed luminosity, suggesting that all the X-ray emission comes from star formation in this object.

gmw_00005045.name: NGC 6907
gmw_00005045.overview: NGC 6907 is an SBbc spiral galaxy that is currently interacting with the nearby S0 galaxy NGC 6908 [arXiv:0802.1713]. Its observed X-ray luminosity is lower than expected from star formation, suggesting that there is no AGN contribution to its X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2004.02884].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6907 as an SBbc spiral interacting with NGC 6908, using H I kinematics, photometry, and spectroscopy to separate its rotation from interaction-induced components.

quotes.quote: NGC 6908, a S0 galaxy situated in direction of NGC 6907, was only recently recognized as a distinct galaxy, instead of only a part of NGC 6907. We present 21 cm radio synthesis observations obtained with the GMRT and optical images and spectroscopy obtained with the Gemini North telescope of this pair of interacting galaxies. From the radio observations we obtained the velocity field and the H i column density map of the whole region containing the NGC 6907/8 pair, and by means of the Gemini multi-object spectroscopy we obtained high quality photometric images and 5 resolution spectra sampling the two galaxies. By comparing the rotation curve of NGC 6907 obtained from the two opposite sides around the main kinematic axis, we were able to distinguish the normal rotational velocity field from the velocity components produced by the interaction between the two galaxies.

Inside a field of 24^ 24^ centered on NGC 6907 (Figure fig1 ), only two extragalactic objects dominate the optical emission: NGC 6907 itself, an SBbc spiral galaxy (Eskridge et al., 2002) with an absolute B magnitude M _ B = -21.34 and a surface brightness of 22.29 mag arcsec ^ 2 at half B total light (Lauberts Valentijn 1989) and NGC 6908, a S0(6/7) galaxy with M _ B =-17.4 (Madore et al. 2007), 43.6 arcsec north-west from NGC 6907.

sources.summary: NGC 6907 was undetected by NuSTAR but detected by Swift/XRT; its X-ray luminosity is below the star-formation expectation, arguing against a significant AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: NGC 6907 NGC 6907 was not detected by NuSTAR , and we determined a 3 upper limit to the observed 3-24 keV luminosity <1.83 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 . The source is clearly detected by Swift /XRT, and the spectrum can be well fit (C-Stat/DOF=67/58) with the same model adopted for NGC 5104. The observed X-ray luminosity in the 2--10 keV range is 7.3_ 1.5 ^ +1.9 10^ 39 erg s^ 1 , which is lower than that expected by star formation ( L_ 2-10 ^ SF =8.4 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 ), suggesting that there is no significant AGN contribution to the X-ray spectrum.

gmw_00005046.name: J123619+621252
gmw_00005046.overview: J123619+621252 is a starburst candidate galaxy located at a redshift of 0.473 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its optical structure suggests it may have a companion or be part of a merging system [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123619+621252 is a starburst candidate with nuclear radio emission in a z=0.473 galaxy whose optical structure suggests a companion or merger.

quotes.quote: J123619+621252 (Starburst Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( > 0.8) and has radio emission extended over 0.4 arcsec. It lies at the nucleus of an I = 19.9 mag galaxy at redshift = 0.473 (Cowie et al. 2004). The optical galaxy appears to have a companion and may be part of a merging system. It is detected by Chandra .

gmw_00005047.name: J123622+621544
gmw_00005047.overview: J123622+621544 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of 0.6471 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is classified as an active galactic nucleus candidate because its extended, low surface-brightness radio emission reaches well beyond the optical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123622+621544 is classified as an AGN candidate because its extended radio emission reaches beyond a spiral galaxy at z=0.6471, implying an undetected core.

quotes.quote: J123622+621544 (AGN Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( > 0.6). The radio structure is extended and low surface-brightness emission seen in the VLA-only image (not shown) extends for nearly 3 arcsec to the north-west. A brighter region of emission region lies 0.75 arcsec to the south-east of the bright nucleus of an I = 19.8 mag spiral galaxy in the HFF at a redshift of 0.6471 (Cowie et al. 2004). As the radio emission extends well beyond the optical galaxy the source is classified as an AGN candidate with an undetected core.

gmw_00005048.name: J123629+621045
gmw_00005048.overview: J123629+621045 is an unclassified galaxy at a redshift of 1.013 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It features a steep-spectrum radio source that extends east-west over 2.7 arcseconds and is detected by Chandra [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123629+621045 is an unclassified steep-spectrum extended radio source coincident with a z=1.013 galaxy nucleus, also identified as a submillimeter source and Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: J123629+621045 (Unclassified): has a steep radio spectrum with >0.86 and is extended east-west over 2.7 arcsec with no evidence of compact emission at the highest angular resolution. The eastern end of the radio source is coincident with the nucleus of an I = 22.2 mag galaxy at a redshift of 1.013 on the CFHT frame (Cohen et al. 2000) but the extension is not correlated with optical emission. Chapman et al. (2004b) identify this system as a sub-mm source (S _ 850 =5.0 1.3 mJy). It is detected by Chandra .

gmw_00005049.name: J123632+620759
gmw_00005049.overview: J123632+620759 is a galaxy located at a redshift of 1.9939 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is associated with an unclassified, steep-spectrum radio source that exhibits extended emission and is detected in the X-ray band by Chandra [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123632+620759 is an unclassified steep-spectrum extended radio source associated with a z=1.9939 galaxy and detected by Chandra.

quotes.quote: J123632+620759 (Unclassified): has a steep radio spectrum ( >0.58 ) and is extended over some 2.6 arcsec on the VLA-only image (not shown). At higher resolution a compact component is found with two-sided extended emission, although the majority of the extended emission seen in the VLA-only image is resolved out. The radio source lies outside the CFHT frame so no optical image is shown in Fig. colourpix . However Cowie et al. (2004) catalogue an R = 24.0 mag galaxy at the position of the radio source with a redshift of 1.9939. It is detected by Chandra .

gmw_00005050.name: J123633+621005
gmw_00005050.overview: J123633+621005 is a face-on spiral galaxy located at a redshift of 1.016 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is classified as a starburst candidate, exhibits a steep radio spectrum, and has been detected by the Chandra X-ray Observatory [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123633+621005 is a starburst candidate extended over a face-on spiral galaxy at z=1.016, lacking compact radio emission and detected by Chandra.

quotes.quote: J123633+621005 (Starburst Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( >0.98 ) and is extended over approximately 1.2 arcsec. There is no evidence for any compact emission at the highest angular resolution. It overlies a face-on I = 21.6 mag spiral galaxy at a redshift of 1.016 (Cowie et al. 2004) on the HFF frame. It is detected by Chandra .

gmw_00005051.name: J123634+621213
gmw_00005051.overview: J123634+621213 is a bright disc galaxy located at a redshift of 0.456 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is classified as a steep-spectrum starburst galaxy that exhibits a dust lane or a possible double nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123634+621213 is a steep-spectrum starburst associated with a bright disc galaxy at z=0.456 whose HFF image shows a dust lane or possible double nucleus.

quotes.quote: J123634+621213 (Starburst): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.74). The extended radio emission is two-sided, surrounding a compact component. However EVN observations at 20 mas resolution do not detect this compact component (Garrett et al. 2001). The source is identified with a bright disc galaxy ( I = 18.7 mag), at a redshift of 0.456 (Cowie et al.

gmw_00005052.name: J123634+621241
gmw_00005052.overview: J123634+621241 is an Scd-type galaxy located at a redshift of 1.219 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is classified as a starburst galaxy that hosts a steep-spectrum radio source with extended emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123634+621241 is a steep-spectrum starburst in a z=1.219 Scd galaxy, with extended radio emission, ISO mid-infrared flux, and likely association with SCUBA HDF850.7.

quotes.quote: J123634+621241 (Starburst): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.74). There is extended emission to the south-west of a compact component which is not detected by the EVN at 20 mas resolution (Garrett et al. 2001). The compact component overlies the nuclear region of an I = 22.3 mag Scd galaxy at a redshift of 1.219 on the HFF WFPC2 frame (Cohen et al. 2000).

gmw_00005053.name: J123635+621424
gmw_00005053.overview: J123635+621424 is a compact radio source that overlies the bright optical nucleus of a face-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is classified as a candidate starburst and active galactic nucleus, with conflicting redshift estimates ranging from low or moderate values to approximately 2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123635+621424 is presented as a compact starburst/AGN candidate on a face-on spiral nucleus, with conflicting redshift evidence ranging from low/moderate redshift to about z=2.

quotes.quote: J123635+621424 (Starburst + AGN?): has a steep radio spectrum ( >0.87 ). It appears relatively compact, with an angular size of only 0.3 arcsec, in contrast to the earlier angular size estimate of 2.8 arcsec reported by Richards et al. (1998). The radio source overlies the bright optical nucleus of a face-on I = 22.6 mag spiral galaxy on the HFF frame. The spatial extent of the galaxy suggests that it is of low to moderate redshift. However, Bauer et al. (2002) list this object as having a spectroscopic redshift close to 2 and Dawson et al. (2002) assign a redshift of 2.011 from several detected emission lines.

gmw_00005054.name: J123640+621009
gmw_00005054.overview: J123640+621009 is an active galactic nucleus candidate that consists of two radio components separated by 1.2 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The northern component likely has a flat radio spectrum, and the system is associated with a faint compact galaxy counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123640+621009 is an AGN candidate with two radio components, a likely flat-spectrum northern component, and a faint compact galaxy counterpart in the HFF.

quotes.quote: J123640+621009 (AGN Candidate): consists of two components separated by 1.2 arcsec. The source as a whole has a radio spectrum of = 0.44, which suggests the presence of a flat-spectrum component. The 8.4-GHz position (Richards et al. 1998) is close to the northerly component indicating that it has a flat spectrum and therefore may be associated with an AGN. Although no evidence for a compact feature can be found at 1.4 GHz, the radio image has a low signal-to-noise ratio.

gmw_00005055.name: J123642+621331
gmw_00005055.overview: J123642+621331 is a dust-obscured starburst galaxy at a redshift of z=4.424 [arXiv:1301.1253, arXiv:1304.3408] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1301.1253, arXiv:astro-ph/0210552]. It features a compact, radio-loud core [arXiv:astro-ph/0202116, arXiv:astro-ph/0409197] with a jet-like extension [arXiv:1301.1253] and diffuse, extended star-forming emission [arXiv:0706.3777].

sources.summary: The paper describes VLA J123642+621331 as a red z=4.424 dust-obscured starburst with an embedded AGN, radio excess, and a compact VLBI core with a jet-like extension.

quotes.quote: VLA J123642+621331 lies just outside the HDF-N, in an adjacent HFF. It has a steep radio spectrum ( =0.94, S_ ^ ); its optical counterpart has I=25 R98, muxlow05 . HST NICMOS 1.1 m (F110W), 1.6 m (F160W), and KPNO 4-m K band imaging detect a red host ( I_ 814 K = 2.0 0.2 ) for the radio source, and spectra obtained by the Keck II telescope show a single strong Ly emission line at z = 4.424 waddington99, dickinson98 . This source is also detected in the ISO 15 m and Chandra soft X-ray bands aussel99, brandt01 . waddington99 interpret the object as a dust-obscured starburst with an embedded AGN.

The deep MERLIN-VLA observations at 1.4 GHz muxlow05 resolved VLA J123642+621331, showing extended emission adjacent to an unresolved core, which corresponds to the core detected by the EVN 1.6 GHz observations garrett01 . The ratio of its radio and FIR luminosity indicates that the object has a modest radio excess garrett02 , providing further evidence for an embedded AGN: its q_ 24 value is 0.35 . The deep, high-resolution global VLBI image removes all doubts, revealing a jet-like extension ( 120 Jy) emanating from a compact AGN core. The integrated flux density is in agreement with the garrett01 measurement. The separation between the jet and the AGN core is about 70 pc.

sources.summary: The paper describes VLA J123642 + 621331 as a z=4.424 Seyfert galaxy and radio-detected AGN whose hard X-ray emission and radio loudness support an active nucleus with a flat X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: We investigate here the X-ray properties of the three z>4 AGNs spectroscopically identified in the : the z=5.186 quasar \ (Barger et al. 2002, hereafter B02; 23.4 ), and the two Seyfert galaxies \ (B02; 21.4 ) and \ (VLA J123642 + 621331; Waddington et al. 1999, hereafter W99; Brandt et al. 2001b; 21.6 ) at z=4.137 and z=4.424 , respectively; the latter Seyfert galaxy is the only object in our sample with detected radio emission (0.47 mJy at 1.4 GHz; Richards 2000).

faintest X-ray source in our sample and lies just outside the . Detailed studies of this source indicate a redshift of z=4.424 (W99; R. A. Windhorst 2001, private communication; but also see 2.2 of Barger, Cowie, Richards 2000). The presence of an AGN is suggested by multi-wavelength studies. The apparent radio jet in the combined MERLIN/VLA image (W99) indicates the presence of an AGN, as does the source's large 1.4 GHz radio luminosity ( 7.8 10^ 32 ) , which is more typical of AGNs than starburst galaxies (e.g., Bauer et al. 2002). From an X-ray perspective, Brandt et al. (2001b), based on a 480 ks \ observation, argued that its X-ray to-optical flux ratio and X-ray luminosity were suggestive of the presence of an active nucleus. This is confirmed by the 2 Ms results reported in this Letter.

In particular, the detection of \ in the 2--8 keV band (corresponding to the rest-frame 11--43 keV band ) strongly supports the presence of an active nucleus rather than only a starburst. The relatively flat X-ray spectrum ( =1.55^ +0.61 _ 0.54 ) can be due either to the presence of intrinsic absorption (not constrained by the present observation; see Table 2) or to the radio loudness of this source.

sources.summary: J123642+621331 is described as a compact steep-spectrum starburst/AGN candidate with no HFF optical counterpart, likely a very red high-redshift starburst galaxy containing a weak embedded AGN.

quotes.quote: J123642+621331 (Starburst + AGN?): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.94). The radio structure consists of a compact component together with emission extended over 0.4 arcsec. Garrett et al. (2001) detect the dominant compact component with the EVN at an angular resolution of 20 mas; it may therefore be a steep-spectrum AGN core. There is no optical counterpart at the detection threshold in the HFF I band image.

Waddington et al. (1999) detect a very red object at the radio source position with NICMOS, in J and H-band, and with the KPNO 4-m in K band. On the basis of a single Ly emission line and the observed spectral energy distribution, they ascribe a redshift of 4.424 and argue that it is a distant starburst galaxy with a weak embedded AGN. This interpretation is consistent with the radio morphology. The source is detected by both ISO and Chandra .

sources.summary: The paper describes J123642+621331 as a z=4.424 radio source with starburst-like infrared properties, diffuse star-forming emission, and an AGN-dominated compact core that may be a type-II AGN.

quotes.quote: The highest redshift radio source, J123642+621331, at z=4.424 , has a high total 1.4-GHz flux density (467 Jy). It has a steep radio spectrum, it is a very reddened NICMOS detection Waddington99 and it was detected by ISO at 15 m, all properties consistent with starforming activity. Its 1.4-GHz/FIR ratio, however, is 20--50 times higher than other HDF(N) star-forming galaxies Garrett02 . The MERLIN+VLA image shows that about 10 of the flux is diffuse and extended at between 100--200 mas from the core ( 1 kpc), which is likely to contain the starburst component.

The star formation rate inferred from the IR flux density is 1000 M _ yr ^ 1 , comparable to the other highest star formation rates deduced for starbursts in the HDF(N), which would account for 1 of the radio emission. The compact core is AGN-dominated; the EVN detected over half the 1.4-GHz flux Garrett02 and global VLBI Chi06 resolves a jet component a few tens of pc from the core. J123642+621331 has a measured =1.35 , above the limit for type-II AGN, but Padovani04 noted that high-redshift sources might be misclassified.

sources.summary: The paper describes J123642+621331 as a z=4.424 dusty starburst with an embedded AGN, compact 5 GHz emission, and a steep spectrum consistent with a composite high-redshift source.

quotes.quote: J123642+621331 : Waddi99 argued that this source is most likely a distant ( z=4.424 ) dusty starburst with a weak embedded AGN. At 1.4 GHz the source shows both diffuse and compact structures (M05). The latter has been revealed at higher resolution by VLBI observations at 1.4 GHz Garrett01,Chi13 , confirming the presence of AGN emission. At 5 GHz, we detect a compact component coincident with the peak of the 1.4 GHz emission.

Since at 1.4 GHz the radio source is extended, we use only the flux of the compact emission at 1.4 GHz to derive a spectral index of 0.9 0.2 . This value is consistent with the spectral index derived at arcsec-scale between 1.4 and 8.5 GHz. The steepness of the spectrum may confirm the composite nature of the source, and/or be explained by steepening effects introduced by the (large) k correction of this high redshift source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies VLA J123642+621331 as a z=4.4 radio-loud AGN/star-forming galaxy, detected by deep-field VLBI and standing out from the FIR/radio correlation used for the HDF-N sample.

quotes.quote: The sample is ``contaminated'' by at least 1 distant ( z=4.4 ), radio-loud AGN, VLA J123642+621331. This source has recently been detected by the first deep field VLBI observations of the HDF-N and is clearly identified as an outlier in the FIR/radio correlation.

There is one obvious outlier observed in both Figure 1 and 2. This outlier (seen to the extreme right of both figures) corresponds to a z=4.4 source (VLA J123642+621331), and is widely believed to be an optically faint, dust obscured star forming galaxy, which also harbours an AGN (Waddington et al. 1999, Garrett et al. 2001, Brandt et al. 2001). The fact that it deviates from the FIR/radio correlation is entirely to be expected, and suggests that just as the locally derived correlation can distinguish between sources powered by AGN and star formation processes, the extended correlation can very likely do the same thing for moderate redshift sources.

sources.summary: The review describes VLA J123642+621331 as an optically faint, high-redshift radio source whose compact VLBI component strongly indicates AGN-dominated radio emission.

quotes.quote: One of the sources detected by the EVN (VLA J123642+621331) is not detected in I-band ( I > 25^ m ) and is only just detected in the near-IR in H J-band ( ). Given K 23^ m (and assuming the K-z relation holds for these faint systems), Waddington et al. (1999) identified a single spectral-line detected by the Keck telescope as Ly  at a redshift of z=4.424 . Although the radio emission is extended over a scale of 0.6 arcseconds ( ), the detection of an unresolved radio-loud component by the EVN places a limit on the brightness temperature of the source, T_ b > 10^ 5  K, strongly suggestive of an AGN origin for the radio emission. This system is also detected in the ISO 15 supplemental catalogue ( ) and is an obvious out-lier in the K-corrected and extrapolated FIR-Radio correlation derived by Garrett (2002). There is little doubt that this is an optically faint radio source for which the radio emission is very much dominated by AGN processes.

gmw_00005056.name: J123642+621545
gmw_00005056.overview: J123642+621545 is an unclassified galaxy at a redshift of 0.857 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its radio structure consists of a compact core component overlying the galaxy nucleus and extended emission reaching nearly 1.5 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123642+621545 has compact and extended radio emission centered on a z=0.857 galaxy nucleus; ISO and Chandra detections leave its possible AGN nature unclear.

quotes.quote: J123642+621545 (Unclassified): has an intermediate radio spectrum ( =0.50 ). The radio structure consists of a compact component and extended emission to the NE and SW with the latter reaching nearly 1.5 arcseconds from the core. The core component overlies the nucleus of an I = 20.7 mag galaxy on the CFHT frame at a redshift of 0.857 (Hornschemeier et al. 2001). The source is detected by both ISO and Chandra ; the compact component could be an AGN, but the nature of this object remains unclear.

gmw_00005057.name: J123644+621133
gmw_00005057.overview: J123644+621133 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:1304.3408, arXiv:astro-ph/0501679] hosted by an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of 1.050 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It features a bright, flat-spectrum compact core [arXiv:1304.3408, arXiv:astro-ph/0501679] and a short radio jet extending southward [arXiv:1304.3408].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123644+621133 as an FR I radio galaxy with a compact core, short jet, 5 GHz core-plus-jet morphology, and a relatively flat spectrum.

quotes.quote: J123644+621133 : This is a FR I radio galaxy. 1.4 GHz sub-arcsec scale imaging shows a bright compact core and a short jet extending southwards (M05). 1.4 GHz VLBI observations Garrett01,Chi13 detected the compact core component. A core+jet morphology is observed also in our 5 GHz image, confirming the AGN nature of the source. Considering the same area emitting at 5 and 1.4 GHz, we derive a rather flat spectrum with 0.2 0.1 .

sources.summary: J123644+621133 is identified as an FRI radio galaxy with a bright flat-spectrum core, classical FRI structure, an elliptical host at z=1.050, and faint Chandra emission.

quotes.quote: J123644+621133 (AGN -- FRI radio galaxy): has a bright core with a flat radio spectrum ( = 0.1, Richards et al. 1998) with steep spectrum emission oriented N--S and extending for about 15 arcsec in the VLA-only image (not shown). Overall the radio source exhibits a classical FRI structure. It is optically identified with an I = 20.9 mag elliptical galaxy at a redshift of 1.050 (Cohen et al. 2000).

gmw_00005058.name: J123645+620754
gmw_00005058.overview: J123645+620754 is an unclassified extended radio source associated with an R = 23.0 magnitude galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Located at a redshift of 1.433, the source is extended east-west over approximately 3 arcseconds and has been faintly detected by Chandra [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123645+620754 is an unclassified extended radio source associated with an R=23.0 galaxy at z=1.433 and detected faintly by Chandra.

quotes.quote: J123645+620754 (Unclassified): lies close to the edge of the 10-arcmin field. The source is extended east-west over some 3 arcsec, and there is no evidence for any compact emission. The position given in Table 10arcmin.tab is from the VLA-only image at 2-arcsec resolution. The radio source lies outside the CFHT frame so no optical image is shown in Fig. colourpix . However Cowie et al. (2004) catalogue an R = 23.0 mag galaxy at the position of the radio source with a redshift of 1.433.

gmw_00005059.name: J123646+621404
gmw_00005059.overview: J123646+621404 is an active galactic nucleus hosted by a nearly face-on disc galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The source exhibits an inverted radio spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679, arXiv:1304.3408] and features a compact core with extended emission to the southeast [arXiv:1304.3408].
