sources.summary: J123646+621404 is described as an inverted-spectrum AGN in a nearly face-on disc galaxy; broad emission lines, ISO emission, and Chandra detection support nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: J123646+621404 (AGN): has an inverted radio spectrum ( =-0.04). The radio structure has a compact component and two-sided extended emission. The radio emission overlies the nucleus of a nearly face-on disc galaxy ( I = 20.7 mag) at a redshift of 0.961 (Cowie et al. 2004), which exhibits a broad emission line spectrum (Phillips et al. 1997).

sources.summary: The paper reports that J123646+621404 has a VLBI core, southeast extended emission at 1.4 and 5 GHz, and an inverted spectrum confirming AGN radio emission.

quotes.quote: J123646+621404 : An unresolved core has been detected by VLBI observations at 1.4 GHz Garrett01,Chi13 . Extended emission southeast of the nucleus was detected at 1.4 GHz by M05. At 5 GHz we observe a compact core and again some extended emission southeast of the nucleus. The radio spectrum is inverted on both arcsec and sub-arcsec scales, confirming the AGN nature of the radio emission.

gmw_00005060.name: J123646+621445
gmw_00005060.overview: J123646+621445 is an unclassified radio source that overlies a faint, compact galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It exhibits a steep radio spectrum and is extended over 2.3 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123646+621445 is a steep-spectrum extended source on a faint compact galaxy; nearby optical and ISO sources are argued not to be confidently associated.

quotes.quote: J123646+621445 (Unclassified): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.98 ) and is extended over 2.3 arcsec with the major axis roughly aligned with J123646+621448 which lies 6 arcsec to the north west. However, J123646+621445 overlies a fairly compact faint (I 23.9 mag) galaxy. Since both sources overlie separate optical identifications, it is most likely that they are unrelated. The I = 20.4 mag galaxy 2.5 arcsec to the NE of J123646+621445 has a measured redshift of 0.558 (Cohen et al. 2000), but does not seem to be related to the radio source.

gmw_00005061.name: J123649+621313
gmw_00005061.overview: J123649+621313 is a steep-spectrum starburst galaxy at a redshift of 0.475 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The radio emission is extended along the major axis of the host disk galaxy, which is associated with faint Chandra X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123649+621313 is a steep-spectrum starburst extended along a disc galaxy at z=0.475, with ISO detection, possible SCUBA association, and faint Chandra emission.

quotes.quote: J123649+621313 (Starburst): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.72) with no evidence of any compact structure at 0.2-arcsec resolution. It is extended by approximately 1 arcsec along the major axis of an I =21.1 mag disc galaxy at a redshift of 0.475 (Cohen et al. 2000). The galaxy is in the ISO catalog of HDF sources with S_ 15 m = 320 Jy and may be associated with SCUBA HDF850.4 (see Section sect-HDF850.4 ). There is a faint Chandra counterpart.

gmw_00005062.name: J123651+621030
gmw_00005062.overview: J123651+621030 is a steep-spectrum starburst galaxy at a redshift of 0.410 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The radio source is extended over the nuclear region of a disc galaxy and has a faint Chandra counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123651+621030 is a steep-spectrum starburst extended over a z=0.410 disc-galaxy nucleus, with ISO 15 micron emission and faint Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: J123651+621030 (Starburst): has a steep-spectrum ( =0.74 ). The radio source is extended over 1.2 arcsec and overlies the nuclear region of an I = 19.9 mag disc galaxy at a redshift of 0.410 (Cohen et al. 2000). It is detected by ISO with S_ 15 m = 341 Jy. There is a faint Chandra counterpart.

gmw_00005063.name: J123651+621221
gmw_00005063.overview: J123651+621221 is a faint galaxy with a steep radio spectrum that is considered a candidate starburst and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its extended radio structure lacks a compact component, which favors a starburst interpretation over an active nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123651+621221 as an extended steep-spectrum starburst/AGN candidate whose radio morphology favors starburst emission and whose optical counterpart is very faint.

quotes.quote: J123651+621221 (Starburst + AGN?): has a steep spectrum ( >0.71 ). The MERLIN+VLA image shows a source extended over 1.2 arcsec with no evidence for any compact component. Garrett et al. (2001) also find no evidence for any compact component with the EVN at an angular resolution of 20 mas. The radio structure thus favours a starburst rather than an AGN interpretation. The radio source is identified with very faint emission (I > 28 mag) on the HDF frame.

gmw_00005064.name: J123652+621444
gmw_00005064.overview: J123652+621444 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679, arXiv:1304.3408] that exhibits a variable inverted-spectrum core [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679, arXiv:1304.3408]. Its radio structure features a one-sided jet-like structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679] and shows evidence of low surface-brightness emission extending over 30 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123652+621444 is treated as an AGN with a variable inverted-spectrum core, a one-sided jet-like feature, possible 100 kpc extension, and an elliptical-galaxy nucleus.

quotes.quote: J123652+621444 (AGN): has an inverted spectrum ( = -0.12 ) (Richards et al. 1998) and is observed to vary in intensity on the time scale of months, suggesting the presence of an AGN (Richards 2000). The radio structure shows a compact core and one sided jet-like feature to the east 1.3 arcsec in length. The low resolution WSRT image of this source (Garrett et al. 2000) shows evidence for low surface-brightness emission extending over 30 arcsec to the east.

sources.summary: The paper describes J123652+621444 as an AGN with a flat arcsecond-scale spectrum, a compact 5 GHz core coincident with the 1.4 GHz peak, and an inverted spectrum.

quotes.quote: J123652+621444 : This source is characterized by a flat arcsec-scale spectral index and was classified as an AGN by M05. The radio structure at 5 GHz shows a compact core coincident with the peak at 1.4 GHz. The derived spectral index is inverted ( 0.2 0.2 ), confirming the AGN nature of the source.

gmw_00005065.name: J123653+621139
gmw_00005065.overview: J123653+621139 is a steep-spectrum starburst galaxy located at a redshift of 1.275 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its radio structure features a compact component overlying the galaxy's nucleus, which exhibits a slight eastward extension and is detected at 15 micrometers [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123653+621139 is a steep-spectrum starburst whose compact radio component lies on a z=1.275 compact galaxy nucleus, with ISO and faint Chandra counterparts.

quotes.quote: J123653+621139 (Starburst): has a steep spectrum ( >0.77 ). The radio structure shows a compact component with a slight ( 0.3 arcsec) extension to the east. The compact component overlies the nucleus of an I = 21.9 mag compact galaxy at a redshift of 1.275 (Cohen et al. 2000). It is also detected by ISO at 15 m with a flux density of 180 Jy.

gmw_00005066.name: J123659+621449
gmw_00005066.overview: J123659+621449 is a starburst candidate galaxy at a redshift of 0.761 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It features a disturbed disc structure with a dust lane or double nucleus, suggesting it may be undergoing a merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123659+621449 is a starburst candidate over a disturbed z=0.761 disc galaxy showing a dust lane or double nucleus and possible merger activity.

quotes.quote: J123659+621449 (Starburst Candidate): is extended over 1.0 arcsec and shows no sign of compact emission at 0.2-arcsec resolution. The radio structure overlies a disturbed I = 20.9 mag disc galaxy at a redshift of 0.761 (Cowie et al. 2004). The galaxy appears to show a dust-lane or double nucleus and may be undergoing a merger. The source is detected by ISO and has a faint Chandra counterpart.

gmw_00005067.name: J123707+621408
gmw_00005067.overview: J123707+621408 is a high-redshift starburst candidate galaxy located at a redshift of 2.48 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The system is identified as a submillimeter galaxy with extended radio emission and a faint optical counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123707+621408 is described as a starburst candidate with extended radio emission, no compact component, a faint optical galaxy counterpart, submillimeter identification, and Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: J123707+621408 (Starburst Candidate): has a relatively flat radio spectrum ( =0.29 ). The source is extended over 0.9 arcsec and there is no evidence for any compact component. The radio morphology favours a starburst classification and the relatively flat spectrum may be attributed to low-frequency free-free absorption. There is no obvious optical identification on the HFF frame although there does appear to be a faint I = 24.9 mag galaxy at the radio position on the CFHT frame. Chapman et al. (2004b) identify the system as a sub-mm galaxy (S _ 850 =4.7 1.5 mJy) with a redshift of 2.48.

gmw_00005068.name: J123708+621056
gmw_00005068.overview: J123708+621056 is a starburst candidate galaxy located at a redshift of 0.422 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It features a relatively flat radio spectrum likely caused by free-free absorption, with its radio emission displaced from the central region of an edge-on disc galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: The paper describes J123708+621056 as a starburst candidate with a flat radio spectrum likely affected by free-free absorption and radio emission offset from an edge-on disc galaxy.

quotes.quote: J123708+621056 (Starburst Candidate): has a relatively flat radio spectrum ( = 0.35) but there is no evidence for any compact radio component at 0.2-arcsec resolution. The radio spectrum may therefore be due to substantial free-free absorption at 1.4 GHz. The source is extended over 0.7 arcsec and is displaced by 0.5 arcsec from the central region of an I = 19.8 mag edge-on disc galaxy at a redshift of 0.422 (Cowie et al. 2004). This separation is probably real since the registration errors, even towards the edge of the HFF, are substantially lower than 0.5 arcsec.

gmw_00005069.name: J123711+621330
gmw_00005069.overview: J123711+621330 is an active galactic nucleus candidate with an estimated photometric redshift of 1.112 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The host galaxy is optically asymmetric and contains a compact radio component with a one-sided, jet-like emission structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J123711+621330 as an AGN candidate whose compact radio component, jet-like extension, asymmetric optical galaxy, and offset Chandra emission define a complex source.

quotes.quote: J123711+621330 (AGN Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( = 0.69). The source contains a compact radio component with one-sided jet-like emission extending 1.2 arcsec to the east. The compact component is coincident with the brightest part of an I = 22.7 mag galaxy. The optical galaxy is asymmetric in the same sense as the radio structure. Bauer et al. (2002) estimate a photometric redshift of 1.112 for this galaxy.

gmw_00005070.name: J123711+621325
gmw_00005070.overview: J123711+621325 is a submillimeter starburst galaxy candidate at a redshift of 1.99 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. It is identified with a faint optical galaxy, exhibiting a steep radio spectrum and faint X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123711+621325 is a steep-spectrum starburst candidate identified with a faint galaxy and as a z=1.99 submillimeter galaxy, with faint Chandra emission.

quotes.quote: J123711+621325 (Starburst Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( >1.16 ) and extends over 0.8 arcsec and shows no sign of compact emission at 0.2-arcsec resolution. The source is identified with an I = 25 mag galaxy on the CFHT frame. Chapman et al. (2004b) identify the system as a sub-mm galaxy (S _ 850 =4.2 1.3 mJy) with a redshift of 1.99. There is a faint Chandra counterpart.

gmw_00005071.name: J123716+621512
gmw_00005071.overview: J123716+621512 is an active galactic nucleus candidate located at a redshift of 0.230 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. The source exhibits a relatively flat radio spectrum, a compact component, and one-sided emission extending 0.4 arcsec to the south-west [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123716+621512 is an AGN candidate with a flat radio spectrum, compact component, one-sided emission, and a z=0.230 galaxy nucleus counterpart.

quotes.quote: J123716+621512 (AGN Candidate): has a relatively flat radio spectrum ( = 0.41). The source consists of a compact component and one-sided emission extending 0.4 arcsec to the south-west. The source overlies the nucleus of an I = 19.8 mag galaxy on the CFHT frame with a redshift of 0.230 (Cowie et al. 2004). There is a faint Chandra counterpart.

gmw_00005072.name: J123716+621643
gmw_00005072.overview: J123716+621643 is a starburst candidate galaxy located at a redshift of 0.557 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its radio emission is offset from the brighter galaxy and overlies what appears to be a pair of merging galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123716+621643 is a starburst candidate whose radio emission overlies a possible merging-galaxy pair and is offset from the brighter z=0.557 galaxy.

quotes.quote: J123716+621643 (Starburst Candidate): is extended over 0.9 arcsec. The radio emission overlies what appears to be a pair of merging galaxies on the CFHT frame. The radio emission is offset from the brighter galaxy by 1 arcsec. This is real since the registration errors in this region are < 250 mas. The brighter galaxy has I = 21.6 mag and a redshift of 0.557 (Cohen et al.

gmw_00005073.name: J123716+621733
gmw_00005073.overview: J123716+621733 is an active galactic nucleus candidate located at a redshift of 1.146 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679]. Its radio structure features a steep-spectrum compact component overlying the galaxy nucleus, accompanied by a one-sided extension and a Chandra X-ray detection [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123716+621733 is an AGN candidate whose steep-spectrum compact component and one-sided extension overlie a z=1.146 galaxy nucleus and have Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: J123716+621733 (AGN Candidate): has a steep radio spectrum ( >0.76 ). The radio structure contains a compact component with a 0.6 arcsec one-sided extension to the S-W. The compact component overlies the nucleus of an I = 22.2 mag galaxy with a redshift of 1.146 (Cowie et al. 2004). It is detected by Chandra .

gmw_00005074.name: J123721+621129
gmw_00005074.overview: J123721+621129 is an active galactic nucleus system [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679] characterized by a VLBI-detected core [arXiv:1304.3408]. The source features an inverted-spectrum compact radio component [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679, arXiv:1304.3408] and exhibits one-sided emission extending 0.8 arcseconds to the north [arXiv:astro-ph/0501679].

sources.summary: J123721+621129 is presented as an almost certain AGN with an inverted-spectrum compact radio component, one-sided emission, no bright optical identification, and a very red counterpart.

quotes.quote: J123721+621129 (AGN): has an inverted spectrum ( = --0.28) with a dominant compact radio component and one-sided emission extending 0.8 arcsec to the north. This source is thus almost certainly an AGN system. There is no optical identification at the radio source position brighter than I = 25 mag. There is a distorted disc galaxy to the NE with an estimated photometric redshift of 2.468 (Bauer et al. 2002), but the radio-optical offset of over 1 arcsec is far too high to be accounted for by astrometric errors.

sources.summary: The paper says J123721+621129 has a VLBI-detected core, inverted arcsecond-scale spectrum dominated by the AGN core, and an unresolved inverted-spectrum 5 GHz core.

quotes.quote: J123721+621129 : The core was detected at 1.4 GHz by global VLBI observations Chi13 , confirming the AGN classification by M05. The arcsec-scale radio spectrum is inverted, indicating that the overall radio emission is dominated by the AGN core. At 5 GHz we also detect an inverted-spectrum ( 0.4 0.2 ) unresolved core, that overlaps the peak emission at 1.4 GHz.

gmw_00005075.name: ACO496J043306.97-131238.8 (G-01)
gmw_00005075.overview: ACO496J043306.97-131238.8 (G-01) is an early-type spiral galaxy featuring an extended disc, a small bar, and a small red bulge [arXiv:0805.0219]. It is located in the outer regions of a bright X-ray halo and exhibits an intermediate stellar age with slightly sub-solar metallicity [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-01 as an early-type spiral with an extended disc, a small bar, a red bulge, intermediate age, slightly sub-solar metallicity, and unusually high magnesium enhancement.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043306.97-131238.8 (G-01) : This early-type spiral exhibits an extended disc and a small bar, which is aligned nearly along the minor axis of the external disc, explaining the isophotal twisting. The colour maps indicate the presence of a small red bulge and the velocity dispersion is in agreement with a low-mass bulge. Intermediate age and slightly sub-solar metallicity are normal for such a low-luminosity spiral. However, [ Mg/Fe ] is super-solar and is among the highest values obtained for such faint galaxies. The galaxy is located in the outer parts of the bright X-ray halo observed with Chandra (Dupke White 2003) and XMM-Newton (Tanaka et al. 2006).

gmw_00005076.name: ACO496J043308.85-130235.6 (G-02)
gmw_00005076.overview: ACO496J043308.85-130235.6 (G-02) is a nearly edge-on, late-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a dust lane on its west side, central H II emission, and obscured star-forming regions in the southern disc [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G-02 as an edge-on late-type spiral with a dust lane, obscured southern star formation, central HII emission, and a not older than 4 Gyr stellar population.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043308.85-130235.6 (G-02) : This late type spiral is nearly edge-on. A dust lane is seen on the westside of the galaxy on the colour maps. Star formation regions in the southern part of the disc seem strongly obscured by dust. The residuals of the fitting exhibit a narrow H line in emission, indicating that HII regions are found in the centre. As for the previous galaxy, the stellar population is not older than 4 Gyr, and the metallicity is slightly sub-solar.

gmw_00005077.name: ACO496J043312.79-130449.3 (G-03)
gmw_00005077.overview: ACO496J043312.79-130449.3 is a faint, non-nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the outskirts of the Abell 496 cluster's X-ray halo [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts an old, metal-poor stellar population and shows no signs of tidal interaction or ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper presents G-03 as a regular non-nucleated dwarf elliptical with low velocity dispersion, an old metal-poor stellar population, and no observed stripping or tidal interaction.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043312.79-130449.3 (G-03) : This faint and regular dwarf elliptical galaxy is not nucleated and has no colour gradient. Its low velocity dispersion, relatively old age, very metal-poor stellar population, and low surface brightness make it a normal dwarf elliptical. Its projected distance (d _ proj = 470 kpc) is large and the galaxy is found in the outskirts of the X-ray halo. No sign of ram-pressure stripping or tidal interaction is observed, as expected if the galaxy is on a roughly circular orbit or just entering the X-ray halo.

gmw_00005078.name: ACO496J043317.75-131536.6 (G-04)
gmw_00005078.overview: ACO496J043317.75-131536.6 (G-04) is a smooth, elongated dwarf lenticular or elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts intermediate-age, metal-poor stars and a faint embedded bar or internal disc, with no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-04 as a smooth dwarf lenticular or elliptical with a faint embedded bar or disc, intermediate-age metal-poor stars, and possible core star formation induced by cluster gas interaction.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043317.75-131536.6 (G-04) : This dwarf lenticular or elliptical galaxy has a smooth and elongated appearance. The unsharp masked image seems to indicate the presence of a faint bar or nearly edge-on internal disc, but this embedded structure is certainly faint since it is not seen on colour maps. With a low velocity dispersion, an intermediate age for its stellar population and a correspondingly low metallicity, this galaxy does not differ strongly from ``classical'' dwarfs. Both its projected distance (d _ proj = 183 kpc) and its radial velocity suggest that interaction with the hot intracluster medium could have induced a short star formation episode in the ACO496J043317.75-131536.6 (G-04) centre. Despite a low S/N spectrum, we can be sure that no emission lines are present.

gmw_00005079.name: ACO496J043318.95-131726.9 (G-05)
gmw_00005079.overview: ACO496J043318.95-131726.9 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy with a central region that exhibits redder colors than its outskirts [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts an old, low-metallicity stellar population and may have experienced tidal disc stripping during a trajectory through its cluster's core [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-05 as a dwarf elliptical with a redder center, old low-metallicity stellar population, and a trajectory suggesting previous core crossing and tidal disc stripping.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043318.95-131726.9 (G-05) : The central part of this dwarf elliptical has redder colours than the outskirts and the velocity dispersion corresponds to the mean value for this absolute magnitude. However the age of the stellar population is close to the maximum value allowed with our PEGASE.HR database (17.6 Gyr) both with quite a low metallicity. Like for the previous galaxy, its projected distance (d _ proj = 184 kpc) and its radial velocity suggest ACO496J043318.95-131726.9 (G-05) is on a trajectory crossing the cluster core. But the progenitor of this dwarf could have already consumed most of its gas when it entered the X-ray halo and part of its disc could have been tidally stripped, leaving a low-mass bulge nearly intact.

gmw_00005080.name: ACO496J043320.35-130314.9 (G-06)
gmw_00005080.overview: ACO496J043320.35-130314.9 (G-06) is an intermediate-luminosity lenticular galaxy that features a strong bar and ring system [arXiv:0805.0219]. It contains an intermediate-age, slightly metal-poor stellar population and shows no detectable emission lines in its spectrum [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-06 as the brightest lenticular in the spectroscopic sample, with a strong bar-ring system, intermediate-age metal-poor enhanced stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043320.35-130314.9 (G-06) : This lenticular galaxy has an intermediate luminosity and is the brightest galaxy of the spectroscopic sample. The unsharp masking technique reveals a strong bar and ring system, which is also redder on the colour maps compared to the underlying disc. The galaxy has an intermediate age, a slightly metal poor and overabundant stellar population, with a quite normal velocity dispersion for the bulge of an S0 galaxy. No emission lines are detected in the spectrum. ACO496J043320.35-130314.9 (G-06) is found outside the X-ray halo in the northern part of the cluster.

gmw_00005081.name: ACO496J043321.37-130416.6 (G-07)
gmw_00005081.overview: ACO496J043321.37-130416.6 is a peculiar dwarf lenticular or early-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features an elongated central structure that is bluer than its underlying disc, an extended spiral pattern, and active star formation [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes G-07 as a peculiar dwarf lenticular or early-type spiral with a blue elongated center, extended spiral pattern, [OIII] emission, and very blue central color.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043321.37-130416.6 (G-07) : This dwarf lenticular or early-type spiral is very peculiar since the elongated central structure is bluer than the underlying disc. Both the unsharp masked image and colour maps exhibit a spiral pattern extending over most of the inclined disc. An [ OIII ] line is noticed in emission on the residual after fitting the stellar population but H is located outside the observed wavelength range. This galaxy has the second bluest ( u^* - ' ) colour index in an aperture of 1.2 arcsec of our whole sample. The stellar population fitting gives a very young age and a slightly sub-solar metallicity, but both estimations have large uncertainties and we suspect that a stellar population with an age of 1.2 Gyr and a metallicity of [ Fe/H ] =-0.55 dex could also be present in the galaxy centre, indicating that star formation began at that time and is continuing up to now.

gmw_00005082.name: ACO496J043324.61-131111.9 (G-08)
gmw_00005082.overview: ACO496J043324.61-131111.9 (G-08) is a faint, regular dwarf elliptical galaxy that may host a nucleus [arXiv:0805.0219]. It is composed of an old, low-metallicity stellar population and exhibits narrow absorption lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-08 as a faint regular dwarf elliptical that may host a nucleus, with old low-metallicity stars, narrow absorption lines, and a position about half an X-ray-halo radius from the cluster center.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043324.61-131111.9 (G-08) : This is a faint regular dE galaxy, which could be nucleated as evidenced by a slightly bluer centre in the colour maps. The faint S/N ratio of the spectrum does not allow us to determine the age and metallicity precisely, but the stellar population seems to have an old age and low metallicity, both consistent with the values generally expected in such dwarfs. Only the central velocity dispersion can be determined with a good precision since the absorption lines are clearly very narrow. Its velocity is smaller than the mean cluster velocity and its projected distance to the cluster centre is 209 kpc, which is about half the radius of the X-ray halo.

gmw_00005083.name: ACO496J043324.91-131342.6 (G-09)
gmw_00005083.overview: ACO496J043324.91-131342.6 (G-09) is a dwarf elliptical galaxy with a slightly concentrated structure and a redder core [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts an old, low-metallicity stellar population with no detected emission lines, and its outer regions exhibit colors typical of lenticular galaxy discs [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-09 as a dwarf elliptical with central concentration, a redder core, lenticular-like outer colors, low velocity dispersion, and an old low-metallicity stellar population.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043324.91-131342.6 (G-09) : This dE exhibits a slightly concentrated structure revealed by the unsharp masking technique and a colour gradient with a redder colour in the centre. The outer part has colour indices typical of those seen in the discs of lenticulars in our sample. Its velocity dispersion is low, and the age of its stellar population is quite old, with a low metallicity and a nearly solar abundance ratio. No emission lines are detected. Its velocity is about 700 km s ^ 1 lower than the mean cluster velocity.

gmw_00005084.name: ACO496J043325.10-130906.6 (G-10)
gmw_00005084.overview: ACO496J043325.10-130906.6 (G-10) is a faint dwarf elliptical galaxy that lacks any substructure or a nucleus [arXiv:0805.0219]. It displays a slight color gradient, has no detected emission lines, and exhibits a relatively high velocity dispersion for its luminosity [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-10 as a faint dwarf elliptical without substructure, showing only a slight color gradient, uncertain stellar-population parameters, no emission lines, and relatively high velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043325.10-130906.6 (G-10) : This dE is faint and does not show any substructures. Only a slight colour gradient appears on colour maps, but there is no signature of a nucleus. Stellar population parameters are uncertain because of low signal-to-noise ratio in the spectrum. No emission lines are detected. The velocity dispersion is low, but higher than for galaxies of similar luminosity.

gmw_00005085.name: ACO496J043325.40-131414.6 (G-11)
gmw_00005085.overview: ACO496J043325.40-131414.6 (G-11) is a faint dwarf elliptical galaxy with a marginally resolved, red central nucleus [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a metal-poor stellar population of intermediate age, normal velocity dispersion, and no detectable emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-11 as a faint dwarf elliptical with a marginally resolved red nucleus-like center, intermediate age, metal-poor stars, normal velocity dispersion, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043325.40-131414.6 (G-11) : This is a faint dE galaxy with no substructure, except a slightly concentrated red central part. This red centre is, however, only marginally resolved and could be considered as a nucleus. The galaxy exhibits an intermediate age, a metal poor stellar population and a quite normal velocity dispersion for such a faint dwarf. No emission lines are detected in the spectrum. Like the previous galaxy, this dwarf is in projection near the cluster core (d _ proj = 130 kpc), but also very near another dE, ACO496J043324.91-131342.6 (G-09).

gmw_00005086.name: ACO496J043325.54-130408.0 (G-12)
gmw_00005086.overview: ACO496J043325.54-130408.0 (G-12) is a low-surface-brightness dwarf elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a metal-poor, intermediate-age stellar population with a small color gradient that becomes bluer in its outer regions, and its spectrum shows no emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-12 as a low-surface-brightness dwarf elliptical with a small blueing color gradient, metal-poor intermediate-age stars, low velocity dispersion, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043325.54-130408.0 (G-12) : This low surface brightness dE is quite regular and with a small gradient in colour changing from an intermediate colour in the centre to a blue one in the outer regions. The poor S/N ratio spectrum still allows us to determine metal-poor intermediate age stellar population. The velocity dispersion is low, as expected for its faint luminosity. No emission lines are present in the residual of the fitting. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 449 kpc and the galaxy is located just at the northern border of the X-ray halo.

gmw_00005087.name: ACO496J043326.49-131717.8 (G-13)
gmw_00005087.overview: ACO496J043326.49-131717.8 (G-13) is a dwarf lenticular galaxy featuring a faint, elongated disc structure [arXiv:0805.0219]. This inclined disc may contain a blue spiral pattern surrounding a red, compact central bulge [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-13 as a dwarf lenticular with a possible blue spiral pattern, red compact central bulge, low velocity dispersion, intermediate age, and low metallicity.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043326.49-131717.8 (G-13) : This dwarf lenticular exhibits a faint elongated structure. This inclined disc may contain a spiral pattern, which appears bluer on some colour maps despite the noise. In the galaxy centre a red, compact, and slightly elongated component could be a small bulge. Its velocity dispersion is quite low, with an intermediate age and a low metallicity. This object has the lowest value of [ Mg/Fe ] of the sample, indicating that subsequent star formation episodes might have occurred in this object.

gmw_00005088.name: ACO496J043329.79-130851.7 (G-14)
gmw_00005088.overview: ACO496J043329.79-130851.7 (G-14) is a faint dwarf elliptical galaxy characterized by a low velocity dispersion [arXiv:0805.0219]. It exhibits a strong color gradient that is redder in the center and bluer toward the outskirts, alongside possible evidence of a complex star formation history [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-14 as a faint centrally concentrated dwarf elliptical with a strong color gradient, low velocity dispersion, and possible evidence for a complex star-formation history.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043329.79-130851.7 (G-14) : This is a faint dE galaxy with no substructure except for a trend to be centrally concentrated. A strong colour gradient is observed, with redder colours in the centre and becoming bluer toward the outskirts. Its velocity dispersion is low. One can notice two minima of ^2 in the age-metallicity space. This can be considered as a hint for a complex star formation history in this object, however, higher signal-to-noise ratio in the spectrum is required to disentangle the two star formation episodes.

gmw_00005089.name: ACO496J043331.48-131654.6 (G-15)
gmw_00005089.overview: ACO496J043331.48-131654.6 (G-15) is a low-luminosity lenticular galaxy that exhibits an inclined Z-shaped spiral pattern [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a moderately old stellar population with sub-solar metallicity, low velocity dispersion, and no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-15 as a low-luminosity lenticular with a prominent inclined Z-shaped spiral pattern, moderately old sub-solar stellar population, no emission lines, and low velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043331.48-131654.6 (G-15) : This low-luminosity lenticular galaxy exhibits prominent Z-shaped structure, revealed by the unsharp masking technique, which is probably a spiral pattern in the strongly inclined stellar disc. The stellar population of the galaxy is moderately old, with sub-solar metallicity and exactly solar [ Mg/Fe ] ratio. No emission lines are detected. The velocity dispersion is low compared to other galaxies of similar luminosities, suggesting that ACO496J043331.48-131654.6 (G-15) may be a stripped spiral or a later-type galaxy with a small bulge (see colour maps). The galaxy is located only 75 kpc in projected distance from the cluster cD, and its radial velocity is close to the cluster redshift.

gmw_00005090.name: ACO496J043332.07-131518.1 (G-16)
gmw_00005090.overview: ACO496J043332.07-131518.1 (G-16) is a low-luminosity elliptical galaxy located in the inner region of its cluster [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a red central structure, an old, metal-poor stellar population, and no emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-16 as an inner-cluster low-luminosity elliptical with a red central structure, old metal-poor enhanced stellar population, moderate velocity dispersion, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043332.07-131518.1 (G-16) : This low-luminosity elliptical galaxy is found in the inner part of the cluster (d _ proj = 54 kpc). It shows a red concentrated structure in the centre. The stellar population is quite old and metal-poor, and [ Mg/Fe ] is super-solar. ACO496J043332.07-131518.1 (G-16) exhibits a moderate velocity dispersion value and no emission lines. Its radial velocity does not differ from the mean cluster redshift.

gmw_00005091.name: ACO496J043333.17-131712.6 (G-17)
gmw_00005091.overview: ACO496J043333.17-131712.6 (G-17) is a low-luminosity elliptical galaxy located in the central dense region of the Abell 496 cluster [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a compact appearance, a high velocity dispersion, and an old, metal-rich stellar population with no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G-17 as an unusual central low-luminosity elliptical whose high velocity dispersion, old metal-rich enhanced stars, and compact appearance distinguish it from similar-luminosity ellipticals.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043333.17-131712.6 (G-17) : This low-luminosity elliptical galaxy belongs to the population of unusual galaxies in the central dense part of the Abell 496 cluster (72 kpc in projected distance). Its stellar population of high velocity dispersion, very old age, metal-rich and enhanced, and compact appearance make it very different from nearby E galaxies of this luminosity. No emission lines are detected. No presence of embedded structures is revealed by the unsharp masking. The central part of the galaxy is relatively red and extended; the stellar population properties correspond to those of a normal bulge as seen in more massive lenticulars.

gmw_00005092.name: ACO496J043333.53-131852.6 (G-18)
gmw_00005092.overview: ACO496J043333.53-131852.6 is a low-luminosity, early-type SB0a galaxy that contains a bar aligned nearly along the minor axis of its external isophotes [arXiv:0805.0219]. The galaxy's bulge and bar are redder than its disc, and its stars exhibit an intermediate age with a nearly solar metallicity [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper classifies G-18 as a low-luminosity SB0a with a prominent bar, distinct bulge-disc colors, intermediate-age near-solar stars, moderate velocity dispersion, and enhanced magnesium abundance.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043333.53-131852.6 (G-18) : This is an early-type galaxy that can be classified as a low-luminosity SB0a with an absolute magnitude that places it slightly above the brightest dE/dS0. Unsharp masking reveals a prominent bar aligned nearly along the minor axis of the external isophotes. The difference in colour between the bulge and disc is clearly seen on all the colour maps, as well as for the bar which is also redder compared to the disc. Its intermediate age, nearly solar metallicity, and moderate velocity dispersion resemble properties of objects of this luminosity in the nearby Universe. However, the [ Mg/Fe ] abundance ratio is somewhat higher than solar, indicating a short period of star formation ( < 1.5 Gyr).

gmw_00005093.name: ACO496J043334.54-131137.1 (G-19)
gmw_00005093.overview: ACO496J043334.54-131137.1 (G-19) is a faint dwarf elliptical galaxy located 156 kpc from its cluster center [arXiv:0805.0219]. It lacks embedded structures, shows no emission lines, and hosts an intermediate-age stellar population with sub-solar metallicity [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-19 as a faint dwarf elliptical lacking embedded structure, with a redder central component, low velocity dispersion, intermediate-age sub-solar stellar population, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043334.54-131137.1 (G-19) : This is a faint dE galaxy showing no evidence for embedded structures but a central component redder than the outer parts, however, not as red as typical bulges already seen in the other brighter galaxies. Its velocity dispersion is low, and its stellar population has an intermediate age and significantly sub-solar metallicity. No emission lines are detected. This galaxy is located quite close to the cluster centre, at a projected distance of 156 kpc.

gmw_00005094.name: ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 (G-20)
gmw_00005094.overview: ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 is a compact elliptical galaxy located in the halo of the central cD galaxy of the Abell 496 cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0703322, arXiv:0805.0219]. It is dominated by an old stellar population [arXiv:astro-ph/0703322, arXiv:0805.0219] and is likely the remnant of an intermediate-luminosity S0 galaxy that underwent severe tidal stripping [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper reports A496cE as a compact elliptical in Abell 496, measuring its two-component light profile, old stellar spectrum, high Mg/Fe, and possible tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: We report here the discovery of A496cE, a M32 twin in the cluster Abell 496, located in the halo of the central cD. Based on CFHT and HST imaging we show that the light profile of A496cE requires a two component fit: a S ersic bulge and an exponential disc. The spectrum of A496cE obtained with the ESO-VLT FLAMES/Giraffe spectrograph can be fit by a stellar synthesis spectrum dominated by old stars, with high values of [ Mg/Fe ] and velocity dispersion. The capture of A496cE by the cD galaxy and tidal stripping of most of its disc are briefly discussed.

sources.summary: The paper describes G-20 as a new M32-like compact elliptical in the cD halo, with high velocity dispersion, old nearly solar-metallicity stars, and likely severe tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 (G-20) : This new M32-like compact elliptical has been extensively discussed by Chilingarian et al. (2007c) and is located in projection on the central cD halo. The central very bright component of this very peculiar dwarf has colours similar to most of the other ellipticals and lenticulars. With its high velocity dispersion for its luminosity, very old stellar population, nearly solar metallicity and mild enhancement, it seems that ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 (G-20) is the disturbed remnant of an intermediate luminosity S0 where most of the disc has been tidally stripped.

gmw_00005095.name: ACO496J043338.22-131500.7 (G-21)
gmw_00005095.overview: ACO496J043338.22-131500.7 (G-21) is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located at a projected distance of approximately 30 kpc from the cluster's central cD galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It exhibits an unusually large velocity dispersion for its small size and hosts a stellar population with a high chemical enhancement [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-21 as a dwarf elliptical near the cD with central colors and ages like G-20, slightly lower metallicity, higher enhancement, and unusually large velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043338.22-131500.7 (G-21) : This dwarf elliptical exhibits very different structural properties from the previous galaxy (A496cE), although it is located only at a projected distance of about 30 kpc of the cD (Chilingarian et al., 2007b). On the other hand, the colours in the central parts of the galaxy seem quite similar in both objects, and the stellar population in ACO496J043338.22-131500.7 (G-21) is only slightly more metal poor than that of ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 (G-20) and with higher enhancement, while their ages are nearly the same. The velocity dispersion of ACO496J043338.22-131500.7 (G-21) is even larger and more unusual for such a small object. If the tidal stripping of a larger disc galaxy could also be invoked to explain the spectral properties, in the present case the remnant structural properties are quite normal compared to bulges.

gmw_00005096.name: ACO496J043339.07-131319.7 (G-22)
gmw_00005096.overview: ACO496J043339.07-131319.7 (G-22) is a low-luminosity, low surface brightness dwarf elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts a relatively old, metal-poor stellar population, shows no detectable emission lines, and may contain a faint nucleus [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-22 as a low-luminosity dwarf elliptical with a possible faint nucleus, noisy color differences, low velocity dispersion, old metal-poor stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043339.07-131319.7 (G-22) : This low-luminosity dwarf elliptical may host a faint nucleus as evidenced by the unsharp mask image. The colour maps are noisy, but indicate some colour difference between the centre and the outer parts. A low velocity dispersion, a relatively old and metal-poor stellar population, and low surface brightness make it a normal dwarf elliptical. No emission lines are detected. Its radial velocity is larger by 900 km s ^ 1 than the mean cluster velocity; it is projected quite close to the cluster centre, at a projected distance of 89 kpc, but its real distance from the centre could be larger.

gmw_00005097.name: ACO496J043339.72-131424.6 (G-23)
gmw_00005097.overview: ACO496J043339.72-131424.6 (G-23) is a faint dwarf elliptical galaxy with a low velocity dispersion [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features an old, metal-poor stellar population, a possibly bluer center, and no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-23 as a faint dwarf elliptical with a possibly bluer center, low velocity dispersion, old metal-poor stars, no emission lines, and no strong reason to place it in the core.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043339.72-131424.6 (G-23) : The centre of this faint dE is blue and could be slightly bluer than the rest of the galaxy despite the noise in the colour maps. Its velocity dispersion is low, and the age of its stellar population is old and metal-poor. No emission lines are detected. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 51 kpc, but there is no obvious reason to put this dE into the cluster core.

gmw_00005098.name: ACO496J043341.69-131551.8 (G-24)
gmw_00005098.overview: ACO496J043341.69-131551.8 is a low-luminosity elliptical galaxy located in the inner region of its cluster [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a very old, highly enhanced stellar population and likely experienced heavy tidal stripping [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper presents G-24 as an unusual inner-cluster low-luminosity elliptical with the sample's highest velocity dispersion and bulge-like old, enhanced stellar population, probably reflecting heavy tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043341.69-131551.8 (G-24) : This very unusual, low-luminosity elliptical, representative of the peculiar galaxy population in the inner part of the cluster, is projected on the outer parts of the cD halo (d _ proj = 36 kpc). This galaxy is the highest velocity dispersion object in our sample. Its velocity dispersion and stellar population with slightly sub-solar metallicity, high enhancement, and very old age are reminiscent of bulges of bright lenticular galaxies with a luminosity. This galaxy is probably a ``scaled-up'' analogue of A496cE (ACO496J043337.35-131520.2 (G-20); Chilingarian et al., 2007b) and the evolutionary scenarii of these two objects are very similar: heavy tidal stripping of a more luminous S0.

gmw_00005099.name: ACO496J043342.10-131653.7 (G-25)
gmw_00005099.overview: ACO496J043342.10-131653.7 (G-25) is a low-luminosity lenticular galaxy located in the inner region of its cluster [arXiv:0805.0219]. Its stellar disc contains faint spiral arms and a bar or ringlike structure, hosting an old, metal-poor stellar population with no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-25 as an inner-cluster low-luminosity lenticular whose color maps reveal a bar or ringlike structure, faint spiral arms, old metal-poor stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043342.10-131653.7 (G-25) : This low-luminosity lenticular galaxy is found in the inner part of the cluster (d _ proj = 60 kpc). Unsharp masking as well as colour maps reveal a bar or ringlike structure and faint spiral arms in the stellar disc. The stellar population is quite old and metal-poor, although [ Mg/Fe ] is super-solar, which is rather uncommon for such a faint S0 galaxy, and its velocity dispersion is average. No emission lines are detected.

gmw_00005100.name: ACO496J043342.13-131258.8 (G-26)
gmw_00005100.overview: ACO496J043342.13-131258.8 (G-26) is a moderate-luminosity, low-surface-brightness galaxy with a large effective radius of 2.8 kiloparsecs [arXiv:0805.0219]. It exhibits a low velocity dispersion and possesses stellar population properties that are normal for its luminosity [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-26 as a moderate-luminosity low-surface-brightness galaxy with the sample's second-largest effective radius, low velocity dispersion, normal stellar population, and a spectral artifact affecting Mg b.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043342.13-131258.8 (G-26) : This galaxy has the second largest effective radius (2.8 kpc) among the sample of early-type objects, while its luminosity is moderate, making it a low surface brightness galaxy. The projected distance to the centre is d _ proj = 109 kpc and the radial velocity is higher by 465 km s ^ 1 than the mean value for the cluster, but this object does not resemble other galaxies populating the central region of the cluster. The velocity dispersion is low and the stellar population properties are normal for its luminosity. There was an artifact in the spectrum around 5320 \ (5140 \ in rest frame) so the Mg b Lick index could not be measured.

gmw_00005101.name: ACO496J043342.83-130846.8 (G-27)
gmw_00005101.overview: ACO496J043342.83-130846.8 is a low-luminosity elliptical or lenticular galaxy located in the outer parts of the Abell 496 cluster's X-ray halo [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a superimposed object near its center, a medium-age stellar population with low metallicity, and no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-27 as a low-luminosity S0/E galaxy with a nearby superimposed object, no color gradient, low velocity dispersion, medium-age low-metallicity stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043342.83-130846.8 (G-27) : This is a quite low-luminosity S0/E galaxy with an object superimposed less than 2 arcsec from its centre but otherwise no obvious substructures. No colour gradient is observed in this galaxy. Its velocity dispersion is quite low, with a medium age of its stellar population, a quite low metallicity and a high value of [ Mg/Fe ] . No emission lines are detected. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 264 kpc and the galaxy is located in the outer parts of the X-ray halo.

gmw_00005102.name: ACO496J043343.04-130514.1 (G-28)
gmw_00005102.overview: ACO496J043343.04-130514.1 is a bright dwarf lenticular galaxy located at a projected distance of 396 kpc from its cluster center [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a redder central region that may be a small bulge, contains old, low-metallicity stars, and shows no emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-28 as a bright dS0 galaxy with no substructure, a red central bulge-like gradient, low velocity dispersion, old low-metallicity stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043343.04-130514.1 (G-28) : This is a bright dS0 galaxy with no substructure. A strong colour gradient is observed: the very central region is redder than the rest of the galaxy and could be a small bulge (marginally resolved in direction of the minor axis). Its velocity dispersion is quite low, and the age of its stellar population is old, with a low metallicity but a high value of [ Mg/Fe ] . No emission lines are detected. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 396 kpc.

gmw_00005103.name: ACO496J043343.04-125924.4 (G-29)
gmw_00005103.overview: ACO496J043343.04-125924.4 is an inclined, faint dwarf lenticular or dwarf elliptical galaxy with no evident embedded substructure [arXiv:0805.0219]. It hosts an old, low-metallicity stellar population, exhibits a weak outward-blueing color gradient, and shows no detectable emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-29 as an inclined faint dS0/dE with no embedded substructure, a weak outward-blueing color gradient, low velocity dispersion, very old low-metallicity stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043343.04-125924.4 (G-29) : This inclined or elongated faint dS0/dE, with no evident embedded substructure, exhibits a weak colour gradient with redder colours in the centre and becoming bluer towards the outskirts. Its velocity dispersion is low, and the age of its stellar population is quite old, with a very low metallicity. No emission lines are detected. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 614 kpc and the galaxy is found in the north, away from the X-ray halo.

gmw_00005104.name: ACO496J043345.67-130542.2 (G-30)
gmw_00005104.overview: ACO496J043345.67-130542.2 (G-30) is a moderately bright dwarf lenticular galaxy located in a northern-border group of the Abell 496 cluster [arXiv:0805.0219]. It features a redder central region indicating a small bulge, a young stellar population with low metallicity, and no detected emission lines [arXiv:0805.0219].

sources.summary: The paper describes G-30 as a moderately bright dS0 in the northern-border group, with a red central bulge-like gradient, small velocity dispersion, young low-metallicity stars, and no emission lines.

quotes.quote: ACO496J043345.67-130542.2 (G-30) : This moderately bright dS0 galaxy with no substructures belongs to the group mentioned in the description of ACO496J043343.04-130514.1 (G-28). A colour gradient is observed, with the central region redder than the rest of the galaxy, indicating a small bulge. Its velocity dispersion is small and the age of its stellar population is young, with a low metallicity and [ Mg/Fe ] ratio. No emission lines are detected. The projected distance to the cluster centre is 382 kpc.

gmw_00005105.name: 4C + 39.23B
gmw_00005105.overview: 4C + 39.23B is a compact radio source tentatively classified as a compact steep spectrum object [arXiv:1510.06763]. It exhibits a double morphology dominated by steep-spectrum radio lobes with a linear size of 200 parsecs [arXiv:1510.06763].

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C + 39.23B as a compact double radio source with steep-spectrum lobes and a possible but unlikely Fermi-LAT association.

quotes.quote: In particular, three objects are tentatively classified as a compact steep spectrum (CSS) object: 4C + 39.23B, 3C 286, and 3C 380. 4C + 39.23B is a compact radio source with a double morphology dominated by steep-spectrum lobes, with a linear size of 200 pc (Fig. 4c39 ; Orienti et al. 2004). In the 3LAC the source may be associated with the ray source 3FGL J0824.9 + 3916. However, the non-unique association of the ray source (3FGL J0824.9 + 3916 is also associated with the flat spectrum radio quasar 4C + 39.23) makes its CSO counterpart unlikely.

gmw_00005106.name: CFHQ J232908 030158
gmw_00005106.overview: CFHQ J232908 030158 is a radio-quiet quasar at redshift z=6.417 [arXiv:1109.4110, arXiv:1104.1636] that is embedded in a luminous, extended Lyman-alpha halo [arXiv:1109.4110, arXiv:1809.10916]. The host galaxy exhibits very low star formation [arXiv:1302.1587] and may reside within a protocluster of companion star-forming galaxies [arXiv:1109.4110].

sources.summary: The paper presents CFHQS J2329-0301 as a z=6.417 radio-quiet quasar with an extended luminous Ly-alpha halo, complex kinematics, and probable quasar-photoionized cold gas.

quotes.quote: We present long-slit spectroscopic data which reveals extended \ emission around the z=6.417 radio-quiet quasar CFHQS J2329-0301. The \ emission is extended over 15 kpc and has a luminosity of > 8 10^ 36 W, comparable to the most luminous \ halos known. The emission has complex kinematics, in part due to foreground absorption which only partly covers the extended nebula. The velocity ranges from 500 km s ^ 1 to +500 km s ^ 1 , with a peak remarkably close to the systemic velocity identified by broad \ emission of the quasar. There is no evidence for infall or outflow of the halo gas.

CFHQS J232908-030158 (hereafter CFHQS J2329-0301) is a quasar at z=6.417 discovered in the Canada-France High-z Quasar Survey (CFHQS; Willott et al. 2007). It is radio-quiet (Wang et al. 2008) and powered by a black hole with mass 2 10^8 M_ (Willott et al. 2010). There is some evidence for a protocluster of companion star-forming galaxies based on deep multi-color imaging (Utsumi et al. 2010).

sources.summary: CFHQS J2329 0301 is a z=6.417 quasar embedded in an extended halo; new ALMA Cycle 3 data detect weak continuum and line emission from it.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J2329 0301 CFHQS J2329 0301 is a z=6.417 quasar based on the \ redshift with black hole mass M_ BH = 2.5 10^8 M_ . It is embedded in a luminous \ halo extending over 15 kpc Goto:2009,Willott:2011 . In Cycle 0 we made ALMA observations that did not detect either line or continuum reaching limits quoted as I _ CII <0.10 Jy km s ^ 1 and f_ <90 Jy Willott:2013 . Weak \ emission at a significance level insufficient to be called a secure detection was noted in the Cycle 0 data.

Inspection of the \ data cube and continuum map both show weak emission at a position 0.5 arcsec north of the expected position. However this expected position is based on optical data with an astrometric uncertainty of 0.5 arcsec. As noted in there is a lower redshift galaxy 7 arcsec north of the quasar detected in the optical and ALMA continuum. The spatial offset between this galaxy and the weak continuum emission matches that between this galaxy and the optical quasar thereby proving that the \ and continuum detections are indeed from the quasar.

sources.summary: The paper observes QSO CFHQSJ2329-0301 at z=6.4, emphasizing its faintness, corrected redshift, small Stromgren sphere, and usefulness for probing high-redshift neutral hydrogen absorption.

quotes.quote: We investigate neutral hydrogen absorption in a deep, moderate-resolution Keck/Deimos spectrum of QSO CFHQSJ2329-0301 at z=6.4. This QSO is one of the highest redshift QSOs presently known at z=6.4 but is 2.5 mag fainter than a previously well-studied QSO SDSSJ1148+5251 at z=6.4. Therefore, it has a smaller Stromgren sphere, and allows us to probe the highest redshift hydrogen absorption to date.

Our target is a QSO CFHQSJ2329-0301 W07 hereafter 2007AJ....134.2435W . Initially the redshift was reported to be z=6.43 based on Ly (W07). The redshift was later corrected to be z=6.417 based on the Mg II line 2010AJ....140..546W because blueward of the Ly emission line suffers from heavy absorption from neutral hydrogen.

sources.summary: ALMA does not detect J2329-0301 in continuum or [CII], implying very low star formation relative to black-hole accretion and possible feedback-suppressed star formation.

quotes.quote: J2329-0301 is undetected with L_ FIR <1.9 10^ 11 L _ ( 3 limit). We note the incredible sensitivity of these early ALMA observations that reach the lower end of the LIRG classification in the early universe at z=6.4 .

The very low SFR implied for the host galaxy of J2329-0301 is surprising given that it has a 2.5 10^ 8 M _ black hole accreting at the Eddington rate (Willott et al. 2010b) and a very luminous, spatially-extended \ halo (Goto et al. 2009; Willott et al. 2011).

This suggests that J2329-0301 is observed at a rare phase where it has very low SFR compared to its black hole accretion rate. In the Khandai et al. (2012) simulations, the SFR usually drops at the epoch of peak quasar accretion due to feedback heating the host galaxy gas. J2329-0301 appears to have very effectively shut off star formation.

sources.summary: The paper presents Subaru narrow-band imaging of CFHQ J232908 030158, confirming a luminous 37 pkpc Ly-alpha halo that is brighter and more extended than previous measurements.

quotes.quote: We first present new Subaru narrow-band observations of the halo around the quasi-stellar object (QSO) CFHQ J232908 030158 at z=6.42 , which appears the most luminous and extended halo at z>5 ( L_ Ly =9.8 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 within 37 pkpc diameter).

The luminosity and spatial extent of 's halo We find that the emission of \ is more extended than the PSF (Figure fig:radip (a)) as well as than the continuum emission (Figure fig:img ), thus confirming the presence of a halo. We use the SB profile (see also Figure fig:radip ) to measure the spatial extent of the halo, i.e., the largest diameter where the SB is above the SB limit, to be 6 8 , or 37 pkpc. The total luminosity within an annulus of radii from 1 to 3 4 is 9.8 2.3 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 .
The halo found in this study is a factor of >1.7 more extended, and a factor of >1.9 more luminous than those obtained by the previous studies.

sources.summary: The paper observes the z=6.4 quasar field CFHQS J2329 0301 with Subaru/Suprime-Cam and uses the quasar's redshift and luminosity while assessing nearby high-redshift galaxy formation.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we report our NB906 observation of the QSO field centred on CFHQS J2329 0301 Table tab:targets ; 2007AJ....134.2435W at z=6.417 2010AJ....140..546W .
Using this NB906 filter, we observed a field centred on QSO CFHQS J2329 0301 at z=6.4 with Subaru Suprime-Cam.
QSO CFHQS J2329 0301's absolute magnitude is M_ 1450 =-25.2 2007AJ....134.2435W , which is about 1.2 magnitude fainter that in K07 at z=4.87 ( M_ 1450 =-26.4).

gmw_00005107.name: UGC 2165
gmw_00005107.overview: UGC 2165 is a dwarf galaxy characterized by extremely red ultraviolet colors and very faint far-ultraviolet emission [arXiv:1204.3472]. This faint emission may indicate a very old stellar population, and the galaxy exhibits a very low star-formation rate [arXiv:1204.3472].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 2165 as a dwarf with extremely red ultraviolet colors, very faint FUV emission, and the lowest inferred star-formation rate in the group.

quotes.quote: UGC 2165 on the contrary is very red, in particular the FUV flux measured can be considered as an upper limit and may indicate a very old stellar population.
The SFR in the other dwarf galaxies and in the two blobs (C1 and C2) is even lower, with the lowest value, 0.01 10^ 2 M _ yr ^ 1 , for UGC 2165.
In particular UGC 2165 is very faint in FUV showing extremely red FUV-NUV colors.

gmw_00005108.name: PKS 2208-137
gmw_00005108.overview: PKS 2208-137 is a low-redshift quasar located at a redshift below 0.4 [arXiv:2308.06080]. Due to its spectral position, measurements of its doublet feature are affected by high uncertainty [arXiv:2308.06080].

sources.summary: PKS 2208-137 lacks FIRST imaging, uses NVSS morphology, has uncertain low-redshift doublet measurements, and shows a slightly sub-4000 FWHM attributed to narrow-component identification.

quotes.quote: In addition, for two sources (B2 0110+29 and PKS 2208-137) that do not have the FIRST cutout image, we present NVSS radio maps, that we used for the morphological classification.

For the low redshift quasars, with z < 0.4 (B2 0110+29 and PKS 2208-137), the \ doublet measures are affected by a higher uncertainty because it is located at the extreme blue edge of the observed spectrum, which could imply usually more noise and a worse determination of the blue continuum level.

In two cases (3C 179 and PKS 2208-137), the FWHM is slightly less than 4000 . For PKS 2208-137, the barely lower value may be caused by the uncertainty in identifying the NC, which may have been included in the BC producing a slightly narrower and sharper BC profile.

gmw_00005109.name: PHL 923
gmw_00005109.overview: PHL 923 is a quasar that exhibits a complex optical spectrum [arXiv:2308.06080]. It is characterized by an exceptionally high line-ratio value that places the object in the B2 bin [arXiv:2308.06080].

sources.summary: PHL 923 has supplemental FIRST/Pan-STARRS imaging, a complex spectrum requiring an object-specific fitting range, and the sample's exceptional higher line-ratio value.

quotes.quote: In Appendix A of the online supplemental material, we included the FIRST images and their overlay on the optical Pan-STARRS image for PHL 923, 3C 37, PKS 0230-051, 3C 94, and PKS 2344+09.

The fitting for this region was done in a different wavelength range (see Table tab:meashb Col. 2) for each object to account for the complex nature observed in some of the spectra, like PHL 923 and 3C 94.
Our quasars also show weak \ intensities. All except one case (PHL 923 with \ = 0.63 that locates the object in B2 bin) present low \ values, with a mean of 0.29 and a = 0.19.

gmw_00005110.name: PKS 2344+09
gmw_00005110.overview: PKS 2344+09 is a quasar that has been imaged using FIRST and Pan-STARRS data [arXiv:2308.06080]. Spectral analysis of the source reveals a narrow component that appears slightly blueshifted [arXiv:2308.06080].

sources.summary: PKS 2344+09 appears in the supplemental imaging set, is an exception in the line-contribution fitting, and has a slightly blueshifted narrow component.

quotes.quote: In Appendix A of the online supplemental material, we included the FIRST images and their overlay on the optical Pan-STARRS image for PHL 923, 3C 37, PKS 0230-051, 3C 94, and PKS 2344+09.
In all sources except PHL 923, 3C 94, and PKS 2344+09, the \ line contributes to the blue wing of , and we constrained the shift and FWHM of the \ VBC to be the same as \ VBC 2007ApJ...669..126S .
In 3C 94 and PKS 2344+09 the NC appears slightly blueshifted.

gmw_00005111.name: B2 0110+29
gmw_00005111.overview: B2 0110+29 is a low-redshift quasar located at a redshift of less than 0.4 [arXiv:2308.06080]. The galaxy exhibits extremely faint emission lines with high measurement uncertainty due to noise at the blue edge of its observed spectrum [arXiv:2308.06080].

sources.summary: B2 0110+29 lacks FIRST imaging, is classified with NVSS maps, has uncertain low-redshift doublet measurements, and shows extremely faint emission with larger uncertainty.

quotes.quote: In addition, for two sources (B2 0110+29 and PKS 2208-137) that do not have the FIRST cutout image, we present NVSS radio maps, that we used for the morphological classification.

For the low redshift quasars, with z < 0.4 (B2 0110+29 and PKS 2208-137), the \ doublet measures are affected by a higher uncertainty because it is located at the extreme blue edge of the observed spectrum, which could imply usually more noise and a worse determination of the blue continuum level.
Considering \ emission, the uncertainty ranges between 20 \ and 50 \ at 1 confidence level, the latter for quasars with extremely faint \ emission like B2 0110+29.

gmw_00005112.name: NGC 2207
gmw_00005112.overview: NGC 2207 is an interacting spiral galaxy that partially overlaps its companion, IC 2163 [arXiv:1507.06604, arXiv:1609.04444]. The galaxy's dust is concentrated primarily within its spiral arms [arXiv:1507.06604], and the system has hosted multiple supernovae since 1975 [arXiv:1609.04444].

sources.summary: The catalogue identifies NGC 2207 as an interacting-galaxy host with an unusually large ULX-candidate count, discusses its pair with IC 2163, and explains the adopted distance choice.

quotes.quote: The most extreme example is NGC 2207 -- one half of an interacting galaxy pair (the other being IC 2163; Eskridge02 ) -- which appears to host an astonishing 34 ULX candidates, the majority of which (31) are contributed by the CSC2 catalogue. This is notably larger than the 21 ULXs reported to reside in NGC 2207/IC 2163 by , likely due to additional \ observations being included in CSC2 and our explicit consideration of long-term variability in selecting our ULX sample. Owing to the interacting nature of these galaxies, it is not surprising that there should be a large number of ULXs. It is nevertheless worth noting that there seems to be some disagreement over the distance to NGC 2207 in the literature.

The distance we have adopted here is D = 36.4 Mpc, which is based on the recession velocity reported in HyperLEDA. This distance is very similar to that reported based on the supernova SN1975A which occurred in NGC 2207 ( D = 39.6 Mpc; Arnett82 ), which is adopted by . However, the more recent estimates from the Tully-Fisher method typically seem to imply a distance of D 17 Mpc ( Russell02, Theureau07 ). Should this be correct, only 7 of our sources in NGC 2207 would still be considered ULXs.

sources.summary: The review uses NGC 2207 as an overlapping interacting spiral example, describing measured arm and interarm extinction curves, dust concentrated in spiral arms, and patchy dust as a proposed explanation.

quotes.quote: Using this technique, Berlind et al. \ (1997) measured the extinction curves in the BVRJHK visual to near-IR wavelength range for the dust in the spiral arm and interarm regions of NGC 2207 which overlaps IC 2163. This pair consists of two symmetric, almost face-on interacting spiral galaxies which are partially overlapping. These galaxies have been observed and modeled in detail by D. Elmegreen et al. \ (1995) and B. Elmegreen et al. \ (1995). Berlind et al. \ (1997) found that the dust in NGC 2207 is mainly concentrated in its spiral arms, leaving its interarm regions mostly transparent. More interestingly, they found that the extinction curve in the spiral arm region of NGC 2207 is flat, resembling the MW extinction curve for 5.0 , while the interarm dust appears to be even ``grayer'' (see Figure 10).

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2207 as IC 2163's interacting late-type companion observed by Spitzer; its foreground spiral arm may add extinction, and the pair has hosted multiple supernovae since 1975.

quotes.quote: As part of the regular SPIRITS observing program, the interacting pair of late-type galaxies IC 2163 and NGC 2207 was observed at 10 epochs between 2014 January 13.9 and 2016 January 12.1 using Spitzer /IRAC at [ 3.6 ] and [ 4.5 ] .
There is most likely additional extinction from the foreground spiral arm of NGC 2207.
The rate of SNe associated with this galaxy pair is also high, with 5 known SNe hosted in IC 2163 and NGC 2207 since 1975.

gmw_00005113.name: IC 781
gmw_00005113.overview: IC 781 is a nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy located at a distance of approximately 24.82 Mpc [arXiv:0812.3272, arXiv:2404.10059]. The galaxy hosts a probable disc structure and shows no statistical difference in stellar population properties between its nucleus and main body [arXiv:0812.3272].

sources.summary: VCC 389 is a nucleated dE galaxy with a reported probable disc; the two CARELEC datasets agree, but show no nuclear-body stellar-population difference or metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: VCC 389 = IC 781 (Fig figvcc0389 ). This nucleated dE galaxy hosting a ``probable disc'' reported by was observed twice with the CARELEC spectrograph at slightly different positional angles. Both datasets have quite low signal-to-noise ratios, however the derived profiles of the stellar population parameters along the radius agree well. We detected neither statistically significant difference of the stellar population properties in the nucleus and in the main galactic body, nor metallicity gradient.

sources.summary: The paper notes IC 781 as a dwarf galaxy in the NGC 4262 field, gives its distance, estimates its globular-cluster extent, and removes nearby GCs from the main sample.

quotes.quote: We observed a dwarf galaxy, IC 781, in the field at a distance of 12.2 from the center of NGC 4262. We also found that IC 781 has a distance of 24.82 +/- 1.77 Mpc 2014Toloba . Using the relationship of galaxy effective radius and spatial GC extent from sreeja2014 , we have roughly estimated the GC extent of IC 781 as 2 . Then, we removed the corresponding GCs around IC 781 inside 2 from the main sample and accounted for the respective area correction.

gmw_00005114.name: VCC 510
gmw_00005114.overview: VCC 510 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1401.7336]. It is the smallest and faintest known galaxy to host a counter-rotating core [arXiv:astro-ph/0511409], which likely originated from gas accretion or a galaxy merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0511409].

sources.summary: The paper presents VCC 510 as the faintest, smallest known galaxy with a counter-rotating core, with anisotropic support, old sub-solar stellar population, possible central dark matter, and an origin likely involving gas accretion or merging.

quotes.quote: VCC 510 ( M_B 15.7 ) is therefore by far the faintest and smallest galaxy with a counter-rotating core known. From the main body rotation and the velocity dispersion profile we deduce that VCC 510 is anisotropic and clearly not entirely supported by rotation. We derive an old luminosity-weighted age ( 10 3 Gyr) and sub-solar metallicity ( [ ] =-0.4 0.1 ) inside the effective radius.

From the stellar population parameters we obtain a total stellar mass-to-light ratio of 3.6 (M/L_B)_ which is significantly lower than a rough dynamical estimate obtained from the kinematics through the virial theorem ( 15 ). This discrepancy hints toward the possible presence of dark matter in the centre of VCC 510. We discuss the origin of the counter-rotating core and exclude fly-by encounters as a viable possibility. Gas accretion or galaxy merging provide more likely explanations. VCC 510 is therefore the direct observational evidence that such processes do occur in cluster satellite galaxies on dwarf galaxy scales.

sources.summary: The paper discusses VCC 510 as a Virgo dwarf with a literature KDC and nearby projected companions whose radial velocities suggest possible infall as a group.

quotes.quote: VCC 510 VCC 510, with a radial velocity of 838 km s ^ 1 , is at a close projected distance to some galaxies in Virgo radial velocities from unless otherwise indicated GOLDMine . VCC 490 is a dE located at 33.1 kpc ( 6.9' ) in projection from VCC 510 and has a velocity of 1267 km s ^ 1 .

Finally, VCC 596 (M100) is a Sc spiral at 86.2 kpc ( 18.1' ) from VCC 510 with a radial velocity of 1575 km s ^ 1 . All these galaxies, but for VCC 559 and probably also VCC 596, have radial velocities that are close enough to that of VCC 510 to suggest that they are part of a group that fell into the Virgo cluster.

Combined with the KDCs found in VCC 510 by Thomas06 , VCC 917 and VCC 1261 by Chil09 , and in VCC 1475 by Koleva11 , this makes a total of six dEs (defined as early-type galaxies in the luminosity range 19 < M_r < -16 ) with a significant or marginal detection of a KDC in the Virgo cluster.

gmw_00005115.name: LESSJ0333243.6-274644
gmw_00005115.overview: LESSJ0333243.6-274644 is a bright submillimeter galaxy detected in the Hubble Ultra Deep Field [arXiv:1301.3155]. It lacks an optical counterpart in optical catalogs, which suggests that the source is likely optically thick [arXiv:1301.3155].

sources.summary: The paper treats LESSJ0333243.6-274644 as the only known bright submillimeter source in the UDF, absent from its optical catalog, likely optically thick, and omitted from optically based predictions.

quotes.quote: This is the case of LESSJ0333243.6-274644, the only sub-mm source detected in the UDF as part of the LESS survey (LABOCA observations of the Extended Chandra Deep Field South at 870 ; Weiss2009 ), which has no optical counterpart in our optical catalog.

For example, LESSJ0333243.6-274644, the only sub-mm source detected in the Hubble UDF as part of the LESS survey (LABOCA observations of the Extended Chandra Deep Field South), with a flux density of 6.4 mJy at 870 \ Weiss2009 , has no optical counterpart in our catalog, presumably because it is an optically-thick sub-mm galaxy (SMG; cf. Dunlop2011 ).
We note that this figure is based solely on our optically-based predictions, and so they are missing the only known bright (sub-)mm source in the UDF: LESSJ0333243.6-274644.

gmw_00005116.name: J1615+5452
gmw_00005116.overview: J1615+5452 is a remnant radio active galactic nucleus hosted by an early-type elliptical galaxy at a redshift of approximately 0.33 [arXiv:2006.10028]. The source exhibits 100 kpc of diffuse radio emission but lacks a compact core, jets, or hotspots, indicating a dying active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2006.10028].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1615+5452 as a remnant radio AGN hosted by an early-type elliptical at z 0.33, with 100 kpc diffuse radio emission, no compact core, jets, or hotspots, and spectral properties indicating a dying radio AGN.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a remnant radio AGN J1615+5452 in the field of ELAIS-N1. GMRT continuum observations at 150, 325 and 610 MHz combined with archival data from the 1.4 GHz NVSS survey were used to derive the radio spectrum of the source. At a redshift z 0.33, J1615+5452 has a linear size of 100 kpc and spectral indices ranging between ^ 1400 _ 610 < -1.5 and ^ 325 _ 150 = -0.61 0.12 . While the source has a diffuse radio emission at low frequencies, we do not find evidence of core, jets or hotspots in the 1.4 GHz VLA data of 5 arcsec angular resolution. Such morphological properties coupled with a curved radio spectrum suggest that the AGN fueling mechanisms undergo a shortage of energy supply which is typical of a dying radio AGN.

The candidate remnant AGN is likely associated with an early-type elliptical galaxy at redshift of z 0.33 . The potential host galaxy appears to reside in a low-density environment. With an angular extent of 100 kpc, J1615+5452 has a diffuse amorphous radio emission with no evidence of compact core, jets and hotspots.

gmw_00005117.name: AT J0642.9 5118
gmw_00005117.overview: AT J0642.9 5118 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus that exhibits persistent radio variability [arXiv:1611.05580]. Its host system, G2, is tentatively identified as an early-type galaxy at a redshift of approximately 0.805 [arXiv:1611.05580].

sources.summary: The paper identifies AT J0642.9 5118 as a variable radio source with a post-FRB flare and interprets it as compact emission from a radio-loud AGN.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The location of the source on the sky is (J2000) = 6^ h 42^ m 57^ s .154(3), =-51^ 18^ ' 17^ " .70(7) .

quotes.quote: Within the field of view, we have identified a strongly variable source, which we refer to as AT J0642.9 5118. The location of the source on the sky is (J2000) = 6^ h 42^ m 57^ s .154(3), =-51^ 18^ ' 17^ " .70(7) . The light curve for the source is presented in Figure fig:light_curve . In the week after the occurrence of FRB 131104, the source brightens by a factor of 2 , exceeding 1200 Jy in the 7.5 GHz band. During the brightening, the spectrum also inverts.

We interpret the variable radio source AT J0642.9 5118 as emission from compact components in a radio-loud AGN. This is evidenced by the persistent radio variability on timescales of days to months, the optical to mid-infrared colors of its host system, and its possible spectral identification. The lightcurve of the flare following FRB 131104, with the spectrum inverting when it brightens, is consistent with the classic picture of an expanding and cooling synchrotron bubble.

sources.summary: The paper identifies G2 as the south-eastern source in a close double, measures optical and near-infrared magnitudes, and tentatively associates it with an early-type galaxy spectrum at z=0.805.

quotes.quote: We term this source G1, and its south-eastern companion G2. As we did not observe a photometric standard field, we used the GMOS-South photometric equation defined online https://www.gemini.edu/sciops/instruments/gmos/calibration/photometric-stds to set the flux scale. We modeled the point-spread function using nearby stars and used this to model G1 and G2 as two point sources, finding a satisfactory fit to the observation. For G1, we obtained AB magnitudes of g'=22.82 0.02 and '=22.51 0.02 , and for G2 we obtained g'=22.87 0.02 and '=21.77 0.01 .
Using the same technique as above, we measure AB magnitudes of J=21.54 0.03 for G1, and J=20.24 0.01 for G2.

We tentatively identify two redshifted systems in this spectrum: one at z=0.805 0.001 and one at z=0.8875 0.001 . The first system is consistent with the spectra of early-type galaxies swire , a hypothesis which is additionally evidenced by a possible spectral break in the continuum around 4000 . We thus interpret it as corresponding to G2.

gmw_00005118.name: Q1218+472
