All of the galaxies have very compact light profiles. J1558+39, J1613+28, J1713+28, and J2118+00 appear to be in the midst of nuclear coalescence and have not relaxed yet.

gmw_00005213.name: J1713+28
gmw_00005213.overview: J1713+28 is a merger system featuring two distinct, bright cores [arXiv:1404.0677]. The galaxy is in the midst of nuclear coalescence [arXiv:1404.0677] and hosts a Type II active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1404.0677, arXiv:2106.02366].

sources.summary: J1713+28 is a candidate Type II AGN and merger system with two bright cores, high [O III]/H ratios, and signs of incomplete nuclear coalescence.

quotes.quote: For the two galaxies with two distinct, clearly associated, bright cores, in the process of major or minor mergers, J1506+61 and J1713+28, we fit each core with a S e rsic profile. To accommodate the bright, compact cores of our galaxies, we also tried fitting each core with a S e rsic + PSF + sky model (again, all sky model parameters were frozen). In the case of the two galaxies with two distinct, clearly associated, bright cores, J1506+61 and J1713+28, we only added a PSF to the secondary core if there is a noticeable improvement to the fit (relatively large change in ^2/ ) and if the best-fit PSF magnitude was non-negligible (the PSF magnitude had to be 1 of the S e rsic magnitude).

Based on the strength of the high-excitation emission lines, [ O III ] and [ Ne V ] , we consider the following three galaxies as candidate Type II AGN: J1104+59 (ID 3), J1506+54 (ID 4), and J1713+28 (ID 8). Both J1104+59 and J1713+28 have high [ O III ] /H ratios relative to our purely star forming comparison sample (Fig. fig:bpt ). However, the line ratios could still be consistent with star formation given the unusual physical conditions in the galaxies kewley13a,kewley13b .
All of the galaxies have very compact light profiles. J1558+39, J1613+28, J1713+28, and J2118+00 appear to be in the midst of nuclear coalescence and have not relaxed yet.

sources.summary: The paper concludes that J1713 contains a type II AGN, based on its BPT position, ionization and abundance diagnostics, and overlap with the SDSS AGN locus.

quotes.quote: sel14 classified J1713 as the most likely galaxy in their sample to host a type II AGN based on pseudo-BPT diagrams (e.g. [ O 3 ] /H vs [ O 2 ] /H ), and estimated a bolometric Eddington fraction of L _ bol /L _ Edd 0.02-0.13 . The new spectroscopic data and resulting line ratios for this galaxy lead to the same conclusion (see Fig fig:BPT_TotNar ), as this galaxy does not lie in the composite region but is clearly in the AGN region of the BPT diagrams. Moreover, J1713 is distinct from the rest of our sample in the ionization and abundance diagnostics plots (Fig. fig:O32_plot ) and overlaps the SDSS AGN locus in these spaces. We therefore conclude that this source does contain an AGN.

gmw_00005214.name: J2118+00
gmw_00005214.overview: J2118+00 is a compact, massive merger in the midst of unrelaxed nuclear coalescence [arXiv:1404.0677, arXiv:1910.13507]. The galaxy hosts an intense starburst that drives a large, multiphase bipolar outflow extending into the circumgalactic medium [arXiv:1910.13507, arXiv:2106.02366].

sources.summary: J2118+00 has faint Chandra emission consistent with intense star formation, tidal debris, possible near-resolution dual nuclei, and an unrelaxed nuclear-coalescence morphology.

quotes.quote: For the galaxies with 4-count X-ray detections (J1506+54, J1613+28, J2118+00), the IR-based SFRs from restframe WISE data are 250, 230, and 130 M _ yr ^ 1 (see Table table:other_info and section:WISE for details). The predicted X-ray luminosities from star formation for these three galaxies are (L_X( erg s ^ 1 )) = 41.9, 41.9, and 41.6, respectively. Given that these numbers agree with the observations within the error bars and that the spectra are relatively soft (see section:data_red_Chandra ), we conclude that the X-rays from all of the galaxies except the two Type I AGN (J1359+51 and J1634+46) are likely from intense starbursts, not heavily obscured SMBHs.

Five of the nine narrow-line galaxies have PSF fractions in the range of 40 -- 60 , while the remaing four have PSF fractions less than 20 . Notably, galaxies in this later group show indications of having not reached complete nuclear coalescence including double nuclei (J1713+28), elliptical inner light profiles with large GALFIT residuals that may be consistent with dual nuclei slightly below the resolution limit (J1613+28 and J2118+00), and very bright inner tidal features (J1558+39, J1713+28, and J2118+00).
All of the galaxies have very compact light profiles. J1558+39, J1613+28, J1713+28, and J2118+00 appear to be in the midst of nuclear coalescence and have not relaxed yet.

sources.summary: The paper presents Makani as a compact massive merger whose starburst-driven multiphase wind reaches the circumgalactic medium through an 80 by 100 kpc ionized outflow.

quotes.quote: Here we report optical integral field observations of the massive but compact galaxy SDSS J211824.06 + 001729.4. The oxygen \ lines at wavelengths of 3726 and 3729 angstroms reveal an ionized outflow spanning 80 by 100 square kiloparsecs, depositing metal-enriched gas at 10,000 kelvin through an hourglass-shaped nebula that resembles an evacuated and limb-brightened bipolar bubble. We also observe neutral gas phases at temperatures of less than 10,000 kelvin reaching distances of 20 kiloparsecs and velocities of around 1,500 kilometres per second.

The galaxy J211824.06 + 001729.4 that we study here, which we call Makani (Hawai'ian for `wind'), is an example of a merger of two galaxies hosting a galactic wind thought to be powered by extreme star-formation surface density 2012ApJ...755L..26D . At redshift z = 0.459 , Makani is a compact but massive galaxy, with log (M_ / ) = 11.1( 0.2 ), where M_ and \ are the stellar and solar masses, respectively (Extended Data Fig. 4). Our Hubble Space Telescope imaging analysis reveals a highly peaked stellar core (radius 400 pc) framed by two tidal tails of 10--15 kpc so that half of the galaxy's light extends to about 2.5 kpc (ref. 2014MNRAS.441.3417S ; Fig. 1).

sources.summary: The paper describes J2118, or Makani, as a compact starburst whose bipolar outflows reach the CGM and match two recent starburst episodes.

quotes.quote: Our team followed up one of these starburst galaxies (J2118, or Makani) with Keck Cosmic Web Imager KCWI; mor18 . The data reveal a spectacular galactic outflow traced by [ O 2 ] emission line, reaching far into the circumgalactic medium (CGM) of the galaxy rup19 . The [ O 2 ] emission has a classic bipolar hourglass limb-brightened shape, and exhibits a complex structure: a larger-scale, slower outflow ( 300 ) and a smaller-scale, faster outflow ( 1500 ).

The velocities and sizes of these two outflows map exactly to two previous starburst episodes that this galaxy experienced, detected through the rest-frame optical emission and inferred ages of stars in this galaxy. These outflows are therefore consistent with being formed during recent starburst episodes in this galaxy's past. The KCWI data on Makani directly shows that galactic outflows feed the CGM, expelling gas far beyond the stars in galaxies.

gmw_00005215.name: J1359+51
gmw_00005215.overview: J1359+51 is a bright Type I active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1404.0677]. It is detected in X-rays by Chandra and radiates at an estimated one to seven percent of its Eddington luminosity [arXiv:1404.0677].

sources.summary: J1359+51 is one of the paper's bright Type I AGN, X-ray detected by Chandra and inferred to radiate at a few percent of Eddington.

quotes.quote: However, only two galaxies are clearly bright, Type I AGN: J1359+51 and J1634+46, which are detected with 97 and 67 counts, respectively.

We detect J1359+51 and J1634+46 in X-rays, while J2140+12 is undetected (see section:data_red_Chandra for details.) We use the optical continuum light and X-rays separately to estimate the AGN bolometric luminosities. We use the luminosity-dependent bolometric corrections of trakhtenbrot12 for the optical and for the X-rays. We calculate Eddington luminosities using L_ Edd = (1.26 10^ 38 ) (M_ SMBH /M_ ) . We find ratios of 1 -- 7 of Eddington for these galaxies, which are typical for samples of broad-line AGN, but lower than typical SDSS quasars kelly10,steinhardt10,kelly13 .

gmw_00005216.name: J1634+46
gmw_00005216.overview: J1634+46 is a bright Type I active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1404.0677]. The system is a radio-loud source that exhibits tidal tails and other debris indicative of a recent merger [arXiv:1404.0677].

sources.summary: J1634+46 is a clearly bright Type I AGN detected by Chandra, radio-loud at FIRST, and one of the systems with tidal debris.

quotes.quote: However, only two galaxies are clearly bright, Type I AGN: J1359+51 and J1634+46, which are detected with 97 and 67 counts, respectively.
We only detected one galaxy (J1634+46, one of the Type I AGN) at the same flux level as FIRST. This galaxy is clearly a radio-loud AGN (see Table table:other_info ).

Tidal tails and other debris indicative of a recent major or minor merger are evident in two-thirds of the sample (J0826+43, J1104+59, J1506+54, J1558+39, J1613+28, J1713+28, J2118+00, and J1634+46; see Figs. fig:cutouts and fig:smoothed_images and Fig. fig:data_fits in the Appendix).

gmw_00005217.name: J2140+12
gmw_00005217.overview: J2140+12 is a Type 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1404.0677, arXiv:2102.11287] that lacks an X-ray detection [arXiv:1404.0677, arXiv:2102.11287]. This galaxy hosts a slow magnesium II outflow [arXiv:1404.0677] and exhibits a weak broad-line component [arXiv:2102.11287].

sources.summary: J2140+12 is a broad-line AGN that lacks X-ray detection, likely has partial nuclear obscuration, and is the only broad-line AGN with a detected slow Mg II outflow.

quotes.quote: However, three of the 12 galaxies (J1359+51, J1634+46, and J2140+12) display broad Mg II emission indicative of a Type I AGN.

One surprising result is that J2140+12 is not X-ray-detected, yielding a L_ bol ^ X /L_ Edd \ 3 upper limit for this broad-line AGN 2 lower than L_ bol ^ 3000 /L_ Edd . This could be explained by either variability between the time of the optical and X-ray observations, which is causally possible on these timescales e.g., ulrich97,mchardy13 , or by considerable differential attenuation by the large amounts of cold gas that could still be present shortly after a galaxy merger. There could be X-ray absorption from dust-free gas inside the dust sublimation radius maiolino07 . In addition, since the X-rays are likely concentrated much more centrally than the Mg II 2796,2804 broad-line emission and the UV continuum (e.g., in the case of a simple, disk blackbody model), a couple of high-density, AU-scale gas clouds could more easily obscure the X-ray emitting region as compared to the UV emitting BLR. This could also explain the strong absorption dips seen in the broad Mg II line (see Fig. fig:MMT_spectra ).
Only one of the three unequivocal (broad-line) AGN has a detected outflow (J2140+12); this galaxy has one of the slowest outflows in the sample ( v_ avg = -490 km s ^ 1 , v_ max = -950 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes J2140+1209 as the sample galaxy with the slowest outflow, an X-ray upper limit, and a weak broad line indicating a subdominant type 1 AGN component.

quotes.quote: There are two galaxies with weak X-ray detections (J1506+5402 and J1613+2834, 4 X-ray counts each) from sel14 that indicate L_X 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 and four galaxies with X-ray upper limits (J0826+4305, J0944+0930, J1558+3957, J2140+1209) that imply L_X<10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . All of these galaxies are consistent with the relationship between X-ray luminosity and mid-infrared luminosity for starburst galaxies asm11,min14,sel14 .

The galaxy with the slowest outflow velocity in the sample (J2140+1209) has a weak broad \ line, which is a clear indication of a type 1 AGN component, and spectral modeling suggests a 20 AGN continuum contribution at =2800 \ sel14 ; the other 11/12 galaxies show no evidence for any AGN continuum contributions at near-ultraviolet or optical wavelengths on the basis of high signal-to-noise spectroscopy (as described above and shown in Figure fig:spectrum , which we describe below). In summary, there is some evidence for AGN activity in several of these 12 galaxies, but in all cases the upper limits on the AGN emission indicate that it contributes a small fraction of the galaxy's bolometric luminosity.

gmw_00005218.name: S5 0014+81
gmw_00005218.overview: S5 0014+81 is a highly luminous flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of 3.366 [arXiv:1202.2193, arXiv:2206.08042]. The active galaxy features a southward-extending radio jet [arXiv:1202.2193], exhibits unambiguous intrinsic X-ray absorption [arXiv:0801.1692], and shows very little optical variability [arXiv:1202.2193].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0014+813 as a z=3.366 flat-spectrum radio quasar with little optical variability and no detected VLBA outward motion, and maps a southward 5 GHz core-jet with core polarization RM.

quotes.quote: 0014+813 This source was discovered in the radio and classified as a flat spectrum radio quasar by kuhr1981 . kuhr1983 obtained a redshift of 3.366, and found the source to be exceptionally bright in the optical but unpolarized, while kaspi2007 found very little optical variability for the source. From VLBA observations at 8 GHz over 5 years, piner2007 did not detect any outward motion.

Our 5 GHz image shows a core-jet structure extending Southwards to 11 mas, consistent with the VSOP Space-VLBI image at 1.6 GHz of hirabayashi1998 , which has about a factor of two worse resolution than our own image in the North-South direction. At 8.4 GHz the extended jet emission is very faint. If we cross-identify an intensity component in the inner jet with the innermost jet component at 5 GHz in order to align our images, we obtain a physically reasonable spectral index map. After applying this same relative shift to the polarization model fits, both fits indicate a polarized component close to the origin, with a residual offset between frequencies of less than 0.20 mas; we accordingly have identified both components with the core polarization. A comparison of the corresponding model-fitted core EVPAs indicates a minimum RM of 384 rad m ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Q0014+8118 X-ray data, finding unambiguous intrinsic absorption and consistent ionized-absorber constraints from XSTAR and absori models.

quotes.quote: Q0014+8118. --- Page et al. (2004) presented the \ spectrum of this quasar in their analysis of the X-ray Baldwin effect. We reduced the same \ data taken on 2001 August 23 for 14.3 ks (pn) and 16.9 ks (MOS) (Obs-ID: 0112620201, PI: Turner). The spectrum was binned with a minimum of 50 counts per bin. The rest-frame 2--10 keV luminosity that we obtain is in good agreement with that of Page et al. (2004); they do not report any of the parameters of the best-fitting model. This is the only object in which we detect an intrinsic absorber unambiguously, with a column of 1 10^ 22 . The fully and partially covered, neutral absorber models, and the ionized absorber model (models mod:abspow , mod:pcovpow , and mod:absori ) yield a significantly better fit than the simple power-law model (model mod:pow ), as shown by the F-test (see chance probabilities in Table tab:fits ).

sources.summary: The paper studies S5 0014+81 as a high-redshift FSRQ/blazar; LAT does not significantly detect it, and the X-ray spectra show no significant additional curvature.

quotes.quote: Here we present the results of a multi-wavelength study of the four high redshift FSRQs, namely, S5 0014+81 (hereafter J0014+81; z=3.37 ), CGRaBS J0225+1846 (hereafter J0225+1846; z=2.69 ), BZQ J1430+4205 (hereafter J1430+4205; z=4.72 ), and 3FGL J1656.2 3303 (hereafter J1656 3303; z=2.40 ).

Apart from these, LAT data analysis of J0014+81 and J1430+4205 resulted in TS = 1.47 and 5.62, respectively. This suggests the non-detection of J0014+81 and only a marginal 2 detection of J1430+4205 in the ray band.

In the case of J0014+81 the two spectra are indistinguishable, so we fit them simultaneously with the same model. For J0225+1846 the observations are further apart and show significant spectral evolution. We, therefore, fit them simultaneously but allow the model parameters to vary between the two observations. The best fit parameters for each model and object are given in Table table_xrtnustar and the fit residuals are shown in Figure fig_nustar_xrt_residuals . In each case, the quality of the fit improves from M1--M3, with the absorbed power-law preferred over the unabsorbed power-law, and the broken power-law better still. The improvement in ^2 from switching from M2 to M3 is marginal for J0014+81, J1430+4205 and J1656-3303, so we check the significance using an F-test. We find chance probabilities of 0.0003, 0.011 and 0.008, respectively (in all cases, the improvement obtained by switching from M1 to M2 or M3 is significant at 3 ). We also consider the effect of ionized absorption (warm absorber) models. We replace the neutral absorber in M2 with an ionized absorber (modelled using an xstar grid), and re-fit the data for each source. This improves the fit significantly relative to the neutral absorber only in J0225+1846 ( ^2_ =1.04 ), with a column density of N_ H =5.5_ 0.9 ^ +1.7 10^ 23 , and ionization of ( )=2.8 0.1 (the spectral variability between the two observations is mostly in the power-law). XMM-Newton We next fit the \ data for each object. We fit the EPIC-pn spectrum for each source, fitting simultaneously but allowing parameters to vary independently when there are multiple spectra. For J0225+1846, there are also high-resolution RGS spectra, which we include in the fit with model parameters tied to those of the corresponding EPIC-pn spectrum. For this fit we use the higher resolution tbnew Wilms00 . As these observations are not taken simultaneously with a hard X-ray instrument the energy band is significantly narrower, making it harder to detect spectral curvature. As such, additional curvature is not significantly detected in one source, J0014+81.

sources.summary: The paper treats S5 0014+81 as a luminous z>2 quasar target, estimates its M_1450 from spectra, and uses its continuum shape and flux density for synthetic redshift-drift simulations.

quotes.quote: In addition to these three catalogs, we did a literature search to identify any QSOs at z>2 that are sufficiently luminous to warrant consideration, and found two --- S5 0014+81 Kuhr83 and HS 1946+7658 Hagen92 .
For the two additional quasars, the M_ 1450 of S5 0014+81 was estimated using the spectra from ; for HS 1946+7658, the quantity was estimated using composite spectra Jeram20 .
We used the synthetic line list and assumed the continuum shape and flux density to be that of S5 0014+81.

gmw_00005219.name: CGRaBS J0225+1846
gmw_00005219.overview: CGRaBS J0225+1846 is a high-redshift blazar and flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.69 [arXiv:1412.1179, arXiv:1604.08566]. The source exhibits marginal X-ray variability [arXiv:2008.09675], X-ray spectral evolution [arXiv:1604.08566], and experienced a low-amplitude gamma-ray flare in early 2013 [arXiv:1604.08566].

sources.summary: The paper uses RBS 315, a z = 2.69 blazar, as a representative ASTRO-H simulation target for measuring WHIM absorber redshift, temperature, and absorption-line structure.

quotes.quote: In this section, we show the simulated spectrum and the results of evolution, utilizing the same XSTAR based WHIM model as employed for GRB afterglows in sec_TS .1 for the representative Blazar RBS 315, at a redshift of z = 2.69 . We show the ASTRO-H capability in determining the redshift of the possible absorber in Figure fig_RBS315_redshift . All the simulated spectra shown here assumes the exposure time of 300 ks.

In Figure fig_RBS315_temp , we show that the SXS has a capability to measure the temperature of the warm-hot plasmas. The signature of the excess absorption is clearly seen in the data to model ratios, where we removed the intrinsic WHIM absorption model from the model denoted with histogram in Figure fig_RBS315_temp . In the ratios, we see clear absorption lines due to: Si _ VII - XI at 0.48 keV (1.8 keV at rest frame), S _ VIII - XIII at 0.64 keV (2.4 keV at rest frame), and Ar _ X - XII at 0.81 keV (3.0 keV at rest frame), as well as we see in the inset indicated with energy at the source (WHIM) rest frame. In addition, the difference of temperature of WHIM is seen in the lowest energy bin in the ratios. We also show the confidence contours for the two different temperature WHIM.

sources.summary: The paper reports CGRaBS J0225+1846 as a high-redshift FSRQ/blazar newly detected in gamma rays, showing spectral evolution and a low-amplitude flare.

quotes.quote: Here we present the results of a multi-wavelength study of the four high redshift FSRQs, namely, S5 0014+81 (hereafter J0014+81; z=3.37 ), CGRaBS J0225+1846 (hereafter J0225+1846; z=2.69 ), BZQ J1430+4205 (hereafter J1430+4205; z=4.72 ), and 3FGL J1656.2 3303 (hereafter J1656 3303; z=2.40 ).
Other than J1656 3303, we find a statistically significant ray emission from J0225+1846 (TS 188, 13 detection).

For J0225+1846 the observations are further apart and show significant spectral evolution. We, therefore, fit them simultaneously but allow the model parameters to vary between the two observations. The best fit parameters for each model and object are given in Table table_xrtnustar and the fit residuals are shown in Figure fig_nustar_xrt_residuals . In each case, the quality of the fit improves from M1--M3, with the absorbed power-law preferred over the unabsorbed power-law, and the broken power-law better still. The improvement in ^2 from switching from M2 to M3 is marginal for J0014+81, J1430+4205 and J1656-3303, so we check the significance using an F-test. We find chance probabilities of 0.0003, 0.011 and 0.008, respectively (in all cases, the improvement obtained by switching from M1 to M2 or M3 is significant at 3 ). We also consider the effect of ionized absorption (warm absorber) models. We replace the neutral absorber in M2 with an ionized absorber (modelled using an xstar grid), and re-fit the data for each source. This improves the fit significantly relative to the neutral absorber only in J0225+1846 ( ^2_ =1.04 ), with a column density of N_ H =5.5_ 0.9 ^ +1.7 10^ 23 , and ionization of ( )=2.8 0.1 (the spectral variability between the two observations is mostly in the power-law). XMM-Newton We next fit the \ data for each object. We fit the EPIC-pn spectrum for each source, fitting simultaneously but allowing parameters to vary independently when there are multiple spectra. For J0225+1846, there are also high-resolution RGS spectra, which we include in the fit with model parameters tied to those of the corresponding EPIC-pn spectrum. For this fit we use the higher resolution tbnew Wilms00 . As these observations are not taken simultaneously with a hard X-ray instrument the energy band is significantly narrower, making it harder to detect spectral curvature. As such, additional curvature is not significantly detected in one source, J0014+81. This spectrum is well fit with a simple power-law model (M1, ^2/ d.o.f. =112/116 ) and the fit is not significantly improved by either additional absorption or a spectral break. The other three sources show evidence of spectral curvature, with a significant improvement in the fit from M1 to M2 or M3. There is only a significant improvement from M2 to M3 in the case of J1656 3303 (p=0.004). For J0225+1486 the improvement is marginal (p=0.1) and for J1430+4205 the fit is slightly worse with M3 than M2, although not significantly so. The data and residuals to each model are shown in Figure fig_xmm_residuals and the parameters are given in Table table_xmm . Spectral Energy Distributions Model Setup We use a simple one zone synchrotron and inverse Compton emission model fully described in 2009MNRAS.397..985G and here we discuss it in brief. We assume the emission region is a sphere of radius R_ blob , located at a distance R_ diss from the central black hole, and moving with a bulk Lorentz factor . The emitting region is filled with relativistic electrons having a smooth broken power law energy distribution of the following type Q( ) = Q_0 ( _ b )^ n1 ( / _ b )^ n1 + ( / _ b )^ n2 . The size of the emission region is constrained by assuming it to cover entire jet cross-section with semi opening angle of the jet as 0.1 rad. The magnetic field is considered as tangled and uniform in the emission region. We consider several sources of thermal radiation externally to the jet: (i) the direct radiation from the accretion disk; (ii) the X-ray corona sandwiching the accretion disk, reprocessing a fraction f_ cor of the accretion disk luminosity ( L_ disk ), and having a cut-off power law spectrum ( L_ cor ( ) ^ _ cor (- / _ ) ); (iii) the broad line region (BLR), assumed to reprocess a fraction f_ BLR of L_ disk from a spherical shell located at a distance R_ BLR = 10^ 17 L^ 1/2 _ disk,45 cm, where L_ disk,45 is the disk luminosity in units of 10 ^ 45 erg s ^ 1 ; (iv) and the IR emission from a dusty torus located at a distance R_ torus = 10^ 18 L^ 1/2 _ disk,45 cm and re-emit a fraction f_ torus of the accretion luminosity. The spectra of both the BLR and the torus are assumed to be a blackbody peaking at the rest-frame frequency of Lyman- line e.g., 2008MNRAS.386..945T and T_ torus , respectively, where T_ torus is the characteristic temperature of the torus. In the comoving frame, we appropriately evaluate the radiative energy densities of these components and used them to derive the EC spectrum, and then transform into observer frame. We estimate the power that the jet carries in the form of magnetic field ( P_ mag ), radiation ( P_ rad ), relativistic electrons ( P_ ele ), and cold protons ( P_ kin ). The last quantity, i.e., the kinetic jet power, is derived by assuming equal number density of protons and relativistic electrons e.g., 2008MNRAS.385..283C . The flux produced by the accretion disk is estimated by assuming a standard 1973A&A....24..337S disk having a multi-temperature radial profile as follows 2002apa..book.....F T = 3 R_ Sch L_ disk 16 _ acc _ MB R^3 1- 3 R_ Sch R ^ 1/2 ^ 1/4 , where R_ Sch is the Schwarzschild radius and _ acc is the accretion efficiency. This is used to reproduce the thermal radiation (the big blue bump) observed at optical-UV energies and it constrains both L_ disk and the central black hole mass of the source. There are only two parameters to be fitted, the accretion rate M _ acc and the black hole mass, once we assume a typical value of the accretion efficiency ( _ acc =10 ). The former can be derived from the intrinsic accretion disk luminosity L_ disk = _ acc M _ acc ^2 with L_ disk being an observable parameter (provided the peak of the big blue bump is visible in the SED). This leaves only black hole mass as a free parameter and can be calculated from the optical-UV SED fitting. SED Modeling Results The broadband SEDs of all the four blazars are generated using quasi-simultaneous data from , , and LAT. None of the sources are detected by LAT even at 3 level in the month of observations and therefore we calculate their respective 2 upper limits. The generated SEDs are reproduced by the model presented in Section subsubsec:model . For modeling of the SEDs, we adopt the following assumptions: the spectral shape of the X-ray corona emission is assumed to be flat ( _ cor = 1) and the cut-off energy is fixed at 150 keV 2009MNRAS.397..985G . Fractions of the accretion disk luminosity re-emitted by the X-ray corona, the BLR, and the dusty torus are considered as f_ cor =0.3 , f_ BLR =0.1 , and f_ torus =0.5 , respectively. The results of the SED generation and modeling are presented in Figure fig:sed_fit and the associated modeling parameters are given in Table tab:sed_param and tab:jet_power . A study of the high redshift blazars offers a unique opportunity to understand the physical properties of jetted sources at the extreme end of the accretion disk luminosity and the jet power. Since blazars emit a major fraction of their bolometric luminosity in the form of high energy X-ray and ray radiation, it is important to have a good quality sensitive monitoring at these energies. In this regard, the observations from the facilities like LAT, , and XMM-Newton are crucial to learn the radiative processes powering the jet of these distant sources. Gamma-ray Emission The high redshift blazars are, in general, weak in the ray band because of the k correction effect (for increasing redshifts) and also due to intrinsic shift of the IC peak at lower frequencies as their bolometric luminosity increases e.g., 1998MNRAS.301..451G . Three out of four sources studied here were never detected in the ray band, thus supporting the above hypothesis. However, thanks to the recently released Pass 8 data from LAT, which is more sensitive at lower energies and thus important for high redshift objects, it is now possible to search distant blazars in the ray band and constrain their SEDs in a far better way than in the past. We search for the detection of significant ray emission from all the four high redshift blazars studied in this work (including J1656 3303 which is included in 3FGL catalog) and report J0225+1846 as a newly detected ray emitter at 13 significance. This is the first report of the discovery of the ray emission from this object and confirms the prediction of 2008ApJS..175...97H . We also find a marginal 2 evidence for the detection of J1430+4205 at ray energies. Moreover, we search for temporal flux variations among J0225+1846 and J1656 3303 and find the former to exhibit a low amplitude ray flare around the beginning of the year 2013 (see Figure fig:fermi_lc ).

sources.summary: The paper favors intrinsic jet-based radiative processes for RBS 315, while noting prior absorption interpretations and possible host-galaxy absorbing material.

quotes.quote: An intrinsically shaped particle energy distribution (and hence that of the produced photons) accelerated by the jet is found to shape the spectra (EXP model) in four of the FSRQs: RBS 315, PKS 1830 211, QSO B0014 + 810 and PKS 2149 306. For these, the spectral index is in the range 1.22 to 1.97, and the folding energy of the exponential roll-off E_f is in the range 0.356 keV to 1.127 keV (observer frame).

With these, the inferred break energy is in the range 0.04 - 1.42 (QSO B0014 + 810), 0.05 - 1.68 (RBS 315), 0.05 - 1.77 (PKS 1830 211) and 0.05 - 1.85 (PKS 2149 306). These are consistent with the current estimates for these sources (see Tables paratabxmm_2 and paratabswift ) and imply that the observed flattening is more likely from jet based intrinsic radiative processes.

The recent study of includes spectra from XMM-Newton , Swift and NuSTAR observations (0.3 - 70 keV) spanning between 2013 January - 2015 January; the fitted column densities N_H are consistently in the range 3.2 - 4.4 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 in all epochs with 1.4 - 1.6 and indicate a possible absorber in the host galaxy. Our analysis of three XMM-Newton spectra suggests a preference for the EXP model with of 1.26 -- 1.48 and E_ f of 0.36 -- 0.40 keV which is attributed to jet based intrinsic physical processes, consistent with the study of .

sources.summary: The paper presents high-quality X-ray spectra of high-redshift RBS 315, finding that CCD fits cannot decide whether the spectral curvature is photoelectric absorption or intrinsic source curvature.

quotes.quote: We report on observations of one of the brightest X-ray sources at a high redshift, RBS 315 ( z=2.69 ). Despite several previous analyses, no definite conclusion as to the source of the curvature in its spectrum, whether absorption or intrinsic, could be reached. We present observations by \ (EPIC and RGS) as well as \ and . The \ spectra of this source are of unprecedented quality. A purely statistical analysis of the CCD spectra yields no clear results - the spectrum is as likely to be photo-electrically absorbed as it is to be curved at the source, and no constraint on the position of the absorber can be obtained.

sources.summary: The paper describes TXS 0222+185 as the brightest X-ray source in the sample, with marginal X-ray variability and disc and jet luminosities of the same order.

quotes.quote: The flux ranges from 5 10^ 14 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 to 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 , the highest flux of (1.19 0.04) 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 being observed for TXS 0222+185 ( z=2.69 ).

There is a marginal evidence ( P( 2)=0.038 and 2/dof=1.68 ) that the X-ray flux of TXS 0222+185 is variable; it is mostly around (0.96-1.1) 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 which increased to (1.62 0.13) 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 and (1.37 0.16) 10^ 11 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 on 24 December 2014 and 31 July 2006, respectively.

For all the sources, expect for TXS 0800+618 and TXS 0222+185 for which L_ disc /L_ jet =1.17-2.44 , the jet luminosity is lower than that of the disc L_ d (1.09-10.94) 10^ 46 erg s^ 1 which is estimated by fitting the UV excess.

gmw_00005220.name: 3FGL J1656.2 3303
gmw_00005220.overview: 3FGL J1656.2 3303 is a high-redshift blazar located at a redshift of z=2.40 [arXiv:1604.08566]. The source is occasionally detected by the Large Area Telescope without showing any flaring activity, and it exhibits X-ray spectral curvature [arXiv:1604.08566].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3FGL J1656.2 3303 as a z=2.40 high-redshift blazar that is occasionally detected by LAT and shows significant X-ray spectral curvature.

quotes.quote: Here we present the results of a multi-wavelength study of the four high redshift FSRQs, namely, S5 0014+81 (hereafter J0014+81; z=3.37 ), CGRaBS J0225+1846 (hereafter J0225+1846; z=2.69 ), BZQ J1430+4205 (hereafter J1430+4205; z=4.72 ), and 3FGL J1656.2 3303 (hereafter J1656 3303; z=2.40 ).

The source J1656 3303 was discovered with the BAT during its first year of survey operation 2006ATel..799....1O . This object lies close to the galactic plane (galactic latitude 6 ^ .3). Using the X-ray Telescope XRT; 2005SSRv..120..165B observations, 2006ATel..835....1T suggested it to be an extragalactic source. Later, a detailed multi-wavelength study of this source was done by 2008A&A...480..715M with a major focus on its optical spectroscopic properties. They confirmed the extragalactic nature of J1656 3303 and concluded it to be a high redshift blazar at z=2.40 .

On the other hand, J1656 3303 is occasionally detected by LAT without any flaring activity. X-ray Properties We fit the (3 79 keV), XRT (0.3 10 keV), and (0.3 10 keV for EPIC-pn and 0.5 2 keV for RGS) spectra using xspec Arnaud96 version 12.9.0j. In all cases we include Galactic absorption, taken from the online tool of Willingale13 , of 2.19 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 for J0014+81, 1.68 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 for J0225+1486, 1.20 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 for J1430+4205, and 3.35 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 for J1656-3303. We use wilm abundances Wilms00 and vern cross sections Verner96 . Broad-band 0.3 79 keV spectra The most useful spectra for determining the nature of the change in slope in the X-ray band are the simultaneous XRT and \ spectra, which cover the energy range from 0.3 -79 keV. We fit the same three models to each spectrum: a powerlaw absorbed by Galactic absorption only (M1, tbabs * powerlaw in xspec ); a powerlaw absorbed by Galactic absorption and absorption intrinsic to the source (M2, tbabs * ztbabs * powerlaw ); and a broken power-law, absorbed by Galactic absorption only (M3, tbabs * bknpower ). These correspond to a spectrum with no curvature, a spectrum where the curvature is due to absorption of the source by the host galaxy, and a spectrum where the curvature is intrinsic. Two of the sources (J0014+81 and J0225+1846) were observed twice with \ and . In the case of J0014+81 the two spectra are indistinguishable, so we fit them simultaneously with the same model. For J0225+1846 the observations are further apart and show significant spectral evolution. We, therefore, fit them simultaneously but allow the model parameters to vary between the two observations. The best fit parameters for each model and object are given in Table table_xrtnustar and the fit residuals are shown in Figure fig_nustar_xrt_residuals . In each case, the quality of the fit improves from M1--M3, with the absorbed power-law preferred over the unabsorbed power-law, and the broken power-law better still. The improvement in ^2 from switching from M2 to M3 is marginal for J0014+81, J1430+4205 and J1656-3303, so we check the significance using an F-test. We find chance probabilities of 0.0003, 0.011 and 0.008, respectively (in all cases, the improvement obtained by switching from M1 to M2 or M3 is significant at 3 ). We also consider the effect of ionized absorption (warm absorber) models. We replace the neutral absorber in M2 with an ionized absorber (modelled using an xstar grid), and re-fit the data for each source. This improves the fit significantly relative to the neutral absorber only in J0225+1846 ( ^2_ =1.04 ), with a column density of N_ H =5.5_ 0.9 ^ +1.7 10^ 23 , and ionization of ( )=2.8 0.1 (the spectral variability between the two observations is mostly in the power-law). XMM-Newton We next fit the \ data for each object. We fit the EPIC-pn spectrum for each source, fitting simultaneously but allowing parameters to vary independently when there are multiple spectra. For J0225+1846, there are also high-resolution RGS spectra, which we include in the fit with model parameters tied to those of the corresponding EPIC-pn spectrum. For this fit we use the higher resolution tbnew Wilms00 . As these observations are not taken simultaneously with a hard X-ray instrument the energy band is significantly narrower, making it harder to detect spectral curvature. As such, additional curvature is not significantly detected in one source, J0014+81. This spectrum is well fit with a simple power-law model (M1, ^2/ d.o.f. =112/116 ) and the fit is not significantly improved by either additional absorption or a spectral break. The other three sources show evidence of spectral curvature, with a significant improvement in the fit from M1 to M2 or M3. There is only a significant improvement from M2 to M3 in the case of J1656 3303 (p=0.004).

gmw_00005221.name: PS J2107 1611
gmw_00005221.overview: PS J2107 1611 is a quadruply lensed quasar located at a redshift of z=2.673 [arXiv:2310.04494]. The system features a fold configuration with a bright lensed arc and displays spectral residuals attributed to its lensed host galaxy [arXiv:2310.04494].

sources.summary: The paper reports PS J2107 1611 as a z=2.673 quadruply lensed quasar with a bright arc, anomalously faint fold images, and host-galaxy residuals in spectra.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of PS J2107 1611, a fold-configuration 4.3 separation quadruply lensed quasar with a bright lensed arc. It was discovered using a convolutional neural network on Pan-STARRS gri images of pre-selected quasar candidates with multiple nearby Pan-STARRS detections. Spectroscopic follow-up with EFOSC2 on the ESO 3.58m New Technology Telescope reveals the source to be a quasar at z=2.673 , with the blended fold image pair showing deformed broad lines relative to the other images. The flux ratios measured from optical to near-infrared imaging in the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope Legacy Survey, Pan-STARRS, the Legacy Surveys, and the Vista Hemisphere Survey are inconsistent with a smooth mass model as the fold pair images are 15 times too faint.

The 3 spectra are typical of a quasar at z_s 2.673 (Fig. fig:j2107spectra , where the 2 spectra of image A have been averaged). Images B and C are very similar with a flux ratio of B/C 1.8 , approximately constant with wavelength and compatible with 2 images of the same object with differences attributable to extrinsic effects (e.g., microlensing).

We also note the presence of clear residuals in the 2D A--B spectrum, between the two quasar images, at a wavelength corresponding to Ly (Fig. fig:lyacontamination ). We attribute this to the lensed host galaxy already seen in imaging.

gmw_00005222.name: host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB060510B
aliases_local: host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB060510B
gmw_00005222.overview: The host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB060510B is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=4.941 [arXiv:0705.4101]. It is characterized by high neutral hydrogen column density [arXiv:0705.4101], metal enrichment exceeding one-tenth solar [arXiv:0705.4101], and a sub-L* luminosity [arXiv:0905.0001].

sources.original_name: host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB060510B
sources.summary: The paper characterizes the GRB060510B host galaxy as a z=4.941 high-redshift galaxy with a spectrum good enough for metallicity analysis, showing high neutral hydrogen and metal enrichment above one-tenth solar.

quotes.quote: We describe the properties of the host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB060510B based on a spectrum of the burst afterglow obtained with the Gemini North 8m telescope. The galaxy lies at a redshift of z=4.941 making it the fourth highest spectroscopically identified burst host. However, it is the second highest redshift galaxy for which the quality of the spectrum permits a detailed metallicity analysis. The neutral hydrogen column density has a logarithmic value of 21.0--21.2 cm ^ 2 and the weak metal lines of Ni, S and Fe show that the metallicity is in excess of a tenth of solar which is far above the metallicities in damped Lyman alpha absorbers at high redshift.

sources.summary: The GRB 060510B host was detected by Spitzer but not in deep optical imaging, consistent with a high-redshift, sub-L* GRB host behind the Lyman break.

quotes.quote: No object was detected at the P60 position or anywhere inside of XRT and XMM X-ray error circles in either the R or g filters to 26th magnitude. The host galaxy of this burst was imaged by the Spitzer Space Telescope in a study conducted by , and successfully detected with a flux level of 0.23 0.04 Jy. Our g band nondetection can be interpreted as support of this association (a detection of a galaxy blueward of the expected Lyman break in or near the optical position would indicate that the Spitzer source was actually an intervening source at lower redshift). Given the high redshift, the R nondetection is not surprising either; our limit of R > 26 corresponds to a luminosity of M_R > -20.5 , which is still consistent with the luminosities of the majority of GRB hosts which have been observed to date Fruchter+2006 and with the sub- L_ * nature of the reported Spitzer host Chary+2007 .

gmw_00005223.name: LAE 13636
gmw_00005223.overview: LAE 13636 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha galaxy [arXiv:1402.1168]. Its systemic redshift has been studied using Lyman-alpha and nebular emission lines, including observations from LRIS, MagE, and FMOS spectroscopy [arXiv:1402.1168].

sources.summary: The paper revisits LAE 13636/COSMOS-13636, comparing LRIS and MagE Ly redshifts and arguing that higher-resolution FMOS data better constrain its systemic redshift.

quotes.quote: 2013ApJ...765...70H have reported that LAE 13636 has a v_ Ly of 99^ +16 _ 16 km s ^ 1 . However, the H line profile would have been affected by a residual of a neighboring OH line due to the low spectral resolution of their Keck-II/NIRSPEC observation ( R 1500 ), making it difficult to determine accurately the systemic redshift. Our FMOS spectroscopy with R 2200 would securely detect nebular emission less affected by OH lines.

Additionally, we provide a consistency check for our measurement of z_ Ly by using the same object as in 2013ApJ...765...70H , COSMOS-13636. The object has been observed with both of LRIS in this work and Magellan/MagE in a previous work. The redshift of Ly estimated from the LRIS spectrum ( z_ Ly ^ LRIS =2.1626 0.00073 ) is in good agreement with that of MagE ( z_ Ly ^ MagE =2.16229 0.00008 ) within a 1 fitting error. The difference in velocity is 30 70 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00005224.name: 1FHL J0307.4+4915
gmw_00005224.overview: 1FHL J0307.4+4915 is an unassociated gamma-ray source that features a proposed low-frequency counterpart [arXiv:1607.03415]. The source exhibits a VLBI flux density that significantly exceeds its NVSS flux density, suggesting a degree of flux density variability [arXiv:1607.03415].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1FHL J0307.4+4915 as an unassociated gamma-ray source with a proposed low-frequency counterpart and VLBI flux greatly exceeding its NVSS flux, suggesting variability.

quotes.quote: By an inspection of both NVSS and VLBI flux densities, it emerges that there are some interesting sources. In 10 of the sources the ratio S_ 5, VLBI /S_ 1.4, NVSS is significantly higher than 1, like in the case of the UGS 1FHL J0307.4+4915, with S_ 5, VLBI = 300 30 mJy, while S_ 1.4, NVSS is 56.0 1.7 mJy. On the other end of the distribution there are sources for which the ratio S_ 5, VLBI /S_ 1.4, NVSS is significantly lower than 1. For example, for the sources 1FHL J0333.6+2918, 1FHL J0334.0+6539, 1FHL J0601.0+3838, and 1FHL J2127.8+3614, the ratio S_ 5, VLBI /S_ 1.4, NVSS is between 0.1 and 0.2. The immediate interpretation is that the sources with S_ 1.4, NVSS S_ 5, VLBI have extended emission (on arcsecond scale), while in the case of S_ 5, VLBI S_ 1.4, NVSS some degree of variability could be responsible of the higher observed VLBI flux density.

For the source 1FHL J0307.4+4915 a new low-frequency counterpart is now proposed in the second catalog of hard LAT sources 2FHL, Ackermann2016 . All of these new associations are coincident with those proposed in this work.
A clear example of flux density variability is provided by the source 1FHL J0307.4+4915, for which we find S_ 5, VLBI = 300 30 mJy, while S_ 1.4, NVSS is 56.0 1.7 mJy.

gmw_00005225.name: SGAS 1723
gmw_00005225.overview: SGAS 1723 is a strongly lensed emission line galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 1.4 [arXiv:2006.11387]. Observations of the galaxy reveal spatial variation in its star formation rates and rest-frame optical strong emission lines [arXiv:2006.11387].

sources.summary: The paper includes SGAS 1723 among two strongly lensed z~1.4 SGAS galaxies, using grism and supporting spectroscopy to map line-ratio, reddening, and star-formation variations.

quotes.quote: In this Paper, we use \ grism spectroscopy from GO14230 (PI: Rigby) to measure the standard suite of rest-frame optical strong emission lines, from [ O II ] 3727, 3729 \ to [ S II ] 6716, 6733 , in two strongly lensed galaxies at z 1.4 selected from the Sloan Giant Arcs Survey (SGAS). We map the spatial variation of the diagnostic line ratios H /H , R23, O32, and Ne3O2, which are sensitive to dust, metallicity, and ionization parameter. We quantify the spatial variation in these strong line ratios, and examine the corresponding variation in inferred physical characteristics of the nebular gas.

Broadband Spitzer Imaging Data from the IRAC instrument of the Spitzer Space Telescope, acquired during the post-cryogenic ``warm mission'', were obtained through program 90232 (for SGAS 1723; PI J. Rigby) and program 12001 (for SGAS 2340; PI J. Rigby). The individual frame times were 30 s; the total per-pixel integration times were 30 min in IRAC channel 1 (3.6 micron), and 10 min in IRAC channel 2 (4.5 micron).
There is significant spatial variation in the star formation rates across the two galaxies, as shown in Fig. fig:SFR .

gmw_00005226.name: SGAS 2340
gmw_00005226.overview: SGAS 2340 is a strongly lensed emission line galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 1.4 [arXiv:2006.11387]. Observations of the galaxy reveal spatial variation in its star formation rates and diagnostic line ratios [arXiv:2006.11387].

sources.summary: The paper includes SGAS 2340 among two strongly lensed z~1.4 SGAS galaxies, using grism and supporting spectroscopy to map line-ratio, reddening, and star-formation variations.

quotes.quote: In this Paper, we use \ grism spectroscopy from GO14230 (PI: Rigby) to measure the standard suite of rest-frame optical strong emission lines, from [ O II ] 3727, 3729 \ to [ S II ] 6716, 6733 , in two strongly lensed galaxies at z 1.4 selected from the Sloan Giant Arcs Survey (SGAS). We map the spatial variation of the diagnostic line ratios H /H , R23, O32, and Ne3O2, which are sensitive to dust, metallicity, and ionization parameter. We quantify the spatial variation in these strong line ratios, and examine the corresponding variation in inferred physical characteristics of the nebular gas.

Broadband Spitzer Imaging Data from the IRAC instrument of the Spitzer Space Telescope, acquired during the post-cryogenic ``warm mission'', were obtained through program 90232 (for SGAS 1723; PI J. Rigby) and program 12001 (for SGAS 2340; PI J. Rigby). The individual frame times were 30 s; the total per-pixel integration times were 30 min in IRAC channel 1 (3.6 micron), and 10 min in IRAC channel 2 (4.5 micron).
There is significant spatial variation in the star formation rates across the two galaxies, as shown in Fig. fig:SFR .

gmw_00005227.name: NGC 647
gmw_00005227.overview: NGC 647 is a prolate rotator galaxy that displays clear signs of interaction, including a central dust line aligned with its minor photometric axis [arXiv:2103.05006]. It features a distinct northern plume or loop, and two nearby smaller galaxies are candidates for surviving merger cores [arXiv:2103.05006].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 647 as a prolate rotator with a northern plume or loop, a central dust line, and nearby small galaxies that may be merger cores.

quotes.quote: NGC 647 has clear interaction signs, most notably a distinct plume or loop stretching around 80 to the north, visible in the Legacy Surveys image, Fig. fig:ls7 . The galaxy also displays a dust line in the center, roughly allied with the minor photometric axis. Two close smaller galaxies (one 50 to the north, the other 25 to the southeast) are candidates for the surviving cores of the merging secondary galaxies.

gmw_00005228.name: NGC 810
gmw_00005228.overview: NGC 810 is a rich Type I shell galaxy with at least eight detected shells [arXiv:2103.05006]. The system features a dust lane along its minor photometric axis and a possible surviving core of a merging secondary galaxy [arXiv:2103.05006].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 810 as a rich Type I shell system with at least eight shells, a minor-axis dust lane, and a possible surviving merger core.

quotes.quote: NGC 810 is an excellent example of a rich Type I shell system with at least eight shells detected in the Legacy Surveys image, Fig. fig:ls7 . The galaxy also possesses a prominent dust lane along the minor photometric axis. Around 10 from the center, close to the dust line, there is a possible surviving core of the merging secondary galaxy.

gmw_00005229.name: NGC 4572
gmw_00005229.overview: NGC 4572 is a tidally disturbed companion galaxy to the larger NGC 4589 [arXiv:2103.05006]. Deep imaging of the galaxy reveals tidal arms, suggesting it possesses a warped disk with extraplanar material or a strong tidal spiral [arXiv:2103.05006].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4572 as a tidally disturbed companion of NGC 4589, with prominent tidal arms and evidence for a warped disk or tidal spiral.

quotes.quote: A companion galaxy NGC 4572, the northwest corner of Fig. fig:4589 , is tidally disturbed. The deep image reveals prominent tidal arms. considered NGC 4572 to be a strong candidate for a significantly warped disk with much extraplanar material or a strong tidal spiral. NGC 4589 the only bigger galaxy in its surroundings. The radial-velocity difference of the galaxies, 230 km s ^ 1 SIMBAD, simbad , is consistent with them being associated.

gmw_00005230.name: NGC 7052
gmw_00005230.overview: NGC 7052 is an isolated elliptical field galaxy [arXiv:1807.10317, arXiv:2103.05006] characterized as a prolate rotator [arXiv:2103.05006] with boxy outer isophotes [arXiv:2103.05006] and an extended X-ray halo [arXiv:1807.10317]. It hosts a central dust disk [arXiv:2103.05006, arXiv:2101.06579], a narrow radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256], and a central black hole [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256, arXiv:astro-ph/9804194].

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 7052 as an isolated known prolate rotator outside the sample, with HST imaging revealing a new shell, boxy isophotes, and a central dust disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 7052, Fig. fig:7052 , is an isolated field galaxy with no nearby companion. It is a known prolate rotator wa88 that is not a part of our sample. The galaxy was observed with HST, but generally, HST probes galaxies at different scales than CFHT MegaCam. Thus the HST images do not qualify as comparable with MATLAS and we cannot include NGC 7052 in the sample, but we inspected the images anyway. We used the HST/WFC3 F110W archival data obtained on Jun 14, 2016 (exposure time 2496 s; PI: John Blakeslee).

We found a previously unreported shell in the northeast direction, about 42 from the center along the major photometric axis. The galaxy also shows strong outer boxy isophotes lau85,bdm88,gs93,dej94,vk99 indicating a possible presence of more unresolved outer shells. Additionally, there is a large central dust disk with a diameter of 1 kpc (4.0 ) oriented along the optical major axis nie90,dej96 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7052 as an isolated field galaxy with a large central dust disk, emission-gas disk, black hole, HI emission, blue nucleus, and narrow jet.

quotes.quote: NGC 7052 NGC 7052 is a field galaxy with no nearby companion. There is a large central dust disk with a diameter of 1 kpc ( 4.0'' ) (De Juan 1996). The inner part of the dust disk hosts a small emission-gas disk and low-level emission is detected throughout the dust disk. By modeling the kinematics of the gas disk van der Marel van den Bosch (1998) detected a black hole with a mass of 3 10^8 . There is a dust protuberance, also seen in the emission image at the west end of the disk.

HI was detected in emission blueshifted by 500 \ with respect to the systemic velocity of the galaxy (Huchtmeier 1995). The nucleus which is obscured by dust is slightly bluer than its surroundings. NGC 7052 hosts a narrow radio jet with a very weak counter jet (Morganti 1987; CB88; Xu 1999a).

sources.summary: The paper models NGC7052 as an elliptical galaxy with a Chandra-observed X-ray halo, de Vaucouleurs stellar spheroid, and an excellent emergent-gravity mass-profile fit.

quotes.quote: In particular, Memola et al.(2009) have built the mass model of two elliptical galaxies NGC7052 and NGC7785. Chandra X-ray Observatory has observed these sources, discussed in . Observations show that- NGC7052 and NGC7785 have an extended x-ray halo out to 16 \ kpc and 32 \ kpc respectively. The stellar mass distribution is well described by de Vacouleurs spheroid with the half-light radius R_e = 7.7 \ kpc and R_e = 5.6 \ kpc respectively.

The best fit models have: M_ sph =10^ 11.4 M_ for NGC7052 and M_ sph 10^ 11.9 M_ for NGC7785. The value of spheroid mass is similar to the one found in dark matter halo scenario. The fit is excellent for NGC7052 and fairly good for NGC7785.

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 7052 as an H I non-detection with an edge-on dust disk, weak radio core, stringent upper limit, and need for higher-resolution disk observations.

quotes.quote: The 3 absorption peak in NGC 7052 is considered a non-detection, though it shows a hint of a resolved line. Since our observations, four sources have been studied with other telescopes. NGC 315 is presented in morganti_n315 ; 3C 293 was observed at high resolution by beswick02 ; and NGC 5141 and NGC 7052 were observed at lower resolution in emonts10 . Only NGC 7052 was not detected, and a stringent upper-limit was set.

NGC 7052 has the weakest radio core in our sample, which makes it the most difficult source to detect. Nevertheless, emonts10 set a stringent upper limit. Higher resolution observations of this source may provide insight into the structure of the disk.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 7052 nuclear gas kinematics as a disk in the stellar body and finds that a roughly 3.3 x 10^8 solar-mass black hole fits the observed rotation.

quotes.quote: To interpret the gas kinematics we construct axisymmetric models in which the gas and dust reside in a disk in the equatorial plane of the stellar body, and are viewed at an inclination of 70^ . It is assumed that the gas moves on circular orbits, with an intrinsic velocity dispersion due to turbulence (or otherwise non-gravitational motion). The latter is required to fit the observed increase in the line widths towards the nucleus, and must reach a value in excess of 500 in the central 0.1'' . The circular velocity is calculated from the combined gravitational potential of the stars and a possible nuclear black hole. Models without a black hole predict a rotation curve that is shallower than observed ( V_ pred = 92 at =0.2'' ), and are ruled out at >99 confidence.
Models with a black hole of mass = 3.3^ +2.3 _ 1.3 10^8 provide an acceptable fit.

sources.summary: The paper gives K-band decomposition notes for NGC7052, describing its morphology, fitted components, residual structures, and why the adopted model is preferred over simpler alternatives.

quotes.quote: NGC7052 (Fig. fig:NGC7052 ) is a very elongated elliptical galaxy. Stellar foreground contamination is severe: as was the case for Cyg A, creating the mask required a ``second-pass" using the residual image. The galaxy shows a strong ellipticity gradient ( e 0.2 in the center, and 0.6 beyond 30 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 7052 can be fit without a warm torus, and HST images suggest a kpc-scale dust disk as a cold thermal source near the nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 7052 The model of NGC 7052 with the lowest BIC and the model with a forced torus have very similar goodness-of-fit with the best fit having a BIC of 425.7 and the forced-torus fit having a BIC of 429.8. However, inspection of the forced torus fit shows that it is unusually cold for a circumnuclear torus. Indeed, HST images of NGC 7052 by show a promising candidate for such a cold thermal source in the form of a kpc-scale dust disk which crosses the core region just off to the side of our line-of-sight into the nucleus. Overall, we conclude that a warm thermal component, such as a torus, is unnecessary to explain the IRS flux.

gmw_00005231.name: SDSS1420+5300A
gmw_00005231.overview: SDSS1420+5300A is a face-on barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2209.03359] at a redshift of z = 1.646 [arXiv:2209.03359, arXiv:2304.13776]. It hosts a broad-line active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2304.13776] and exhibits higher star formation and younger stellar ages in its central region than in its outskirts [arXiv:2209.03359].

sources.summary: The paper resolves SDSSJ1420+5300A as a face-on barred spiral quasar host whose central 2-4 kpc has younger ages and higher star formation than its outskirts.

quotes.quote: The fortuitous face-on orientation of SDSSJ1420+5300A at z = 1.646 enables us to find higher star formation and younger ages in the central 2-4 kpc region relative to the outskirts, which may help explain the relatively compact nature of quasar hosts and pose a challenge to AGN feedback models.

For SDSS1420A ( z=1.646 ), the 2D fitting results are presented in Figure fig:fitting to highlight the performance of \ (F150W, F444W) compared to \ (F814W and F160W). With the 4000 \ break ( 10,000 \ at z 1.6 ) being redshifted out of the F814W filter, a very marginal detection of the host is obtained with /F814W. At longer wavelengths, a highly significant detection of the host galaxy with /F160W leads to very consistent fitting results with that from /F150W (see Table table:sample ). Now, with the high-resolution power of , we can now clearly see that the quasar host is residing in a spiral galaxy with a prominent bar. Thus, the galaxy can be classified as an SBa or SBb in the Hubble classification scheme.

sources.summary: The paper demonstrates JWST NIRCam AGN-host decomposition on SDSS1420+5300A, a z=1.646 broad-line AGN with reverberation-mapped black-hole mass and a massive host above the local late-type relation.

quotes.quote: As a demonstration of applying our method of optimal PSF modeling to study the properties of host galaxies of broad-line AGNs, we select a broad-line AGN SDSS1420+5300A (R.A. =215.02333 , Decl =53.01020 ) at z=1.646 , covered by Module A of NIRCam imaging in pointing 1 of the CEERS fields. This AGN has a BH mass ( M_ BH ) of M_ BH = 10^ 9.00 0.16 M_ from reverberation mapping Grier+2019ApJ .

The bright emission, large size and relatively high contribution of the host galaxy in SDSS1420 + 5300A enable us to investigate the effects of PSF mismatch and host model mismatch on the derived host properties.

With a more robust M_ BH estimate from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Reverberation Mapping project Shen+2015ApJS, Grier+2019ApJ , we can now place SDSS1420+5300A on the M_ BH M_* plane. We adopt the average M_* estimate from the DS sets, M_*=10^ 11.1 0.1 M_ , given their better modeling of the host structure. Comparing with the local M_ BH M_* relation from inactive galaxies in Greene+2020ARA&A , we find that this object lies 0.6 dex above the upper envelope (best-fit relation + 1 intrinsic scatter) of local late-type (spiral) galaxies, but consistent with early-type (elliptical and S0) galaxies (Figure fig17 ).

gmw_00005232.name: Willman 1
gmw_00005232.overview: Willman 1 is an ultra-faint object discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey that was originally classified as a dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1003.3745]. It has a high mass-to-light ratio of 700, making its dark matter halo an attractive target for indirect dark matter searches [arXiv:1003.3745].

sources.summary: The paper treats Willman 1 as an ultra-faint SDSS dwarf-galaxy candidate and dark-matter target, using X-ray emission expectations and non-detections to constrain possible sterile-neutrino decay.

quotes.quote: Here, we perform such a search using a deep archival \ observation of the enigmatic Willman 1 Willman:2005a , an ultra-faint object discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey ( SDSS ) and originally classified as a dwarf galaxy Willman:2005a,Willman:2006a .

Based on the high mass-to-light ratio ( 700 ) inferred by Martin:2007a , Willman 1 has emerged as an attractive target for indirect DM searches Strigari:2008a . Yet, to date no DM signal has been observed in sensitive observations of Willman 1 at energies > 100 MeV Aliu:2009a,Abdo:2010b . In X-rays, one expect that the diffuse emission in Willman 1 will be dominated either by an unresolved point source population or by a complex gaseous component Hui:2009a . However, there is also a possibility that the DM halo of Willman 1 might produce a detectable X-ray flux via sterile neutrino decay.

gmw_00005233.name: J0909+1928
gmw_00005233.overview: J0909+1928 is a galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.028 [arXiv:2105.05396] that hosts a compact, flat-spectrum radio core [arXiv:1411.0361, arXiv:2105.05396]. It features an asymmetric, two-sided parsec-scale jet [arXiv:2105.05396] and a rotating disk of neutral hydrogen that may have formed during a gas-rich merger [arXiv:1411.0361].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1226 as a flat-spectrum point source with a compact 15 mas radio size, systemic symmetric H I absorption tracing a disk, and possible gas-rich-merger disk formation.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1226 This source has been observed as part of the CRATES sample Healey2007 . It has been classified as a point source with flat spectrum (spectral index =0.284 ). The object has also been observed as part of the VISP survey at 5 GHz Helmboldt2007 . The approximate size of the radio source is 15 mas ( 8 pc at z=0.0279 ).

We detect \ absorption close to the systemic velocity of the host galaxy. This, along with the symmetry of the line, suggests that we are tracing neutral hydrogen rotating in a disk. The host galaxy may have experienced a gas rich merger which has formed the \ disk.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0909+1928 as a z=0.028 galaxy with an asymmetric two-sided parsec-scale EVN jet, possible jet-ISM deflection, complex spectrum, and compact flat-spectrum core.

quotes.quote: J0909+1928 The source J0909+1928 is associated with a galaxy at redshift z = 0.028 SDSS Data Release 6 as obtained 2007 September 17 from http://www.sdss.org/dr6/products/catalogs/index.html . The source is unresolved at 150-MHz LOFAR observation and 1.4-GHz NVSS observation 1998AJ....115.1693C,2020A&A...642A.107C . Our 5- and 8- GHz EVN images (second row of Figure fig:VLBI ) exhibit an asymmetric two-sided structure at mas resolution, with one jet extending to the north and the counter jet apparently bending eastward at 5 mas (2.5 pc), suggesting a local interaction between the jet and dense interstellar medium (ISM) in the narrow-line region of the host galaxy. Such jet–ISM interaction could deflect the jet flow and change its direction 2018ApJ...864..118K . The source has a complex radio spectrum: a flat spectrum with a spectral index of = 0.42 between 150 MHz and 1.4 GHz 2020A&A...642A.107C , an inverted spectrum with = 0.34 between 1.4 GHz and 5 GHz 2019A&A...631A.176C , and a flat spectrum with ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.18.
The central component C shows the highest compactness, the highest flux density and the flat spectrum ( ^ 4.87-8.37 _ VLBA = 0.28, ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.32) and is identified as the core.

gmw_00005234.name: J1246+1153
gmw_00005234.overview: J1246+1153 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.047 [arXiv:2105.05396]. High-resolution observations reveal a compact core and a core-jet structure extending toward the northwest [arXiv:2105.05396].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1246+1153 as a z=0.047 FR 0 with unresolved low-frequency emission, a northwest core-jet structure, flat spectrum, compact core, and measured J1 motion.

quotes.quote: J1246+1153 The source J1246+1153 is at redshift z = 0.047 1985AJ.....90.1681B . J1246+1153 is unresolved in 150-MHz LOFAR observation and 1.4-GHz NVSS observation 1998AJ....115.1693C,2020A&A...642A.107C . Our high resolution VLBI observations show a core-jet structure with the jet extending to the northwest direction, consistent with the previous results 2018ApJ...863..155C . The source has a flat spectrum with a spectral index of = 0.24 between 150 MHz and 1.4 GHz 2020A&A...642A.107C , a spectrum with = 0.34 between 1.4 GHz and 5 GHz 2019A&A...631A.176C , and a flat spectrum with ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.12.

The component C shows the most compact size, highest flux density, and is located upstream of the jet base. It has a flat spectrum ( ^ 4.87-8.37 _ VLBA = 0.40, ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.58) and is identified as the core.
Using the core as a reference, the linear regression fitting gives the proper motion velocities 0.128 0.101 mas yr^ 1 for J1, corresponding to an angular velocity of 0.39 .

gmw_00005235.name: VCC 1249
gmw_00005235.overview: The dwarf irregular galaxy VCC 1249 is currently undergoing a tidal and ram-pressure stripping interaction with the massive elliptical galaxy M49 [arXiv:1205.3095, arXiv:2105.04569]. This interaction has quenched the dwarf's star formation [arXiv:1205.3095, arXiv:1703.06146], displaced its neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:1205.3095, arXiv:1703.06146], and created a stellar tail [arXiv:1205.3095, arXiv:1507.01336].

sources.summary: The paper treats VCC1249 as a gas-stripped dwarf interacting with M49, with displaced HI, tails, truncated star formation about 200 Myr ago, and young outlying HII regions formed from removed gas.

quotes.quote: Owing to a recent interaction between the dwarf galaxy VCC1249 and the halo gas of the elliptical galaxy M49, neutral hydrogen has been displaced from the interstellar medium of this dwarf into the Virgo intracluster medium. Observations also reveal multiple compact star-forming regions ( aka HII regions) that are embedded in this HI cloud, with a projected separation up to 13 kpc from VCC1249 in the northwest direction. Using deep optical ( u, g, i, z ) imaging from the Next Generation Virgo Cluster Survey (NGVS) and new H imaging obtained at the San Pedro Martir observatory together with Keck long-slit spectroscopy, we characterize the star formation rates, ages, and metallicity of VCC1249 and its outlying compact regions.

In this paper, we focus on the interaction between the dIrr galaxy VCC1249 ( aka UGC 7636) and the massive elliptical M49. Our new observations indicate that VCC1249 underwent a recent interaction with M49 in which both ram-pressure stripping and tidal interaction occured. The joint action of the two mechanisms led to the removal of the HI gas from the interstellar medium of VCC1249, while the gravitational tides triggered the stellar tail and counter-tail of VCC1249. Our stellar population synthesis analysis reveals that the star formation in this galaxy was truncated around 200 Myr ago and that the outlying HII regions were born in situ 10 Myr ago out of pre-enriched gas removed from the dwarf galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes UGC 7636/VCC1249 as a dwarf irregular interacting with NGC 4472, reporting a possible connecting tail, deep light profiles, outward reddening, and color continuity between the systems.

quotes.quote: UGC 7636 As a byproduct of this paper, we also analyzed the dwarf irregular UGC 7636 (VCC1249) Nilson73 located 5.6 ' to the southeast of NGC 4472. This object has been extensively analyzed by Battaia12 , who studied the tidal interaction with NGC 4472 and the gas-stripping phenomena. They found an extensive series of shells and filaments, in agreement with Janowiecki10 .

Based on Fig. 7 of Battaia12 , we judge that our light profiles agree with those of these authors, extending twice as deep, down to _ g 28.7 mag/arcsec ^ 2 . We also derived the average (g-i) color profile (Fig. prof_ugc ). It reddens steadily outwards with the higher slope from a>80 arcsec.

Outside the main galaxy body, where the SB profiles steepen, the color becomes consistent with NGC 4472 (Fig. prof ), indicating a continuity between the two systems, as expected in the close interaction of the dwarf irregular with the giant elliptical.

sources.summary: The paper describes VCC 1249 as a dwarf galaxy interacting with M49, with ram-pressure stripping, a tidal plume, and candidate globular clusters likely being stripped into M49's halo.

quotes.quote: M49 is currently interacting with the dwarf galaxy VCC 1249 e.g., arr12 , lying at a projected distance of 5.4 \ (26.2 kpc). VCC 1249 was imaged in B , I , and H I by , who found evidence for ongoing interaction, including significant ram-pressure stripping. Meanwhile, a tidal ``plume'' was found by to connect VCC 1249 and M49, further bolstering the notion that these two galaxies are caught in the act of a high mass-ratio merger.

The ongoing interaction e.g. arr12 between M49 and the nearby (5.4 ; 26 kpc) dwarf galaxy VCC 1249, presents an excellent opportunity to identify GCs in the act of being accreted onto the giant halo. report a kinematic feature marked by a cluster of PNe likely to be associated with the dwarf that are spatially coincident, share a systemic v_ ,los indistinguishable from VCC 1249 itself, and show a collective _ PNe, VCC1249 =40^ +28 _ 19 . While were able to isolate a kinematically cooler subset of PNe corresponding to VCC 1249's _ VCC 1249 40 , we find no clear group of GCs that correspond directly in phase-space with the dwarf. However, recognizing that GCs may be in the process of being stripped from VCC 1249 (i.e. \ may not exactly coincide spatially), we search for candidates in a similar fashion to that described in sec:gc_sample .

sources.summary: The paper links VCC 1249 to M49's substructure: a displaced H I cloud, debris trail, quenched star formation, stripped gas, and possible contribution to M49's bright PN excess.

quotes.quote: The interaction between the dIrr galaxy VCC 1249 and M49 has come to the attention after the detection of an H I cloud displaced from the dwarf towards M49 1983AJ.....88..260K . Follow-up VLA observations of this cloud by 1994AJ....108..844M revealed a debris trail between the dIrr and M49. A multi-wavelength analysis of this region 2012A&A...543A.112A including near-UV data as well as optical imaging, H and spectroscopy comes to the conclusion that star formation in VCC 1249 has been quenched. Due to ram pressure stripping and tidal interaction H I gas has been removed from the dIrr core.

The presence of these bright objects could very well be related to the accretion of VCC 1249, as the shedding of tidal debris after its pericentre passage can lead to a wider distribution of stars (and therefore PNe) than inferred from the distribution of the light e.g., 2015MNRAS.450..575A .

sources.summary: The paper links VCC 1249 to a kinematic substructure in M49's halo, noting its current interaction, systemic velocity, and planetary-nebula signatures of accretion.

quotes.quote: This peak is centred on the position of the dwarf irregular galaxy VCC 1249 (black cross), which is currently interacting with M49 e.g. 2012A&A...543A.112A . VCC 1249 has a systemic velocity of v_ VCC1249,sys =390 30 km s ^ 1 (SDSS), hence the superposition of the LOS velocities of PNe originating from this galaxy and halo PNe results in a high velocity dispersion in the kernel smoothing recall v_ M49,sys = 960 24 km s ^ 1 , 2017arXiv171205833P . We therefore divide the bright population along the major axis into two subsamples. The southern subsample contains signatures of the accretion of VCC 1249. Based on the featureless appearance of the smoothed velocity and velocity dispersion fields north of the minor axis (see Fig.  fig:vel_fields ), we assume that the PN-kinematics in the northern subsample are unaffected by the accretion of VCC 1249.

sources.summary: The paper treats VCC 1249 as a dwarf irregular interacting with M49, producing a blue LOSVD wing and a distinct bright-PN signature near its radius.

quotes.quote: However, the decomposition of the line-of-sight velocity distribution (LOSVD) into halo and PN components was not straightforward due to the presence of a third component, namely the dwarf irregular galaxy VCC 1249. It is interacting with M49 and dominates the blue wing of the LOSVD due to a velocity difference of 500 km s ^ 1 between the two galaxies. By assessing the reduced velocity as function of magnitude 2006aj....131..837s along the major and minor axes of M49, we identified a sample of bright ( m_ 5007 < 27.5 mag ) PNe (258) with large reduced velocities, of which ten can be firmly associated with the interaction of M49 and VCC 1249 (see also poster by Penger in these proceedings). Their imprint on the line-of-sight velocity dispersion profile as a function of radius can be clearly seen in Fig. fig:M49-sigma , where the profile of the bright PNe (blue) peaks at the major-axis radius at which VCC 1249 is located.

gmw_00005236.name: GN20.2b
gmw_00005236.overview: GN20.2b is a submillimeter starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 4.05 [arXiv:0810.3108, arXiv:1403.7992]. It exhibits a compact brightness distribution characteristic of an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2402.18672], and its structure may indicate a late-stage merging event [arXiv:1403.7992, arXiv:2402.18672].

sources.summary: The paper presents GN20.2b as a plausible additional counterpart to GN20.2, a faint radio source with tentative positive PdBI/CO support and evidence suggesting a similar z 4.05 redshift to GN20.2a.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Inspection of the newly reprocessed 1.4 GHz radio map around the position of GN20.2 (Fig. fig:vla ; Morrison et al., in preparation) shows an additional faint radio source with a flux density of 32.2 Jy (5 ) at (J2000)= 12^h37^m09^s.73 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m02^s.6 (radio frame), 3.4 '' from the GN20.2 SCUBA position (P06) and 6.8 '' from the CO detected counterpart discussed above.

quotes.quote: Inspection of the newly reprocessed 1.4 GHz radio map around the position of GN20.2 (Fig. fig:vla ; Morrison et al., in preparation) shows an additional faint radio source with a flux density of 32.2 Jy (5 ) at (J2000)= 12^h37^m09^s.73 , (J2000)= 62^d22^m02^s.6 (radio frame), 3.4 '' from the GN20.2 SCUBA position (P06) and 6.8 '' from the CO detected counterpart discussed above. We refer to the primary counterpart of GN20.2 as GN20.2a, and this newly identified faint radio source is referred to as GN20.2b. GN20.2b is a plausible additional counterpart to the submm emission. Interestingly, positive signal is found in the PdBI map close to the position of GN20.2b. The PdBI D- and B-configuration datasets were independently fit with a point source fixed at the VLA position of GN20.2b (a secure position cannot be derived from the PdBI data alone, due to the low S/N ) and the resulting spectra were corrected for primary beam attenuation (PBA) and coadded weighting according to the noise, similarly to what was done for the other galaxies.

Although the situation is not secure as for the other two sources, we conclude that there is some supporting evidence for a similar z 4.05 redshift also for GN20.2b. Taking the CO signal at face value, we would assign a redshift of z=4.052 0.006 to GN20.2b, where the error corresponds to half the frequency range spanned by the tentative positive emission, if we identify that as CO [ 4-3 ] . This is within z=0.003 of the redshift of GN20 and GN20.2a, while our data is sensitive in principle to emission from a z=0.05 , 17 times larger redshift range of 4.02<z<4.07 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes GN20.2b as a z=4.05 submillimeter galaxy whose gas, dust, line width, and starburst properties may indicate a decaying merger-driven starburst stage.

quotes.quote: Based also on evaluations of the specific SFR, CO-to-H _2 conversion factor and gas depletion timescale, we classify all the three galaxies as starbursts (SBs), although with a lower confidence for GN20.2b that might be a later stage merging event.

Two additional SMGs, GN20.2a and GN20.2b, were found to lie within 25 '' of GN20 (projected physical separation 170 kpc) and have redshifts of z 4.055 0.005. These two galaxies are separated by only a few arcseconds and hence are not spatially separated from each other in existing submm images (e.g., SCUBA 850 m). daddi09b also found 14 B-band dropouts (roughly z 4 ) lying within 25 '' from GN20, which corresponds to an overdensity of 5.8 in the GOODS-N field, suggesting a massive proto-cluster environment at z 4.05 , just 1.6 Gyr after the Big Bang.

The CO(4-3) spectrum of GN20.2b has been presented in hodge13 . Compared to the large uncertainty in the line width fitted in hodge13 , our deeper CO(4-3) data provide a much better constraint on the estimate of the line width. Similarly, we performed Gaussian fits to the spectrum (Fig. fig1 ) extracted at the fixed position of 1.2 mm emission in GN20.2b (Riechers et al., in prep.) with a single Gaussian and double Gaussian functions, respectively.
