Both Gaussian fits give a similar FWHM with value of 220 40 , which is much smaller than GN20 and GN20.2a, and also the SMGs at z 2-4 with mean FWHM of 470 80 bothwell13 .

sources.summary: The paper characterizes GN20.2b as compact and early-type, with possible internal substructure or post-coalescence morphology plus a mature stellar population.

quotes.quote: GN20.2b shows some clear differences in the morphology with respect to the other two DSFGs. It presents a compact brightness distribution that can be modelled by a single S ersic profile with n = 4.51 0.25, R_ eff = 1.30 0.05 kpc and b/a = 0.72 0.03. We have tested, for completeness, the possibility of the presence of a point-source along with the S ersic profile.

This difference in the orientation of the inner regions may indicate the presence of an internal sub-structure, such as an inner disc. Another possible scenario is that GN20.2b is an object in a merging stage very close to the post-coalescence, in which the gravitational interaction could have created this difference between the inner and outer isophotes.

GN20.2b is the galaxy among the DSFGs with the lowest SFR (i.e., 750 ). This massive galaxy ( = (1.8 0.4) 11 ) presents a young burst ( 9 ), as expected from the bright central UV source visible in the HST (see Fig. fig:UV-MIRI_images ). This burst is creating a young stellar population that represents only 5 of the total mass, while the bulk of the stellar mass of this galaxy is dominated by a more mature stellar population.

sources.summary: The paper resolves GN20.2b as an extended high-redshift SMG with lower gas surface density and excitation than GN20.2a, plus CO-based redshift, gas, and dynamical constraints.

quotes.quote: The SMGs GN20.2a and GN20.2b have deconvolved sizes of 5 kpc 3 kpc and 8 kpc 5 kpc (Gaussian FWHM) in CO(2--1), respectively, and we measure gas surface densities up to 12,700/1,700 (sin i ) ( _ CO /0.8) M _ pc ^ 2 for GN20.2a/GN20.2b in the highest--resolution maps. Dynamical mass estimates allow us to constrain the CO--to--H _2 conversion factor to _ CO = 1.7 0.8 M _ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 ) ^ 1 for GN20.2a and _ CO = 1.1 ^ 1.5 _ 1.1 M _ (K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 ) ^ 1 for GN20.2b. We measure significant offsets (0.5 ^ -1 ^ ) between the CO and optical emission, indicating either dust obscuration on scales of tens of kpc or that the emission originates from distinct galaxies. CO spectral line energy distributions imply physical conditions comparable to other SMGs and reveal further evidence that GN20.2a and GN20.2b are in different merging stages.

The CO(2--1) spectrum for GN20.2b is shown in Figure fig:GN20_2_spectra , smoothed to 78 km s ^ 1 (12 MHz) per channel. A Gaussian fit to the spectrum is shown by the black curve, and the velocity offset is shown with respect to the derived redshift. The fit has a peak flux density of 600 270 Jy beam ^ 1 and a derived redshift of 4.056 0.001. The FWHM of GN20.2b is significantly smaller than GN20.2a, at 400 210 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00005237.name: BD29079
gmw_00005237.overview: BD29079 is a UV-selected Lyman-break galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift of z=4.058 [arXiv:1309.5448, arXiv:1307.4763]. Its stellar component is composed of three bright, close clumps [arXiv:2402.18672], and it exhibits no detected CO line emission [arXiv:1309.5448, arXiv:1307.4763].

sources.summary: The paper treats BD29079 as a z=4.058 UV-selected LBG with no detected CO line emission, high inferred gas mass, and weak CO relative to its IR luminosity and SFR.

quotes.quote: BD29079 is an UV-selected B dropout galaxy (Figure  fig1 ) with spectroscopic redshift z=4.058 daddi09 . CO measurements toward this galaxy were previously reported as upper limits carilli11,hodge13 . Combining our new, deep CO(4-3) data with those already published, we searched for CO-emission to deeper levels. Figure  fig1 shows CO spectra binned to 26 km s ^ 1 at the expected frequency of CO  J =2-1, 4-3, and 6-5. No line emission from the individual CO transitions was detected.

BD29079 has a stellar mass of M_ = (2.5 0.5) 10^ 10 M _ , as determined by SED-fitting of the rest-frame UV to near-IR broad-band photometry using bruzual03 models and assuming a constant star formation history (SFH), a chabrier03 IMF, and a calzetti00 extinction law. We infer an extinction-corrected 1500 \ rest-frame luminosity corresponding to SFR of 170 40 M _  yr ^ 1 (see Table  tbl-2 ). Complementary, independent constraints on the L_ IR of BD29079 follow from its JVLA and Herschel coverage.

sources.summary: The paper describes BD29079 as a clumpy Lyman-break galaxy with three near-IR components, young stellar population, low extinction, and possible warm-dust AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: The visual inspection of the Lyman-break galaxy (i.e., BD29079) reveals that the stellar component of this object is composed of three bright clumps very close to each other (i.e., < 4 kpc). Due to its complex sub-structure, we decided to use a different approach to model its morphology. We fit its brightness distribution considering 3 independent point-sources, allowing them to vary their intensity. We obtain that, under this approach, the central, north and southeast clumps present fluxes of 1.26 0.15 , 0.71 0.15 and 0.52 0.15 , respectively.

For the Lyman-Break galaxy BD29079 we find that it presents a total stellar mass ( 2.4 10 ) similar to GN20.2a, while the very young ( 2.5 ) population represents 15 of the total stellar mass budget. The presence of this very young population along with the low extinction ( A_ V =0.71 mag) of this galaxy justify the strong emission observed in the rest-frame UV (see Fig. fig:UV-MIRI_images ). Figure fig:SED shows that there are some residuals in the mid-IR due to the presence of a power-law type behaviour that is not captured by the CIGALE model for this galaxy. This feature is linked to the presence of some warm dust component, usually produced by an AGN.

sources.summary: The paper identifies BD29079 as a z=4.058 LBG in the observing band, reports no CO detection, and constrains its CO luminosity, stellar mass, and gas fraction.

quotes.quote: Aside from one LBG ( 6 in Figure fig:bdrops_130kL ; a.k.a. \ BD29079) with a spectroscopic redshift placing it definitively in our bandpass z=4.058 ; 2009ApJ...694.1517D , the exact redshifts of the rest of the LBGs are unknown, and their redshifted CO emission may lie outside the range covered by our observations ( z= 4.035--4.063). For BD29079 and any other sources which may be covered by our observations, our non--detections of CO emission allow us to constrain their luminosities. If we assume a linewidth of 300 km s ^ 1 (FWHM), then we derive 3 upper limits on their CO luminosities in the range (8--16) 10 ^ 9 K km s ^ 1 pc ^ 2 (depending on the primary beam correction at their position). The stellar mass for BD29079 is 2.6 10^ 10 M _ , and for the rest of the LBGs, we assume the average stellar mass reported for 77 IRAC--detected B -band dropout LBGs in the GOODS-N field as well as assumed above for the possible emission line source LBG 8; 2009ApJ...694.1517D .
For BD29079, the gas--to--stellar mass ratio is <0.35 ( _ CO /0.8) , and the average gas mass fraction is < 26 (or < 60 if _ CO = 3.6 ).

gmw_00005238.name: J123710.76+622204.6
gmw_00005238.overview: J123710.76+622204.6 is an optical galaxy with a coincident infrared Spitzer IRAC source [arXiv:1307.4763]. It has a photometric redshift of approximately 1.34 and is a possible counterpart to a 45 GHz emission-line candidate [arXiv:1307.4763].

sources.summary: The paper discusses J123710.76+622204.6 as the possible optical counterpart to a blind 45 GHz emission-line candidate, with an IRAC match and photometric redshift near z=1.34.

quotes.quote: Only one of the previously unnamed source candidates has a possible optical counterpart (J123710.76+622204.6, circled star in Figure fig:field ). Figure fig:PosSrc+HST show its velocity--averaged VLA contours overlaid on a 4 ^ 4 ^ cutout from the deep HST + ACS 850z--band coverage of GOODS-N. The 45 GHz emission line candidate lies within 1 ^ of an optical galaxy. There is an IRAC source coincident (within 0.078 ^ ) with the optical galaxy, and the corresponding photometric-redshift is 1.34. This could indicate that the 45 GHz emission line, if real, is CO(1--0).

Its redshift (as measured from our spectrum) would then be z 1.52 , consistent with that of the possible counterpart given typical photometric redshift uncertainties. The positional offset (0.9 ^ ) between the IRAC and CO positions implies a small but non--negligible chance that the two are related ( 5 probability). Therefore, as an additional test, we checked whether the CO--derived properties would make sense in light of the possible counterpart.

gmw_00005239.name: ALMA J010748.3 173028
gmw_00005239.overview: ALMA J010748.3 173028 is a distant star-forming galaxy located behind the nearby merging galaxies VV114 [arXiv:1401.0003]. It hosts an obscured, luminous active galactic nucleus and exhibits a high far-infrared luminosity [arXiv:1401.0003].

sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA J010748.3 173028 as a distant CO-emitting star-forming galaxy with an 880 micron counterpart, likely z=2.467, high FIR luminosity, and an obscured luminous AGN.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: We serendipitously detect a 13 line-emitting object, ALMA-J0107, at 99.753 GHz at ( , )_ J2000 = (01^ h 07^ m 48 32, 17 30' 28 1) , as shown in Figure fig:almaimage .

quotes.quote: We report the detection of a distant star-forming galaxy, ALMA J010748.3 173028, which is identified by a 13 emission line at 99.75 GHz ( S v = 3.1 Jy km s ^ 1 ), behind the nearby merging galaxies VV114 using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) Band 3. We also find an 880- counterpart with ALMA Band 7 ( S_ 880 m = 11.2 mJy). A careful comparison of the intensities of the line and the continuum suggests that the line is a redshifted ^ 12 CO transition. A photometric redshift analysis using the infrared to radio data favors a CO redshift of z = 2.467 , although z = 3.622 is acceptable. We also find a hard X-ray counterpart, suggesting the presence of a luminous ( L_ X 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ) active galactic nucleus obscured by a large hydrogen column ( N_ H 2 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 if z = 2.47 ).

The molecular mass and FIR luminosity at the plausible redshifts are M( H_2 ) 8 10^ 10 M_ and L_ FIR 1 10^ 13 L_ , respectively, which correspond to a star formation rate of 2000 M_ yr ^ 1 . We identified a hard X-ray source at the ALMA position, suggesting the presence of a luminous ( L_ X 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ) AGN behind a large hydrogen column ( 23.2 [ N_ H / cm^ 2 ] 23.6 for the likely redshifts). However, the intrinsic properties of the AGN (e.g., the bolometric luminosity) depend strongly on the redshift, although the FIR luminosity and molecular mass are rather insensitive to the redshift.

gmw_00005240.name: TXS 0642 + 449
gmw_00005240.overview: TXS 0642 + 449 is a high-redshift blazar and low-polarization quasar located at z = 3.396 [arXiv:2404.16343, arXiv:1504.04273]. It features a compact core-jet morphology [arXiv:2404.16343, arXiv:1504.04273] with an eastward-extending jet [arXiv:1202.2193] and an extremely luminous accretion disk [arXiv:1702.04006].

sources.summary: The paper studies OH 471 as a z=3.396 blazar whose radio monitoring and VLBI structure reveal synchrotron self-absorption, strong core magnetic fields, and MAD-powered relativistic jets.

quotes.quote: We study the high-redshift ( z=3.396 ) blazar OH 471 to explore the jet launching mechanism in the early Universe. Using multi-frequency radio monitoring observations and high-resolution Very Long Baseline Interferometry imaging over three decades, we study the milliarcsecond structure and long-term variability of OH 471. Spectral modelling of the radio flux densities reveals a synchrotron self-absorbed spectrum indicating strong magnetic fields within the compact core. By applying the flux freezing approximation, we estimate the magnetic flux carried by the jet and find that it reaches or exceeds theoretical predictions for jets powered by black hole spin energy via the Blandford-Znajek mechanism.

In this paper, we study the radio source OH 471 (0642+449, J0646+4451), a high-redshift ( z = 3.396 ) blazar that serves as a unique laboratory for testing MAD models and probing jet physics in the early Universe. It was one of the first distant ( z>3 ) radio quasars identified in the 1970s 1972AJ.....77..557G,1973Natur.242..394C,1973Natur.243...25C . Early radio observations of OH 471 revealed a complex spectral structure with multiple synchrotron self-absorption (SSA) components on different physical scales peaking between 0.5--20 GHz 1974Natur.249..743G, 1974Natur.250..472J , indicating emission from several distinct regions in the relativistic jet. Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations established OH 471 as a blazar with a typical one-sided core-jet morphology on milliarcsecond scales 1980AJ.....85..668M, 1992A&A...260...82G, 1998AJ....115.1295K, 2005AJ....130.2473K, 2013AJ....146..120L,2015AJ....150...58F,2015A&A...583A.100L .

sources.summary: The paper studies TXS 0642 + 449 as a high-redshift low-polarization quasar with a compact core-jet; RadioAstron resolves polarized jet emission on sub-milliarcsecond scales.

quotes.quote: The radio source 0642 + 449 is a distant low-polarization quasar (LPQ) located at a redshift of 3.396 osmer+1994 which corresponds to the luminosity distance of 30.2 Gigaparsecs (Gpc) and a linear scale of 7.58 kpc/arcsecond, assuming the standard CDM cosmology planck2015 . The optical spectrum of 0642 + 449 does not show strong broad lines torrealba+2012 . In radio, its radio structure has a compact ``core--jet'' morphology gurvits+1992,xu+1995 , with a flat spectral index 0.1 hovatta+2014 of the milliarcsecond (mas) scale core (bright narrow end of the jet). The maximum brightness temperature, T_ b , measured in 0642 + 449 decreases with observing frequency.

The total and linearly polarized emission from the quasar 0642 + 449 is detected on the SRT baselines of up to 75,560 km (5.93 Earth diameters) in length, corresponding to angular scales of 0.5 milliarcseconds (mas). The resulting RadioAstron image of linearly polarized emission in 0642 + 449 has a resolution of 0.8 mas, which is 4 times better than the resolution of ground VLBI images at 18 cm. The image probes the physical conditions in the jet on angular scales as small as 0.2 mas (linear scale of 1.5 pc) taking into account the SNR-driven resolution limits of the observation.

sources.summary: NVSS J064632+445116 is discussed as a high-redshift blazar candidate/detection whose modeled SED shows luminous accretion-disk emission and hard inverse-Compton high-energy output.

quotes.quote: NVSS J064632+445116 was predicted as a candidate ray emitter by 2008ApJS..175...97H , whereas NVSS J135406 020603 is included in the ROMA-BZCAT 2015Ap&SS.357...75M .

We generate broadband SEDs of the three objects that have archival X-ray observations and model them using a simple one zone synchrotron-IC emission approach prescribed in 2009MNRAS.397..985G . In brief, the model assumes a spherical emission region located at a distance R_ diss from the central engine and filled with a population of highly energetic electrons that follow a broken power-law distribution. In the presence of a tangled magnetic field, the electrons lose energy via synchrotron, synchrotron self Compton (SSC), and external Compton (EC) processes. For the latter, the seed photons originate from several external AGN components: photons directly emitted from the accretion disk e.g., 1993ApJ...416..458D , from BLR 1994ApJ...421..153S and from the infrared torus e.g., 2000ApJ...545..107B . The calculated jet powers and SED parameters are given in Table tab:sed_par and the modeled SEDs are shown in Figure fig:sed . In each of the three objects, the IR-UV emission is found to be dominated by an extremely luminous accretion disk ( L_ disk >10^ 46 ). The X-ray spectra, on the other hand, are hard and the entire X-ray to ray band of the SED can be explained by the IC scattering off the photons originating from the BLR.

The Compton dominance (ratio of the IC to synchrotron peak luminosities) of each of the three sources is also very large ( >20 , Table tab:sed_par ), a characteristic feature exhibited by powerful blazars.

sources.summary: 0642+449 is the sample's most luminous quasar; prior VLBI shows an eastward jet, the authors recover the 5 GHz structure but no 8.4 GHz extended jet, and find negligible core Faraday rotation.

quotes.quote: 0642+449 This extremely luminous quasar ( L_ 8.4 GHz =6.8 10^ 28 W Hz ^ 1 ; the highest in our sample) is regularly observed by the MOJAVE http://www.physics.purdue.edu/astro/MOJAVE/index.html team with the VLBA at 15 GHz, who have reported subluminal speeds of 0.76 for an inner jet component lister2009 . A 5-GHz global VLBI image by gurvits1992 shows the jet extending almost 10 mas to the East while find the jet extending 5 mas to the East from EVN observations at 1.6 GHz. Our 5-GHz image is consistent with the images of and , with no extended jet emission detected at 8.4 GHz. Due to the lack of extended jet emission at 8.4 GHz the images could not be aligned based on their optically thin jet emission. Polarized emission is detected in the core at both 5 and 8.4 GHz; the minimum difference between the model-fitted polarization angles is less than 1^ , nominally indicating negligible Faraday rotation.

sources.summary: The paper reports significant low-energy gamma-ray detection of high-redshift 5BZQJ 0646+4451 in a crowded Galactic-plane region and confirms positional compatibility with a TS map.

quotes.quote: 5BZQJ 0646+4451. Although this source is located in a relatively crowded region close to the Galactic plane ( |b| =17.9 ^ ), the low-energy detection is very significant with TS ^ Pass 8 _ 7.5 yrs 80 in the 300-550 MeV energy bin alone. We found no significant flaring activity during the 7.5 yrs of observations with Fermi-LAT, and the period with the most significant \ signature extends from October 25 to November 30, 2010. The highest energy photons detected from this region are 10 GeV, and therefore we built a TS map in the 500 MeV - 12 GeV energy range (Fig. bzq0646 , left panel). The \ signature emerges as a point source fully compatible with the blazar position. In particular, the \ counterpart has high redshift of z = 3.396, and absorption due to EBL might hinder the detection of VHE photons.

gmw_00005241.name: WISE J141046.00 + 740511.2
gmw_00005241.overview: WISE J141046.00 + 740511.2 is a high-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.08174] that is associated with gamma-ray and X-ray emission [arXiv:1701.06067, arXiv:2211.08174]. Although initially considered a radio-weak candidate [arXiv:1701.06067], high-resolution observations identify it as a radio-loud jetted active galactic nucleus with compact radio emission [arXiv:1810.01204].

sources.summary: The paper presents WISE J141046.00+740511.2 as a strong radio-weak BL Lac candidate, linked to gamma-ray sources and supported by mid-infrared color analysis plus Swift X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: The remaining case of WISE J141046.00+740511.2 is the most intriguing one. This object is potentially associated to a 1FHL object and its potential \ counterpart lies in the region of the `` \ Gamma-ray Strip'' where all known TeV BL Lacs are located massaro13d,arsioli15 . The lack of any radio counterpart in both the NRAO VLA Sky Survey NVSS; condon98 and the FIRST catalog helfand15 that cover the field of WISE J141046.00+740511.2 makes this source the most promising candidate to be the first, genuine, RWBL. Nevertheless is it worth emphasizing that the distance between this mid-IR source and the gamma-ray position of 3FGL J1410.9+7406 is 90 well below the average of the associated \ blazars listed in the \ corresponding to 130 .

Applying this mid-IR color-based analysis, in combination with optical spectra, we conclude that WISE J064459.38+603131 and WISE J141046.00+740511.2, lying within the positional uncertainty regions of two distinct UGSs listed in the 3FGL, are the first two RWBLs detected so far. Both of them can also be associable with 1FHL sources thus strengthening our interpretation, since the high energy sky above 10 GeV is mostly dominated by BZBs.
We collected 13 counts for WISE J064459.38+603131 in the merged XRT event file while there are 36 counts for WISE J141046.00+740511.2, making both sources well detected above the 5 threshold.

sources.summary: The paper confirms WISE J141046.00 + 740511.2 as a highly polarized HBL-type BL Lac with unusually weak radio emission and SED parameters consistent with an EHBL candidate.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: We performed a multiwavelength observational follow-up campaign on WISE J141046.00+740511.2 RA =14 h 10 m 46 s , DEC =74 d 5 m 11.2 s (J2000.0).

quotes.quote: The multiwavelength analysis indicates that \ is a blazar of the high-frequency peaked (HBL) type that emits highly polarized light and that is likely located at a low redshift. In addition, the one-zone model parameters that best fit its SED are those of an extreme HBL (EHBL); this blazar type has been extensively predicted in theory to be lacking in the radio emission that is otherwise typical of canonical ray blazars. We confirm that is indeed a highly polarized BL Lac of the HBL type. Further studies will be conducted to explain the atypical low radio flux detected for this source.

We conducted a multiwavelength observational campaign of the source \ to study its nature. Originally detected by Fermi, it was later revealed to show several BL Lac characteristics in the ray, X-ray, UV, optical, and infrared bands, but its radio emission was not on par with what is expected for a radio-loud source, as a canonical BL Lac would demonstrate. We state that \ is indeed a ray emitting BL Lac source, given its typical BL Lac optical spectrum as observed by high-sensitivity telescopes and its observed high degree of optical polarization.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1410+7405 as a radio-loud jetted AGN: EVN detects compact 5-GHz emission, VLBI measures its radio position, and Gaia agreement strengthens the optical-radio association.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The right ascension RA = 14^ h 10^ m 45 95002 and declination DEC = +74 05 10 9061 were determined from the image using the aips task tvmaxfit .

quotes.quote: One target, J1410+7405, is clearly detected with the EVN. Its measured 5-GHz flux density, 2.4 mJy, is consistent with recent interferometric measurements with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array, suggesting that the radio emission is confined to the inner 10 mas region. J1410+7405 is therefore identified as a radio-loud jetted active galactic nucleus, and its brightness temperature exceeds 10^ 9 K.

VLBI phase-referencing allows us to measure the accurate J2000 coordinates of the radio brightness peak in J1410+7405. The right ascension RA = 14^ h 10^ m 45 95002 and declination DEC = +74 05 10 9061 were determined from the image using the aips task tvmaxfit .

The Gaia DR1 optical position (see Table tab-1 and Fig. fig-1 ) is consistent with that measured with VLBI within the errors, which strengthens the physical association of the optical and radio source.

gmw_00005242.name: QSO2
gmw_00005242.overview: QSO2 is a faint companion quasar located at a redshift of approximately 3.156 [arXiv:1709.08228]. It lies at a projected distance of 51.7 kiloparsecs from a primary quasar, relative to which it exhibits a blueshift of 576 kilometers per second [arXiv:1709.08228].

sources.summary: QSO2 is a faint companion quasar 51.7 kpc from the main quasar; the paper estimates its luminosity, redshift, and blueshift relative to the primary system.

quotes.quote: These are a quasar, referred to as QSO2, and a strong \ emitter (EW _ rest =105 ), referred to as LAE1, at projected distances of 51.7 kpc ( 6.8 ) and 69.2 kpc ( 9.1 ) from , respectively (Fig. figsources ).

We evaluate the redshift of QSO2 with the same approach as for \ (see appendix appZ ), i.e. following Shen2016 . Specifically, we estimate the peak of the line, as specified in Shen2016 , and obtained z=3.158 . With a measured L_ 1700 = (5.0 0.9) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 for QSO2, we estimate an expected shift of +173 km s ^ 1 for with respect to systemic. We thus obtain a redshift of z=3.156 0.006 for QSO2, where we took into account the systematic uncertainty of 415 km s ^ 1 in the error estimate ( Shen2016 ). Bearing in mind the uncertainties on the redshift determination for both quasars, QSO2 seems to have a blueshift of 576 km s ^ 1 with respect to .

gmw_00005243.name: LAE1
gmw_00005243.overview: LAE1 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.168 [arXiv:1709.08228]. It has an estimated star-formation rate of 50 solar masses per year [arXiv:2111.15392] and is situated 69.2 kiloparsecs from a nearby quasar [arXiv:1709.08228].

sources.summary: The paper treats LAE1 as a Ly alpha emitter associated with the ELAN, detects compact continuum emission, and estimates its star-formation rate with CIGALE.

quotes.quote: The detected sources are QSO, QSO2, AGN1, LAE1, and a newly discovered source SMG1. The positions, S/N and fluxes for the five detections are listed in Table tab:Interf , together with the deboosted fluxes estimated as explained in Appendix app:SMADeboosting . Summing up the deboosted fluxes of all detected sources, we find agreement within uncertainties with the detections in the single-dish datasets, 14.7 2.8 mJy. Therefore, all the continuum emission detected by LABOCA and SCUBA-2 is ascribed to compact sources.

Further, CIGALE determined the instantaneous SFR for each source, indicating SFR =521 74,183 49,104 21,50 11 M _ yr ^ 1 for QSO, QSO2, AGN1, LAE1, respectively. These SFRs are in line with values from the literature. In particular, the SFR in the QSO host agrees well with values usually found in z 2-3 quasars (e.g., Harris2016 ).

sources.summary: LAE1 is a strong Ly emitter 69.2 kpc from the quasar, with redshift z=3.168 and an i-band magnitude of 25.45 in the MUSE data.

quotes.quote: These are a quasar, referred to as QSO2, and a strong \ emitter (EW _ rest =105 ), referred to as LAE1, at projected distances of 51.7 kpc ( 6.8 ) and 69.2 kpc ( 9.1 ) from , respectively (Fig. figsources ).

For LAE1, we have obtained a redshift of z=3.168 0.001 by fitting its \ emission line with a gaussian (despite the presence of a red tail in its shape). For both QSO2 and LAE1, we have extracted an equivalent i band SDSS magnitude from the MUSE datacube, obtaining 24.30 0.02 and 25.45 0.05 , respectively. These sources are therefore much fainter than \ ( i =17.98, fiber magnitude).

gmw_00005244.name: AGN1
gmw_00005244.overview: AGN1 is a compact emitter located at a redshift of z=3.179 [arXiv:1709.08228]. It is classified as a type-2 active galactic nucleus due to its hard-ionizing emission lines and exhibits a large velocity shift [arXiv:1709.08228].

sources.summary: AGN1 is identified as a compact emitter near the quasar redshift, likely a type-2 AGN with hard-ionizing emission, a large velocity shift, and a non-Gaussian line profile.

quotes.quote: This approach led to the identification of two \ emitters at a redshift similar to , AGN1 at z=3.179 0.001 (EW _ rest >168 ) at 11.3 ( 85.9 kpc), and LAE2 at z=3.167 0.001 (EW _ rest >29 ) at 11.9 ( 90.4 kpc) (Fig. figsources ). AGN1 also exhibits a \ emission line, and tentative evidence for and \ emission lines, all indicative of a hard-ionizing source, making this source most likely a type-2 AGN.

Given its redshift, AGN1 has a velocity shift of 864 km s ^ 1 with respect to . Note that AGN1 does not have a fully gaussian \ line, although redshifts determined from both \ and agree within their uncertainties.
